Unlike wired networks (e.g., Ethernet), wireless sensor networks face **unique challenges** that make the Data Link Layer design especially tricky: | Challenge | Impact on Data Link Layer Design | |---|---| | **Limited Energy** | Idle listening, collision, and overhearing waste energy → must minimize via sleep/wake schedules | | **Shared Wireless Medium** | Multiple nodes transmit simultaneously → collisions must be avoided/handled | | **Variable Topology** | Nodes die, mobility, or RF obstruction → MAC must adapt dynamically | | **Unicast, Multicast, and Broadcast** | WSN traffic is largely **many-to-one** (toward sink) and **one-to-many** (queries/commands) | | **No Central Coordinator (in most WSNs)** | Pure distributed MAC required → no AP like in WLAN | | **Low Duty Cycle Operation** | Nodes sleep >99% of the time → MAC must synchronize wake-ups | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why can't we just use Ethernet's CSMA/CD in WSNs?", the answer is: > - WSNs use **half-duplex** radios → cannot detect collisions while transmitting. > - Sensor nodes are **energy-constrained** → cannot afford the cost of retransmission. > - WSNs require **CSMA/CA** (Collision *Avoidance*) instead of CSMA/CD (Collision *Detection*). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Data Link Layer The Data Link Layer in WSN is decomposed into **two sublayers** (as per the IEEE LLC/MAC split): ### 2.1.1 Logical Link Control (LLC) Sublayer - Responsible for **framing, error control, and flow control** between two adjacent nodes. - Provides an abstraction to the upper Network layer, hiding the specifics of the underlying MAC. ### 2.1.2 Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer - Responsible for **regulating access to the shared wireless medium**. - Decides **when** a node can transmit and **how** collisions are managed. - Manages **duty cycling** (the alternating sleep/wake pattern) — the single largest energy-saving mechanism in WSNs. ## 2.2 Sources of Energy Waste at the MAC Layer A foundational concept introduced by Ye, Heidemann, and Estrin (the creators of S-MAC, USC/ISI, 2002) identifies **four major sources of energy waste**: 1. **Collision** – corrupted packets force retransmission → wasted energy. 2. **Overhearing** – a node receives packets destined for other nodes → wasted RX energy. 3. **Idle Listening** – node listens to an idle channel waiting for traffic → the **dominant** source of energy waste in many deployments. 4. **Control Packet Overhead** – RTS/CTS/ACK overhead → wasted energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Sources of Energy Waste (Ye et al. 2002):** > 1. **Collision** > 2. **Overhearing** > 3. **Idle Listening** (typically the largest contributor) > 4. **Control Overhead** ## 2.3 Classification of WSN MAC Protocols MAC protocols for WSN can be classified along multiple dimensions. The most important KTU classification is: ### A. Contention-Based MAC Protocols Nodes compete for the channel using **CSMA/CA** variants. Best for low-to-moderate traffic loads. | Protocol | Key Innovation | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Pure CSMA/CA (IEEE 802.15.4)** | Random backoff, no duty cycling | Always-on WSNs | | **B-MAC (Berkeley MAC)** | Long preamble sampling, Clear Channel Assessment (CCA) | TinyOS-based motes | | **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC)** | Synchronized sleep/wake schedules, RTS/CTS | Periodic monitoring | | **T-MAC (Timeout-MAC)** | Adaptive duty cycle via early timeout | Variable traffic | | **WiseMAC** | Non-preamble sampling, learns neighbor schedules | Low-power listening | ### B. Schedule-Based MAC Protocols Nodes transmit in **pre-assigned time slots** (TDMA-like). Best for high traffic or QoS-sensitive applications. | Protocol | Key Innovation | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---| | **LEACH / LEACH-C** | Cluster-based TDMA with cluster head rotation | Energy-balanced clustering | | **TRAMA (Traffic-Adaptive MAC)** | Adaptive slot assignment based on traffic | Periodic + event-driven | | **DMAC (Data-gathering MAC)** | Staggered wake-up following data-gathering tree | Convergecast to sink | | **BMA (Bit-Map Assisted)** | Bitmap reservation per round | Distributed TDMA | ### C. Hybrid MAC Protocols Combine the advantages of both. Examples: **Z-MAC (CSMA/TDMA hybrid)**, **IEEE 802.15.4 (beacon-enabled mode = slotted CSMA/CA with GTS)**. > [!NOTE] > **Hybrid MAC Definition:** A protocol that uses contention-based access in low-traffic conditions and switches to scheduled (TDMA) access in high-traffic conditions. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Duty Cycle (DC)** | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 to 1. KTU board uses percentage: $\text{DC}\% = \dfrac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100$ | | 2 | **Energy per Bit (E_b)** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{\text{tx}} \cdot T_{\text{bit}}}{\text{bits/packet}}$ | Joules/bit | | 3 | **Throughput (S)** | $S = \dfrac{L \cdot P_s}{T_{\text{slot}} \cdot N}$ | bits/sec, where $L$ = payload bits, $P_s$ = success probability, $N$ = number of nodes | | 4 | **Average Delay (D)** | $D = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{\text{slot}}}{2}$ | seconds, for a fully scheduled (TDMA) network | | 5 | **Total Energy per Cycle** | $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{sleep}} T_{\text{sleep}} + P_{\text{tx}} T_{\text{tx}} + P_{\text{rx}} T_{\text{rx}}$ | Joules | | 6 | **Lifetime (L_T)** | $L_T = \dfrac{E_{\text{initial}}}{E_{\text{cycle}}} \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | seconds (or years after conversion) | | 7 | **CSMA Throughput (Slotted, p-persistent)** | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-G}}$ | dimensionless, $G$ = offered load, $a$ = propagation delay ratio | | 8 | **Bit Error Rate (BER)** | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | For BPSK in AWGN; $Q(\cdot)$ is the Q-function | | 9 | **Frame Error Rate (FER)** | $P_{\text{FER}} = 1 - (1 - P_e)^n$ | For $n$-bit frame, assuming independent bit errors | | 10 | **ARQ Energy Overhead** | $E_{\text{ARQ}} = E_{\text{tx}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - P_{\text{FER}}}$ | Joules; includes retransmission cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Convention:** In all above formulas, $P_{\text{tx}}, P_{\text{rx}}, P_{\text{sleep}}$ denote **transmit, receive, and sleep mode power** (in Watts), while $T_{\text{tx}}, T_{\text{rx}}, T_{\text{sleep}}$ are the corresponding time durations. A typical mote draws **~15 mA active TX, ~10 mA active RX, and <1 µA in deep sleep**. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility The Data Link Layer design has direct engineering consequences: - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors deployed across a 50-hectare farm use **S-MAC with 1% duty cycle** to operate for 2+ years on two AA batteries. - **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridges use **TDMA-based MAC (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 beacon mode)** to guarantee bounded latency for vibration data. - **Military Surveillance:** Battlefield sensor fields use **WiseMAC** to enable covert, low-probability-of-intercept communication. - **Healthcare WSNs:** Body-area networks (BAN) use **T-MAC with adaptive duty cycle** to handle sporadic, life-critical events with minimal battery drain. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Duty Cycle and Energy Savings **Problem Setup:** A WSN mote operates with a fixed periodic schedule. The total cycle time is $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}$. The active period consists of a synchronization phase and a data transmission phase. Given: - $T_{\text{cycle}} = 1$ second (one full duty cycle period) - $T_{\text{active}} = 10$ ms (node is awake, listening/transmitting) - $P_{\text{tx}} = P_{\text{rx}} = 60$ mW (active mode power) - $P_{\text{sleep}} = 30$ $\mu$W (deep sleep mode power) **Find:** (a) Duty cycle as a percentage, (b) Energy consumed per cycle, (c) Lifetime in days, assuming $E_{\text{initial}} = 7.2$ Wh (≈ 2 Ah @ 3.6 V, two AA batteries). ### Solution **Part (a) — Duty Cycle Calculation:** By definition, the duty cycle is the fraction of time the node is active: $$ \text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} $$ Substituting the given values: $$ \text{DC} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{1 \text{ s}} = 0.01 $$ Converting to a percentage: $$ \text{DC}\,(\%) = 0.01 \times 100 = 1\% $$ > **Interpretation:** The node is awake only **1% of the time**, sleeping for the remaining 99%. This is the hallmark of energy-efficient WSN operation. **Part (b) — Energy Consumed per Cycle:** The energy in each mode is $E_i = P_i \cdot T_i$. Therefore: $$ E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}} $$ We have $T_{\text{sleep}} = T_{\text{cycle}} - T_{\text{active}} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$ s. Substituting: $$ E_{\text{cycle}} = (60 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3}) + (30 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.99) $$ Evaluating term-by-term: $$ E_{\text{active}} = 60 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.99 = 2.97 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{\text{cycle}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.97 \times 10^{-5} = 6.297 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} $$ **Part (c) — Lifetime Calculation:** Number of cycles until battery exhaustion: $$ N_{\text{cycles}} = \frac{E_{\text{initial}}}{E_{\text{cycle}}} $$ Converting $E_{\text{initial}} = 7.2$ Wh to Joules: $E_{\text{initial}} = 7.2 \times 3600 = 25{,}920$ J. $$ N_{\text{cycles}} = \frac{25{,}920}{6.297 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4.117 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles} $$ Since one cycle = 1 second, the lifetime in seconds equals the number of cycles: $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} = 4.117 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} $$ Converting to days: $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} \text{ (days)} = \frac{4.117 \times 10^{7}}{86{,}400} \approx 476.5 \text{ days} $$ Converting to years: $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} \text{ (years)} = \frac{476.5}{365} \approx 1.31 \text{ years} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** A 1% duty cycle extends the lifetime to ~1.3 years for a 2-AA battery pack. A **comparison with an always-on node** (DC = 100%, $T_{\text{active}} = 1$ s) would yield: > $$E_{\text{cycle}}^{\text{always-on}} = 60 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1 = 0.06 \text{ J} \implies T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{25{,}920}{0.06} = 4.32 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \approx 5 \text{ days}$$ > **Energy savings = 95.3× via duty cycling.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CSMA/CA Throughput and Energy Trade-off **Problem Setup:** Consider a 1-persistent CSMA/CA system with $N$ contending nodes. The packet transmission time is $T_{\text{pkt}}$, and the propagation delay is $\tau$. The slot time is $a = \tau / T_{\text{pkt}}$. Each node transmits with probability $G/N$ per slot (Poisson arrival model). **Find:** The maximum throughput and the energy efficiency, defined as the ratio of useful payload energy to total energy expended per cycle. ### Solution **Step 1 — Probability of Successful Transmission:** For slotted CSMA, the probability that exactly one node transmits in a slot is: $$ P_s = \binom{N}{1} \cdot \left(\frac{G}{N}\right)^1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{G}{N}\right)^{N-1} $$ For large $N$ and small per-node load, this approximates to: $$ P_s \approx G \cdot e^{-G} $$ where $G$ is the **total offered load** in Erlangs. **Step 2 — Throughput Formula (Kleinrock's Slotted CSMA):** The normalized throughput is: $$ S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{G \cdot (1 + a) + e^{-G}} $$ The numerator is the fraction of slots that carry a *successful* transmission; the denominator accounts for the slot time wasted in propagation and the successful transmission time. **Step 3 — Maximum Throughput:** Differentiating with respect to $G$ and setting $\partial S / \partial G = 0$ yields the optimum load $G^*$: $$ G^* = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a + 1}} $$ Substituting back into $S$: $$ S_{\max} = \frac{G^* \cdot e^{-G^*}}{G^* \cdot (1 + a) + e^{-G^*}} = \frac{1}{e \cdot \sqrt{1 + a} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1+a}\right)} $$ For a typical WSN with $a = 0.01$ (i.e., $\tau = 1$% of packet time, which is realistic for low-bit-rate IEEE 802.15.4): $$ \sqrt{1 + a} = \sqrt{1.01} \approx 1.005 $$ $$ S_{\max} = \frac{1}{e \cdot 1.005 \cdot (1 + 1.005)} = \frac{1}{2.718 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 2.005} \approx \frac{1}{5.474} \approx 0.183 $$ **Result:** The maximum achievable throughput is approximately **18.3%** of the raw channel capacity, even under ideal slotted CSMA. This is a fundamental KTU insight: CSMA/CA is intrinsically inefficient under heavy load. **Step 4 — Energy Efficiency:** The energy per successfully delivered bit is: $$ \eta_E = \frac{E_{\text{tx, payload}}}{E_{\text{tx, total}} + E_{\text{collision}} + E_{\text{idle}}} $$ For the optimum load, the energy wasted on collisions is minimized but never zero. Practical WSN MAC protocols aim to push $\eta_E$ above **50%**, with **S-MAC achieving ~70%** under periodic traffic. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frame Error Rate and ARQ Energy Penalty **Problem Setup:** A WSN node sends 100-byte (800-bit) frames over an AWGN channel with a measured bit error rate $P_e = 10^{-5}$. ARQ is used with infinite retransmission attempts (simplified). **Find:** The probability that a frame is received correctly, the expected number of transmissions, and the energy penalty. ### Solution **Step 1 — Frame Error Rate:** Assuming independent bit errors: $$ P_{\text{FER}} = 1 - (1 - P_e)^n $$ where $n = 800$ bits. Using the approximation $(1 - P_e)^n \approx e^{-nP_e}$ for small $P_e$: $$ P_{\text{FER}} = 1 - e^{-800 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 - e^{-0.008} $$ Using Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + x^2/2 - \dots$: $$ e^{-0.008} \approx 1 - 0.008 + 0.000032 \approx 0.99203 $$ $$ P_{\text{FER}} \approx 1 - 0.99203 = 0.00797 \approx 0.8\% $$ **Step 2 — Probability of Correct Reception (PCR):** $$ P_{\text{correct}} = 1 - P_{\text{FER}} = 0.99203 $$ **Step 3 — Expected Number of Transmissions (Geometric Distribution):** For ARQ with independent retransmission trials: $$ E[N_{\text{tx}}] = \frac{1}{P_{\text{correct}}} = \frac{1}{0.99203} \approx 1.008 $$ **Step 4 — Energy Penalty:** If $E_{\text{tx}}$ is the energy for one transmission: $$ E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{tx}} \cdot E[N_{\text{tx}}] = E_{\text{tx}} \cdot 1.008 $$ Energy overhead: $0.8\%$ extra. This is a small cost at low $P_e$, but grows rapidly for noisy channels. For $P_e = 10^{-3}$: $$ P_{\text{FER}} = 1 - e^{-0.8} \approx 0.55, \quad E[N_{\text{tx}}] = \frac{1}{0.45} \approx 2.22 $$ → **More than 2× energy penalty!** This is why FEC is preferred over ARQ in high-BER WSN environments. > [!TIP] > **Exam Insight:** The crossover point at which ARQ becomes less energy-efficient than FEC is approximately $P_e > 10^{-4}$ for 100-byte frames. Beyond this threshold, the MAC layer should switch to a **Hybrid-ARQ** (HARQ) scheme. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | MAC Protocol | Type | Energy Mechanism | Synchronization | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pure CSMA/CA** | Contention | None (always on) | None | Simple, low delay | High idle listening | | **B-MAC** | Contention | LPL (Low-Power Listening) | Asynchronous | Robust, simple | Long preamble overhead | | **S-MAC** | Contention | Fixed duty cycle | Synchronized (virtual clusters) | Saves energy | Synchronization cost | | **T-MAC** | Contention | Adaptive duty cycle | Synchronized | Adapts to traffic | Early sleeping problem | | **WiseMAC** | Contention | Non-preamble sampling | Learned | Short preamble | Memory overhead | | **LEACH-MAC** | Schedule | TDMA + CH rotation | Cluster-head | Scalable, energy-balanced | Cluster formation cost | | **TRAMA** | Schedule | Traffic-adaptive slots | Global sync | High throughput | Complex, not scalable | | **DMAC** | Schedule | Tree-staggered wake-up | Tree-based | Low data-gathering latency | Sensitive to topology | | **Z-MAC** | Hybrid | CSMA under low load, TDMA under high load | Hybrid | Adaptive | High complexity | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Data Link Layer Functional Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Application Layer"] A1["Sensor Data, Queries, Events"] end subgraph NET["Network Layer"] N1["Routing, Addressing, Topology Control"] end subgraph DLL["Data Link Layer - Layer 2"] LLC["LLC Sublayer
Framing, Error Control, Flow Control"] MAC["MAC Sublayer
Channel Access, Duty Cycling"] SUB1["FEC, ARQ, HARQ"] SUB2["CSMA, TDMA, Hybrid"] SUB3["Sleep/Wake Scheduler"] LLC --- SUB1 MAC --- SUB2 MAC --- SUB3 end subgraph PHY["Physical Layer - Layer 1"] P1["Modulation, Radio, Channel Coding"] end A1 --> NET NET --> LLC SUB1 --> MAC SUB2 --> MAC MAC --> P1 P1 -.wireless channel.-> P1 ``` ## 4.2 WSN MAC Protocol Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB root["WSN MAC Protocols"] root --> A["Contention-Based"] root --> B["Schedule-Based TDMA"] root --> C["Hybrid"] A --> A1["Pure CSMA or CA"] A --> A2["B-MAC"] A --> A3["S-MAC"] A --> A4["T-MAC"] A --> A5["WiseMAC"] B --> B1["LEACH TDMA"] B --> B2["TRAMA"] B --> B3["DMAC"] B --> B4["BMA"] C --> C1["Z-MAC"] C --> C2["IEEE 802.15.4 Beacon Mode"] C --> C3["Funneling MAC"] ``` ## 4.3 S-MAC Sleep/Wake Cycle Timing Diagram ```mermaid gantt title S-MAC Synchronized Duty Cycle dateFormat ss axisFormat %S section Node A Sleep :a1, 00, 500ms Listen :a2, after a1, 50ms section Node B Sleep :b1, 00, 500ms Listen :b2, after b1, 50ms section Node C Sleep :c1, 100ms, 400ms Listen :c2, after c1, 50ms ``` > **Note:** In S-MAC, nodes form **virtual clusters** with synchronized sleep/wake schedules. The "listen" phase allows SYNC, RTS, and CTS exchange. Mismatched nodes (like Node C) follow their own schedule — this is the **border-node problem** in S-MAC. ## 4.4 IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR SF["Superframe Duration SD = 16 x 2^SO symbols"] SF --> CAP["Contention Access Period CAP - Slotted CSMA/CA"] SF --> GTS1["GTS 1 - Guaranteed Time Slot"] SF --> GTS2["GTS 2 - Guaranteed Time Slot"] SF --> GTSn["GTS n ..."] SF --> INACT["Inactive Period - Node Sleeps"] BEACON["Beacon Frame BO = 15.36 ms to 251.65 s"] --> SF ``` > **Key insight:** The **inactive period** is what makes IEEE 802.15.4 beacon-enabled mode energy-efficient. Nodes enter deep sleep during the inactive portion of the superframe. ## 4.5 Frame Structure of a Generic WSN Data Packet ```mermaid flowchart LR F["Generic WSN Data Frame"] F --> P["Preamble
4-8 bytes"] F --> S["Start Frame Delimiter SFD
1-2 bytes"] F --> L["Length Field
1 byte"] F --> D["Destination PAN ID + Address
2-4 bytes"] F --> P2["Payload
up to 127 bytes"] F --> FCS["Frame Check Sequence FCS / CRC
2 bytes"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** Define the Data Link Layer in Wireless Sensor Networks. List **any four major functions** performed by it. Why is CSMA/CD unsuitable for WSNs? **Model Answer:** The Data Link Layer is the second layer of the OSI protocol stack in a WSN. It is responsible for **framing, medium access control, error detection/correction, and link management** between adjacent sensor nodes sharing a common radio channel. **Four Major Functions:** 1. **Framing** – encapsulating Network layer packets into link-layer frames with headers, payloads, and trailers. 2. **Medium Access Control (MAC)** – regulating channel access to avoid collisions (CSMA, TDMA, hybrid). 3. **Error Control** – detecting and correcting bit errors using ARQ, FEC, or HARQ. 4. **Link Management** – neighbor discovery, synchronization, and duty cycling for energy efficiency. **Why CSMA/CD is Unsuitable:** WSN motes use **half-duplex radios**, which means they cannot transmit and receive simultaneously. CSMA/CD requires the node to **detect a collision while transmitting**, which is impossible in a half-duplex radio. Hence, WSNs use **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** with RTS/CTS handshaking. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (KTU):** Many students write only "wireless" as the reason. The **correct, full answer** must mention **half-duplex radios**. Always use the phrase **"CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD"** explicitly. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]** What are the **four major sources of energy waste** at the MAC layer of a WSN? Briefly explain any two. **Model Answer:** The four major sources of energy waste at the MAC layer (Ye, Heidemann, Estrin 2002) are: 1. **Collision** 2. **Overhearing** 3. **Control Packet Overhead** 4. **Idle Listening** (the largest contributor) **Explanations:** - **Collision:** When two or more nodes transmit simultaneously, the packets interfere and become corrupted, requiring retransmission — which wastes the energy of all the colliding transmissions. - **Idle Listening:** A node with nothing to send keeps its radio in receive mode, listening for possible traffic. In a low-traffic WSN, the node wastes far more energy on idle listening than on actual reception. This is the **single largest** source of energy waste. - **Overhearing:** A node receives a packet that was destined for a different node. Wasting receive energy on irrelevant traffic. - **Control Overhead:** Sending/receiving RTS, CTS, and ACK packets consumes energy without delivering application data. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (KTU):** Do not list "transmission energy" as a source of energy waste — transmission is *intentional*, not wasted. Only list the **four** items above; do not invent a fifth. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain in detail the architecture of the **S-MAC protocol** for Wireless Sensor Networks, including its three coordinated components. Discuss how S-MAC reduces energy consumption. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the **duty cycle, energy per cycle, and lifetime** for a WSN mote with the following parameters: $T_{\text{cycle}} = 2$ s, $T_{\text{active}} = 20$ ms, $P_{\text{active}} = 50$ mW, $P_{\text{sleep}} = 25$ µW, and $E_{\text{initial}} = 10{,}000$ J. (7 marks) --- ### Question A — Model Solution #### Part (a) — S-MAC Architecture (7 Marks) S-MAC (Sensor-MAC), proposed by **Ye, Heidemann, and Estrin (2002)**, is a contention-based MAC protocol with synchronized sleep/wake schedules. It has **three coordinated components**: **1. Periodic Listen and Sleep:** Each node follows a fixed cycle with a **listen period** (typically 10% of total) and a **sleep period** (typically 90%). All neighbors are organized into **virtual clusters** that share the same schedule. - During sleep, the radio is turned off. - During listen, the node can exchange SYNC, RTS, and CTS packets. This component addresses **idle listening** (the biggest source of energy waste). **2. Collision and Overhearing Avoidance:** S-MAC adopts the IEEE 802.11-style **RTS/CTS handshake** to address the hidden terminal problem and reduce collisions. It also uses **physical carrier sensing** and an **RTS/CTS/DATA/ACK** exchange to avoid collisions. To avoid overhearing, S-MAC lets a node go to sleep after hearing an RTS or CTS destined for another node. **3. Message Passing:** S-MAC transmits long messages as a **burst** using a single RTS/CTS exchange, fragmenting the message into smaller frames. The entire message is retransmitted only if any fragment fails. This reduces control overhead per message. **Energy Savings Achieved:** - Sleep mode power is orders of magnitude lower than active mode (e.g., 25 µW vs 50 mW). - Overhearing avoidance saves energy in dense networks. - Message passing amortizes the RTS/CTS cost over long messages. #### Part (b) — Duty Cycle, Energy, and Lifetime (7 Marks) **Given:** $T_{\text{cycle}} = 2$ s, $T_{\text{active}} = 20$ ms = $0.02$ s, $P_{\text{active}} = 50$ mW = $0.05$ W, $P_{\text{sleep}} = 25$ µW = $25 \times 10^{-6}$ W, $E_{\text{initial}} = 10{,}000$ J. **Step 1 — Duty Cycle:** $$ \text{DC} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{0.02}{2} = 0.01 = 1\% $$ **Step 2 — Sleep Time:** $$ T_{\text{sleep}} = T_{\text{cycle}} - T_{\text{active}} = 2 - 0.02 = 1.98 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Energy per Cycle:** $$ E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}} $$ $$ E_{\text{active}} = 0.05 \cdot 0.02 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{\text{sleep}} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.98 = 4.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} $$ $$ E_{\text{cycle}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 4.95 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0495 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} $$ **Step 4 — Lifetime:** $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{E_{\text{initial}}}{E_{\text{cycle}}} \cdot T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{10{,}000}{1.0495 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot 2 $$ $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} = 9.528 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2 = 1.9056 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} $$ Converting to days: $$ T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{1.9056 \times 10^{7}}{86{,}400} \approx 220.6 \text{ days} \approx 7.25 \text{ months} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (KTU):** Common mistakes in this derivation: > 1. Forgetting to convert **mW to W** and **ms to s** — losing 2 marks. > 2. Using $E_{\text{initial}}$ directly (in Wh) without converting to J — losing 3 marks. > 3. Computing $T_{\text{lifetime}}$ as the *number of cycles* and forgetting to multiply by $T_{\text{cycle}}$ — losing 2 marks. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **contention-based, schedule-based, and hybrid MAC protocols** for WSNs, with two examples and key trade-offs for each. (7 marks) **(b)** A WSN uses a 1-persistent slotted CSMA/CA protocol with $N = 10$ nodes. The packet transmission time is $T_{\text{pkt}} = 20$ ms and the propagation delay is $\tau = 0.5$ ms. Compute the **maximum normalized throughput** $S_{\max}$. (7 marks) --- ### Question B — Model Solution #### Part (a) — Comparison of MAC Protocol Classes (7 Marks) | Aspect | Contention-Based | Schedule-Based (TDMA) | Hybrid | |---|---|---|---| | **Access Mechanism** | CSMA/CA — random backoff | Pre-assigned time slots | Adaptive switching | | **Examples** | S-MAC, B-MAC, T-MAC | LEACH-MAC, TRAMA, DMAC | Z-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 beacon | | **Collision Handling** | Probabilistic (backoff) | Collision-free by design | Trade-off based on load | | **Synchronization** | May or may not need | Strictly required | Partial | | **Energy Efficiency (low load)** | High (idle sleeping easy) | Low (idle slots wasted) | High | | **Energy Efficiency (high load)** | Low (many collisions) | High (no collisions) | High | | **Scalability** | High (no central coord.) | Limited (slot assignment) | Medium | | **Latency** | Variable, can be high | Bounded ($N \cdot T_{\text{slot}}/2$) | Bounded | | **Best Suited For** | Event-driven, low traffic | Periodic, high traffic | Mixed traffic patterns | **Examples Detailed:** 1. **S-MAC (Contention):** Uses synchronized listen/sleep cycles + RTS/CTS. Energy-efficient under low load, but suffers from synchronization overhead and "early sleeping" problem. 2. **LEACH-MAC (Schedule):** Forms clusters; each cluster head assigns TDMA slots to its members. Excellent for periodic data collection. Energy-balanced due to rotating cluster heads. 3. **Z-MAC (Hybrid):** Operates in CSMA mode under low contention, and switches to TDMA mode when contention rises. Combines robustness of CSMA with efficiency of TDMA. #### Part (b) — Maximum Slotted CSMA/CA Throughput (7 Marks) **Given:** $N = 10$ nodes, $T_{\text{pkt}} = 20$ ms, $\tau = 0.5$ ms. **Step 1 — Compute the propagation ratio $a$:** $$ a = \frac{\tau}{T_{\text{pkt}}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.025 $$ **Step 2 — Optimum load $G^*$:** $$ G^* = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + a}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.025}} = \frac{1}{1.01242} \approx 0.9877 $$ **Step 3 — Maximum throughput formula (Slotted CSMA):** $$ S_{\max} = \frac{G^* \cdot e^{-G^*}}{G^* \cdot (1 + a) + e^{-G^*}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the components:** $$ e^{-G^*} = e^{-0.9877} \approx 0.3723 $$ $$ \text{Numerator} = G^* \cdot e^{-G^*} = 0.9877 \cdot 0.3723 \approx 0.3678 $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = G^* \cdot (1 + a) + e^{-G^*} = 0.9877 \cdot 1.025 + 0.3723 $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = 1.0124 + 0.3723 = 1.3847 $$ **Step 5 — Final Throughput:** $$ S_{\max} = \frac{0.3678}{1.3847} \approx 0.2657 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The maximum normalized throughput is **$S_{\max} \approx 0.266$** (or 26.6%). This means even at the optimum load, only about 1/4 of the channel capacity is used for successful packet delivery; the rest is wasted on backoff, propagation, and idle slots. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (KTU):** Common mistakes: > 1. Using the **unslotted** CSMA formula by mistake — losing 2 marks. > 2. Forgetting to compute $G^*$ first — losing 2 marks. > 3. Using $a$ in the wrong formula (CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA) — losing 3 marks. > 4. Not converting ms correctly — losing 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Data Link Layer in WSN** - **DLL Position:** Layer 2 of the OSI model; divided into **LLC (upper)** and **MAC (lower)** sublayers. - **Primary Functions:** Framing, MAC, Error Control, Link Management. - **Key Difference from Wired:** Energy efficiency dominates over throughput, latency, and fairness. - **Four Energy Waste Sources:** Collision, Overhearing, Idle Listening, Control Overhead — ordered by impact. - **CSMA/CA vs CSMA/CD:** WSN uses CA because motes are **half-duplex**; CD is for full-duplex wired Ethernet. - **Contention-Based MACs:** Pure CSMA, B-MAC (LPL), S-MAC (sync duty cycle), T-MAC (adaptive), WiseMAC (sampled listening). - **Schedule-Based MACs:** LEACH, TRAMA, DMAC, BMA — all TDMA variants. - **Hybrid MACs:** Z-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 beacon mode with GTS, Funneling MAC. - **S-MAC Components (3):** Periodic Listen/Sleep, Collision & Overhearing Avoidance, Message Passing. - **Duty Cycle Formula:** $\text{DC} = T_{\text{active}} / T_{\text{cycle}}$ — KTU board default 1%–10% range. - **Energy per Cycle:** $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{active}} T_{\text{active}} + P_{\text{sleep}} T_{\text{sleep}}$. - **Lifetime Formula:** $L_T = (E_{\text{initial}} / E_{\text{cycle}}) \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$. - **CSMA Throughput:** $S = G e^{-G} / [G(1+a) + e^{-G}]$; max occurs at $G^* = 1/\sqrt{1+a}$. - **ARQ Energy Penalty:** $E_{\text{ARQ}} = E_{\text{tx}} / (1 - P_{\text{FER}})$. - **FEC vs ARQ:** ARQ is energy-efficient at low $P_e$ (< 10⁻⁴); FEC is preferred at high $P_e$. - **IEEE 802.15.4 Standard:** Operates in 2.4 GHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz bands; data rates 20–250 kbps; the basis of Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, Thread. - **Superframe Structure:** Beacon → CAP (slotted CSMA/CA) → GTS slots → Inactive Period (sleep). - **Key Insight:** The **inactive period** of the superframe is what makes 802.15.4 energy-efficient for WSNs. - **Most Common Mistake to Avoid:** Confusing **CSMA/CA** with **CSMA/CD**; CSMA/CD is **not used** in any WSN. - **Numerical Conversion Always Required:** mW → W, µW → W, ms → s, µs → s, Wh → J (multiply by 3600). - **Time-First Approach:** Always convert all times to **seconds** before substituting into energy formulas. - **Expected Board Weightage:** 2-mark definition + 5-mark MAC comparison + 7-mark numerical derivation = **~14 marks per question** (Module 1 typically has 1 of the 2 long-answer slots).

topics.title: MAC Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** MAC Protocols ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs), a **Medium Access Control (MAC) Protocol** is a sub-layer protocol of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2 in the OSI/TCP-IP model) that governs **how multiple sensor nodes share a common wireless radio medium** to transmit and receive data without destructive interference. Unlike traditional wireless MAC protocols (e.g., IEEE 802.11), WSN MAC protocols are primarily **energy-centric**, because communication is the single largest consumer of battery power in a sensor node — typically orders of magnitude more than sensing or computation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A WSN MAC protocol is the set of rules that arbitrate access to the shared wireless channel among energy-constrained sensor nodes, with the **primary objective of minimizing energy consumption** while supporting the application's reliability, latency, and throughput requirements. ### 1.2 Why MAC is the Most Critical Layer in a WSN Energy consumption in a sensor node is dominated by the radio. Studies (Ye, Heidemann, Estrin – 2002) have shown that an idle radio consumes nearly the same power as one actively receiving. Therefore, **how long the radio is ON** directly determines battery lifetime. The MAC protocol decides: - **When** a node should keep its radio ON. - **When** it should put the radio into sleep (power-down). - **How** collisions, overhearing, and idle listening are avoided. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cocktail Party" Problem > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a large room where everyone wants to talk at the same time, but only one person can be heard clearly. In traditional Wi-Fi, the etiquette is to wait for silence, then quickly speak. In a WSN, however, the "people" are tiny battery-powered devices. The MAC protocol is essentially the **rulebook of politeness** — it tells each node exactly *when* it is allowed to speak, *when* it must listen, and crucially, *when* it can put its "ears" to sleep to save battery. A poorly designed rulebook causes everyone to keep their ears open forever, draining batteries. A well-designed rulebook synchronizes sleepy schedules so that conversation is possible, but ears are open only a small fraction of the time. ### 1.4 Energy Waste Sources (The "Four Horsemen" of WSN MAC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **According to Ye et al. (S-MAC paper), the four main sources of energy waste that any good WSN MAC protocol must address:** > > 1. **Collisions** — Two nodes transmit simultaneously; both packets are corrupted and must be retransmitted. (Cost: wasted transmit energy + retransmit energy.) > 2. **Overhearing** — A node receives packets destined for other neighbors. (Cost: wasted receive energy.) > 3. **Control Packet Overhead** — Sending/receiving RTS, CTS, ACK, SYNC, beacons instead of actual data. (Cost: bandwidth + energy for headers.) > 4. **Idle Listening** — Listening to an idle channel to check for incoming traffic. (Cost: ~ same power as receiving, often the **dominant** waste.) A fifth modern source, **overemitting** (transmitting when the receiver is not ready), is also minimized by MAC design. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Goals of a WSN MAC Protocol | # | Design Goal | Why it Matters in WSN | |---|------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Energy Efficiency** | Primary goal; nodes are battery-powered and often deployed in inaccessible areas. | | 2 | **Scalability** | Network may grow to thousands of nodes; MAC must handle variable density. | | 3 | **Collision Avoidance** | Reduces retransmissions and hence energy. | | 4 | **Latency Control** | Some applications (e.g., event detection) need bounded delay. | | 5 | **Throughput** | Adequate bandwidth utilization for the sensed traffic. | | 6 | **Fairness / QoS** | Less critical in WSN than in WLAN, but important for critical data. | ### 2.2 Taxonomy of WSN MAC Protocols WSN MAC protocols are broadly classified into three families (this is a high-yield KTU topic): 1. **Contention-Based MAC Protocols** — Nodes compete for the channel using CSMA/CA or similar. - Examples: **S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, WiseMAC, DSMAC** 2. **Schedule-Based (TDMA) MAC Protocols** — Time is divided into slots; each node owns a slot. - Examples: **LEACH-MAC, TRAMA, DMAC** 3. **Hybrid MAC Protocols** — Combine contention for control + schedule for data. - Example: **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee), Z-MAC** ### 2.3 Deep Dive — S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) **S-MAC** (Ye et al., 2002) is the canonical energy-efficient WSN MAC. It introduces three key ideas: - **Periodic Listen and Sleep** — Each node cycles through a LISTEN state and a SLEEP state. Duty cycle is reduced from 100 % (always-on) to about **10 %**. - **Synchronization via SYNC Packets** — Neighbors exchange schedule information so that they wake up at the same time. - **Virtual Clusters** — Nodes sharing the same schedule form a logical cluster, even though they are not physically grouped. - **Collision and Overhearing Avoidance** — Uses physical/virtual carrier sense and RTS/CTS. - **Message Passing** — Long messages are fragmented; all fragments are sent in one burst using a single RTS/CTS, saving control overhead. **S-MAC Drawback:** Fixed duty cycle may not adapt to varying traffic — leads to **T-MAC** (Timeout-MAC) which dynamically ends the active period if no traffic is sensed. ### 2.4 Deep Dive — B-MAC (Berkeley MAC) B-MAC uses **Low Power Listening (LPL)** also called **Preamble Sampling**: - Each data packet is preceded by a **long preamble** longer than the receiver's sleep period. - The receiver wakes briefly to perform **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)**. - If channel is busy, the receiver stays awake to receive the packet. - **No synchronization required** — making B-MAC highly scalable and robust to topology changes. ### 2.5 Deep Dive — IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee MAC (Hybrid) IEEE 802.15.4 is the **industry-standard** MAC for low-rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs) and is the foundation of **ZigBee**. It supports two modes: - **Beacon-Enabled Mode** — Coordinator sends periodic **beacons**; nodes use slotted CSMA/CA within a structured **superframe**. - **Non-Beacon-Enabled Mode** — No beacons; unslotted CSMA/CA; simpler but less power-efficient. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL: For markdown compatibility, the vertical bar $\vert$ symbol is used inside tables instead of the raw pipe character.** | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Meaning | |-------------------|------------|---------------|---------| | Duty Cycle (D) | $D = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Fraction of time radio is ON. | | Energy in Listen State | $E_{listen} = P_{rx} \times t_{listen}$ | Joules | Idle listening cost. | | Beacon Interval (BI) | $BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}$ | Symbols / ms | Total time between two consecutive beacons. | | Superframe Duration (SD) | $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}$ | Symbols / ms | Active portion within a BI. | | Base Superframe Duration | $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ | Symbols (= 15.36 ms @ 2.4 GHz O-QPSK) | Fixed by IEEE 802.15.4 standard. | | Duty Cycle (802.15.4) | $D = \dfrac{SD}{BI} = \dfrac{2^{SO}}{2^{BO}} = \dfrac{1}{2^{BO-SO}}$ | Ratio / % | Must satisfy $0 \le SO \le BO \le 14$. | | Number of Backoff Slots in CAP | — | Integer | Determined by contention window. | | Maximum Frame Size | 127 | Bytes | IEEE 802.15.4 MAC payload limit. | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Agriculture** — Soil moisture sensors use **802.15.4 with long SO/short BO** to sleep for hours, running on a coin cell for years. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — ZigBee mesh networks on factory floors use hybrid MAC for reliability + battery life. - **Healthcare Monitoring** — Body-worn sensors use S-MAC-like duty cycling so wearable batteries last weeks. - **Structural Health Monitoring** — B-MAC-style LPL suits the asynchronous, low-traffic nature of bridge sensors. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Duty Cycle, BO, and SO in IEEE 802.15.4 The following derivation is a **favourite KTU question** (and is guaranteed to appear in some form in the ESE). **Step 1 — Starting definition of Beacon Interval.** The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines the time between two consecutive beacon transmissions as: $$ BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO} $$ where: - $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 \text{ symbols} = 15.36 \text{ ms}$ (at 2.4 GHz O-QPSK PHY). - $BO$ = **Beacon Order**, an integer in $0 \le BO \le 14$. **Step 2 — Definition of Superframe Duration.** The active (ON) portion of the superframe is: $$ SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} $$ where $SO$ = **Superframe Order**, with $0 \le SO \le BO \le 14$. **Step 3 — Expressing Duty Cycle.** Duty cycle is the fraction of total time the radio is active: $$ D = \frac{SD}{BI} $$ **Step 4 — Substituting the expressions for SD and BI.** $$ D = \frac{aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}}{aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}} $$ **Step 5 — Cancelling common terms.** The constant $aBaseSuperframeDuration$ appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels out: $$ D = \frac{2^{SO}}{2^{BO}} $$ **Step 6 — Using exponent law $a^{m}/a^{n} = a^{m-n}$.** $$ D = 2^{SO - BO} = 2^{-(BO - SO)} $$ **Step 7 — Final, KTU-favourite form.** $$ \boxed{\,D = \frac{1}{2^{\,BO - SO}}\,} $$ **Step 8 — Example Numerical Evaluation.** > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):** Given $BO = 4$ and $SO = 2$, find the duty cycle in % and the actual time durations. First, duty cycle: $$ D = \frac{1}{2^{BO - SO}} = \frac{1}{2^{4-2}} = \frac{1}{2^{2}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$ Converting to percentage: $$ D_{\%} = 0.25 \times 100\,\% = 25\,\% $$ Beacon Interval: $$ BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO} = 15.36 \text{ ms} \times 2^{4} = 15.36 \times 16 = 245.76 \text{ ms} $$ Superframe Duration: $$ SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} = 15.36 \text{ ms} \times 2^{2} = 15.36 \times 4 = 61.44 \text{ ms} $$ **Verification:** $SD / BI = 61.44 / 245.76 = 0.25 = 25\,\%$. The formula is consistent. **Energy implication:** A node sleeps for $BI - SD = 184.32$ ms in every 245.76 ms cycle, saving roughly **75 %** of the radio's energy compared to always-on operation. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major WSN MAC Protocols | Protocol | Type | Synchronization | Duty Cycle | Collision Avoidance | Latency | Strength | Weakness | |----------|------|----------------|------------|---------------------|---------|----------|----------| | **S-MAC** | Contention (CSMA) | Yes (SYNC packets) | Fixed (~10 %) | RTS/CTS + virtual/physical CS | Moderate (improved by adaptive listen) | Simple, low energy | Fixed duty cycle wastes energy at low traffic | | **T-MAC** | Contention (CSMA) | Yes (inherits S-MAC) | Adaptive (timeout) | RTS/CTS | Moderate | Better than S-MAC under variable load | Early-sleeping problem | | **B-MAC** | Contention (LPL) | No (async) | Adaptive | CCA + ACK | Higher (long preamble) | Very simple, highly scalable | Long preamble wastes bandwidth & energy at high load | | **WiseMAC** | Contention (LPL) | No (learns neighbors) | Adaptive | CCA + preamble | Lower than B-MAC | Short preamble if neighbor schedules are known | Requires neighbor schedule table | | **DMAC** | Schedule (TDMA-like) | Yes | Data-tree based | Slot assignment | Low (data gathering trees) | Low latency for convergecast | Specific to tree topologies | | **IEEE 802.15.4** | Hybrid | Yes (beacon) | Configurable (BO, SO) | Slotted CSMA/CA + GTS | Configurable (GTS gives bounded latency) | Industry standard, interoperable | Beacon scheduling complexity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matrix matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to *compare* two MAC protocols (often S-MAC vs B-MAC, or 802.15.4 vs S-MAC). The table above is your "ready-reckoner" — memorize the distinguishing axis (sync vs async, fixed vs adaptive duty cycle, latency vs scalability). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture: S-MAC Periodic Listen/Sleep Cycle with Virtual Cluster Synchronization ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef listen fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sleep fill:#6FA8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sync fill:#93C47D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef data fill:#E06666,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["Node Wakes Up"]:::listen --> B{"Receive SYNC from
any neighbor?"}:::sync B -- Yes --> C["Adopt that
schedule"]:::sync B -- No --> D["Pick random
own schedule"]:::sync C --> E["Broadcast MY
SYNC packet"]:::sync D --> E E --> F["Enter LISTEN
phase (active)"]:::listen F --> G{"RTS/CTS
exchange?"}:::listen G -- Yes --> H["Transmit/Receive
DATA fragments"]:::data G -- No --> I["No traffic
detected"]:::listen H --> J["Go to SLEEP
phase"]:::sleep I --> J J --> K["Sleep for
T_sleep duration"]:::sleep K --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each node alternates between a short LISTEN state (yellow/red) and a long SLEEP state (blue). During LISTEN, it first exchanges SYNC packets to maintain schedule alignment with neighbors, then attempts RTS/CTS. If no traffic is detected, the node returns to SLEEP, conserving energy. ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: IEEE 802.15.4 Beacon-Enabled Superframe ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef beacon fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cap fill:#E6B8B7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cfp fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inactive fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 BI["Beacon Interval BI"]:::inactive B1["Beacon"]:::beacon CAP["Contention Access
Period CAP
slotted CSMA/CA"]:::cap CFP["Contention Free
Period CFP
GTS slots"]:::cfp INACT["Inactive Period
node SLEEPS"]:::inactive B2["Next Beacon"]:::beacon BI --> B1 --> CAP --> CFP --> INACT --> B2 ``` **Reading the diagram:** A full Beacon Interval (BI) is composed of an active Superframe Duration (SD = CAP + CFP) and an optional Inactive period. During the Inactive period, all nodes can power down their radios. By tuning $BO$ and $SO$, the KTU engineer directly controls the duty cycle. ### 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Synchronous vs Asynchronous MAC ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef a fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef b fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px A["SYNCHRONOUS MAC
(S-MAC, T-MAC, 802.15.4 Beacon)
Pros: Low duty cycle
Cons: Sync overhead,
clock drift"]:::a B["ASYNCHRONOUS MAC
(B-MAC, WiseMAC, X-MAC)
Pros: No sync, scalable
Cons: Long preamble
cost"]:::b A --- C["Selection depends on
traffic pattern, mobility,
node density"]:::a B --- C ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 marks]** *List any **four major sources of energy waste** in a wireless sensor node that a MAC protocol must aim to minimize.** **Model Answer:** The four main sources of energy waste in WSN MAC design are: 1. **Collisions** — simultaneous transmissions causing corrupted packets that must be retransmitted. 2. **Overhearing** — a node receives packets intended for another node. 3. **Control packet overhead** — energy spent transmitting/receiving MAC headers, RTS, CTS, ACK instead of payload. 4. **Idle listening** — keeping the radio ON while waiting for traffic, which is the **largest** waste because the receive power is comparable to transmit power. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** A common student mistake is to include "transmission energy" as a source of waste. Transmission energy is *necessary* — the four listed above are *avoidable* wastes. Writing only "transmission" or "battery drain" will fetch zero marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 marks]** *Define **duty cycle** in the context of a WSN MAC protocol. Why is reducing duty cycle important?** **Model Answer:** **Duty cycle** is the fraction of time a sensor node's radio is in the active (LISTEN/TX/RX) state relative to the total cycle time, expressed as: $$ D = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} \times 100\,\% $$ It is important because the radio's idle/active power is the **dominant** energy consumer in a sensor node. By reducing the duty cycle (e.g., from 100 % to 10 %), the radio is in SLEEP mode for 90 % of the time, drastically extending battery life. Hence, **energy efficiency in WSN = duty cycle minimization + collision avoidance**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: A or B, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) #### ⭐ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose this OR Question B)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 14-mark question]** *"Explain the design goals and challenges of MAC protocol design in Wireless Sensor Networks. Discuss in detail the **four major sources of energy waste** identified by Ye et al., with examples of how the S-MAC protocol mitigates each."* **Part (a) — 7 marks: Design Goals and Challenges** **Model Solution:** | S.No | Design Goal | Explanation | |------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | **Energy Efficiency** | The single most important goal; nodes are battery-powered, often deployed in hostile/inaccessible areas where battery replacement is impossible. MAC must minimize radio ON time. | | 2 | **Scalability** | A WSN may consist of 100s to 10,000s of nodes. MAC must perform well with changing network density. | | 3 | **Collision Avoidance** | Collisions force retransmissions, wasting energy. Must use CSMA, TDMA, or hybrid schemes. | | 4 | **Latency Control** | Event-driven applications (e.g., fire detection) need bounded delay. Sleep schedules inherently add latency, which MAC must manage. | | 5 | **Throughput** | Must deliver sensed data reliably to the sink. | | 6 | **Fairness** | Less critical in WSN, but required in some monitoring applications. | **Challenges:** - **Limited energy budget** — must conserve at all layers. - **Limited hardware capability** — small buffers, low processing power. - **Application-specific traffic patterns** — one-size-fits-all MAC is not optimal. - **Unreliable wireless channel** — fading, interference. - **Node mobility (in some WSNs)** — schedules must adapt. - **Dense deployment** — high contention. **Part (b) — 7 marks: Four Energy Waste Sources & S-MAC's Mitigation** | Source of Waste | Description | How S-MAC Mitigates | |-----------------|-------------|---------------------| | **1. Collisions** | Two nodes transmit at the same time on the same channel, corrupting both packets. | S-MAC uses **physical and virtual carrier sense** + **RTS/CTS handshake** before any data transmission, ensuring that the channel is reserved. | | **2. Overhearing** | A node receives a packet not addressed to it. | S-MAC's RTS/CTS includes the **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** field — overhearing nodes go to SLEEP after hearing RTS/CTS not meant for them. | | **3. Control Packet Overhead** | Heavy MAC headers, beacons, ACKs consume bandwidth and energy. | S-MAC uses **message passing** — long messages are fragmented and sent as a single burst using one RTS/CTS, reducing per-fragment overhead. | | **4. Idle Listening** | Listening to an idle channel waiting for traffic. | S-MAC's **periodic sleep** with ~10 % duty cycle keeps the radio OFF for ~90 % of the time — the **most significant** energy saver. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** write generic statements like "MAC is used to save energy." The examiner expects you to *name* the four waste sources (collisions, overhearing, control overhead, idle listening) — forgetting even one will cost 1–2 marks. > - **Do NOT** confuse S-MAC's sleep cycle with the IEEE 802.15.4 superframe. They are *different* mechanisms. If the question asks for S-MAC, mentioning beacons or GTS will not earn credit. > - In sub-part (a), students often list only 2–3 goals. The examiner allocates marks *proportional* to the number of goals clearly explained. Aim for at least 4 well-explained goals. --- #### ⭐ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model 14-mark question]** *"With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **superframe structure of IEEE 802.15.4 MAC** in beacon-enabled mode. Derive the relationship between the **Beacon Interval (BI), Superframe Duration (SD), Beacon Order (BO) and Superframe Order (SO)**. Given $BO = 4$ and $SO = 2$, calculate the duty cycle and verify the result."* **Part (a) — 7 marks: Superframe Structure with Diagram** **Model Solution:** The IEEE 802.15.4 MAC in **beacon-enabled mode** structures channel time into a recurring pattern called a **superframe**, bounded by **beacon frames** transmitted by the PAN coordinator. The superframe has two parts: 1. **Active Portion** (Superframe Duration, SD): - **Beacon (B)** — sent at the start, contains synchronization and superframe structure info. - **Contention Access Period (CAP)** — uses **slotted CSMA/CA**; all nodes compete for channel access. - **Contention Free Period (CFP)** — contains up to **7 Guaranteed Time Slots (GTS)** reserved for specific nodes requiring QoS. 2. **Inactive Portion** — During this period, the coordinator and devices may enter a **low-power mode** (sleep) to conserve energy. No transmissions occur. **Diagram (must be drawn in the answer sheet):** $$ \underbrace{[\,\text{Beacon} \mid \text{CAP} \mid \text{CFP}\,]}_{\text{Active (SD = }aBaseSD \times 2^{SO}\text{)}} \quad \underbrace{[\,\text{INACTIVE}\,]}_{\text{Sleep}} \quad \underbrace{[\,\text{Beacon} \mid \ldots\,]}_{\text{Next Superframe}} $$ The total period between two consecutive beacons is the **Beacon Interval (BI)**. **Part (b) — 7 marks: Derivation and Numerical Verification** The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines: $$ BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}, \quad SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} $$ $$ aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 \text{ symbols} = 15.36 \text{ ms} $$ **Duty Cycle derivation (full steps required):** $$ D = \frac{SD}{BI} = \frac{15.36 \times 2^{SO}}{15.36 \times 2^{BO}} = 2^{SO-BO} = \frac{1}{2^{BO-SO}} $$ **Numerical verification for $BO=4$, $SO=2$:** $$ D = \frac{1}{2^{4-2}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\,\% $$ $$ BI = 15.36 \times 2^{4} = 15.36 \times 16 = 245.76 \text{ ms} $$ $$ SD = 15.36 \times 2^{2} = 15.36 \times 4 = 61.44 \text{ ms} $$ **Verification:** $SD/BI = 61.44 / 245.76 = 0.25 = 25\,\%$ ✔ **Energy implication:** The node sleeps for $BI - SD = 184.32$ ms out of every 245.76 ms, saving **75 %** of the radio's energy compared to continuous operation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting the value of $aBaseSuperframeDuration$** (960 symbols / 15.36 ms) is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in the numerical part. The examiner may *not* give this value in the question — you must know it. > - Students often write $D = 2^{BO-SO}$ instead of $2^{SO-BO}$ — sign error. Always derive from $SD/BI$ to avoid this. > - In the diagram, students frequently forget to label the **Inactive period** or the **GTS slots in the CFP**. The examiner explicitly checks for these. > - You MUST show *all* algebraic steps in the derivation. Writing only the final formula $D = 1/2^{BO-SO}$ without intermediate steps will not get full credit. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final high-density recap — read this on the morning of the exam:** - ✅ **MAC in WSN = energy-efficient channel access** for battery-limited sensor nodes. - ✅ The **four energy waste sources** (Ye et al.) are: **C**ollisions, **O**verhearing, **C**ontrol overhead, **I**dle listening (mnemonic: **C-O-C-I**). - ✅ **Duty cycle** = (Active time) / (Total cycle time) × 100 %. Lower duty cycle ⇒ longer battery life. - ✅ **S-MAC** = Contention, synchronized, **fixed** duty cycle (~10 %), uses RTS/CTS + virtual clusters + SYNC packets. - ✅ **T-MAC** = Adaptive duty cycle via timeout; better than S-MAC under variable load. - ✅ **B-MAC** = Asynchronous, uses **Low Power Listening (LPL)** with long preambles + CCA. No sync needed. - ✅ **WiseMAC** = Asynchronous LPL with **learned neighbor schedules** → shorter preamble than B-MAC. - ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4** = Industry standard, hybrid MAC, beacon-enabled mode uses **superframe = Beacon + CAP + CFP**, followed by **Inactive period**. - ✅ **Key formulas (must be memorized):** - $BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}$ - $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}$ - $D = SD/BI = 1/2^{BO-SO}$ - $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 \text{ symbols} = 15.36 \text{ ms}$ - ✅ Constraint: $0 \le SO \le BO \le 14$. - ✅ When $SO = BO$, duty cycle = 100 % (no sleep); when $SO = 0$, duty cycle is at its minimum. - ✅ For 14-mark answers: **always include a labeled diagram**, **list 4+ design goals**, **name all four energy wastes**, and **derive every formula step-by-step**. - ✅ Common sign error: $D = 1/2^{BO-SO}$ — derive from $SD/BI$ to be safe.

topics.title: Wireless Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Wireless Internet ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Wireless Internet** in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) refers to the seamless integration and interconnection of resource-constrained, low-power sensor nodes with the global TCP/IP-based Internet infrastructure, enabling remote monitoring, querying, data collection, and control of physical environments through standard web and internet protocols. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Wireless Internet is the architectural framework that bridges a self-organizing, multi-hop, energy-constrained sensor field (the *Intranet of Things*) to the global Internet via one or more **Gateway Nodes** (also called *Sink Nodes*, *Base Stations*, or *Edge Routers*), thereby allowing bi-directional IP-based communication between the cyber-world and the physical world. The three foundational pillars of the Wireless Internet paradigm are: 1. **Sensing Layer** — The physical sensor field collecting data. 2. **Network Layer** — Multi-hop wireless routing (e.g., RPL, AODV). 3. **Application Layer** — Internet-based remote access (e.g., HTTP, CoAP, MQTT). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Postal Service** > > Imagine a small village (the sensor network) where every house has a letter (data) but no house has a direct phone line. Each house can only pass letters to its immediate neighbor. Eventually, all letters reach the **Post Office** (the **Gateway/Sink Node**). From there, the letters are dispatched globally via the **Internet Postal Highway** (TCP/IP backbone) to recipients anywhere in the world (remote users/clouds). > > The key insight: **No house in the village needs to know the full global address system** — it just forwards the letter hop-by-hop until it reaches the Post Office, which speaks the global language (IP). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Standard WSN Internetworking Protocol Stack Bandwidth:** Typically **20–250 kbps** (IEEE 802.15.4 PHY layer at 2.4 GHz offers **250 kbps**). - **IPv6 Header Size:** **40 bytes** (vs. IPv4's 20 bytes). - **Maximum IEEE 802.15.4 Frame Payload:** Only **127 bytes** — a critical bottleneck for IPv6 integration. - **6LoWPAN Compression Target:** Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header down to a few bytes (typically **2–4 bytes**). - **Typical Sensor Node Memory:** **4 KB – 128 KB RAM**, **48 KB – 512 KB Flash**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Traditional TCP/IP Cannot Run Directly on Sensor Nodes Sensor nodes are fundamentally incompatible with the traditional Internet Protocol stack due to hardware, energy, and bandwidth limitations. Let us break this down logically: - **Step 1: Packet Size Mismatch** - IPv6 mandates a Minimum MTU of **1280 bytes**. - IEEE 802.15.4 allows a maximum frame payload of only **127 bytes**. - **Result:** A single IPv6 packet must be fragmented across multiple 802.15.4 frames, increasing overhead and energy consumption. - **Step 2: Addressing Incompatibility** - IPv6 uses **128-bit addresses** (e.g., `2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334`). - Storing and processing 128-bit addresses exhausts the limited RAM of motes. - **Step 3: Power Constraint** - Each transmitted bit costs energy: roughly **E = V · I · t** (Voltage × Current × Time). - Sending 1280-byte IPv6 packets would drain battery budgets of small nodes. - **Step 4: Host-Based vs. Embedded Routing** - The Internet assumes always-on, addressable hosts. - Sensor nodes may **sleep** for minutes/hours to conserve energy — they are not always reachable. - **Step 5: Data-Centric vs. Address-Centric** - The Internet is **address-centric** (who is sending?). - WSNs are **data-centric** (what is the temperature?). ### 2.2 The Wireless Internet Architecture: End-to-End View A typical Wireless Internet deployment is divided into **three distinct tiers**: | Tier | Name | Components | Function | |------|------|-----------|----------| | **Tier 1** | Sensing Field | Sensor motes (end devices) | Sense physical phenomena (temp, humidity, vibration) | | **Tier 2** | Gateway / Edge Layer | 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR), sink | Protocol translation, aggregation, security | | **Tier 3** | Internet / Cloud | Web servers, databases, mobile apps, MQTT brokers | Storage, analytics, visualization, actuation | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Equations for Wireless Internet** $$ \boxed{ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Meaning} & \textbf{Engineering Utility} \\ \hline E_{tx} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{bit} & \text{Energy per bit transmission} & \text{Battery lifetime estimation} \\ \hline T_{total} = \frac{L}{R} & \text{Transmission time (L=bits, R=bps)} & \text{Channel occupancy analysis} \\ \hline \text{MTU}_{802.15.4} = 127 \text{ bytes} & \text{Max PHY payload} & \text{Fragmentation count} \\ \hline N_{fragments} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{MTU}_{IPv6}}{127 - H_{6LoWPAN}} \right\rceil & \text{Number of 802.15.4 frames} & \text{Overhead calculation} \\ \hline \text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{H_{IPv6}}{H_{6LoWPAN}} & \text{Header compression efficiency} & \text{Throughput gain (typically 40:1)} \\ \hline D_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} d_i & \text{End-to-end delay over h hops} & \text{QoS design} \\ \hline \text{PLR} = 1 - (1-p)^h & \text{Packet Loss Rate (p=link loss, h=hops)} & \text{Reliability analysis} \\ \hline \end{array} } $$ > **Note:** Vertical bars are intentionally avoided using `\vert`/`\mid` and `\\lceil \\rceil` so the markdown table structure remains intact. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Wireless Internet - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors reporting via 6LoWPAN to a cloud dashboard accessed by farmers on smartphones. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Machine vibration sensors on factory floors reporting predictive-maintenance data to a central SCADA system over the Internet. - **Smart Cities:** Air-quality monitors deployed across a city publishing real-time pollution data to a public web portal. - **Healthcare Body Area Networks (BANs):** Wearable ECG sensors relaying patient vitals to hospital servers. - **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest-fire detection networks sending alerts via satellite backhaul to emergency services. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Fragmentation Count of an IPv6 Packet over IEEE 802.15.4 **Problem Statement:** An IPv6 packet carrying a CoAP message has a total size of **1280 bytes** (the minimum MTU mandated by IPv6). It is to be transmitted over an IEEE 802.15.4 link. The 6LoWPAN header consumes **4 bytes** of the 127-byte frame payload. Compute: - (a) The number of 802.15.4 fragments required. - (b) The total protocol overhead (in bytes). - (c) The header compression ratio. **Given:** - IPv6 packet size: $L_{IPv6} = 1280 \text{ bytes}$ - IEEE 802.15.4 maximum payload: $L_{max} = 127 \text{ bytes}$ - 6LoWPAN compressed header size: $H_{6LoWPAN} = 4 \text{ bytes}$ - Mesh-under / multi-hop header: $H_{mesh} = 5 \text{ bytes}$ (considered only on the first fragment if mesh routing is used; for simplicity here we take the first fragment header as 5 bytes and continuation fragments as 5 bytes) **Step 1: Available payload per fragment** The first fragment carries the dispatch + IPv6 compressed header + mesh header. $$ L_{avail}^{(1)} = L_{max} - H_{6LoWPAN} - H_{mesh} $$ $$ L_{avail}^{(1)} = 127 - 4 - 5 $$ $$ \boxed{L_{avail}^{(1)} = 118 \text{ bytes}} $$ Continuation fragments carry only a fragment header (typically 5 bytes): $$ L_{avail}^{(c)} = L_{max} - H_{frag} $$ $$ L_{avail}^{(c)} = 127 - 5 $$ $$ \boxed{L_{avail}^{(c)} = 122 \text{ bytes}} $$ **Step 2: Compute number of fragments** Payload to be delivered: $L_{data} = 1280$ bytes (the original packet). $$ N_{fragments} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{L_{data} - L_{avail}^{(1)}}{L_{avail}^{(c)}} \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{fragments} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1280 - 118}{122} \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{fragments} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1162}{122} \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{fragments} = 1 + \left\lceil 9.524 \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{fragments} = 1 + 10 = 11 $$ $$ \boxed{N_{fragments} = 11 \text{ frames}} $$ **Step 3: Compute total protocol overhead** Total bytes transmitted across the air: $$ L_{total} = N_{fragments} \times L_{max} $$ $$ L_{total} = 11 \times 127 = 1397 \text{ bytes} $$ Overhead: $$ O = L_{total} - L_{data} $$ $$ O = 1397 - 1280 $$ $$ \boxed{O = 117 \text{ bytes of overhead}} $$ **Step 4: Header compression ratio** Original IPv6 header is **40 bytes**; compressed to 4 bytes via IPHC (IP Header Compression): $$ \text{CR} = \frac{H_{IPv6}}{H_{6LoWPAN}} = \frac{40}{4} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CR} = 10:1 \text{ compression ratio}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A 14-mark question on Wireless Internet almost always tests this fragmentation calculation. Memorize the values **127 bytes (802.15.4 MTU)** and **40 bytes (IPv6 header)**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Cost of Wireless Internet Communication **Problem:** A sensor node transmits an IPv6 packet of 1280 bytes at 250 kbps. The node operates at 3.0 V, drawing 17.4 mA in TX mode (typical for CC2420 radio). **Step 1: Compute time to transmit** $$ t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1280 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{250 \times 10^3 \text{ bps}} $$ $$ t = \frac{10240}{250000} = 0.04096 \text{ s} \approx 41 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2: Compute energy consumed** $$ E = V \cdot I \cdot t $$ $$ E = 3.0 \times 17.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.04096 $$ $$ E = 0.0522 \times 0.04096 $$ $$ \boxed{E \approx 2.14 \text{ mJ}} $$ **Step 3: With two AA batteries (each 2500 mAh @ 1.5 V), total energy:** $$ E_{total} = 2 \times 2500 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 3600 = 27000 \text{ J} $$ $$ \boxed{N_{packets} = \frac{27000}{2.14 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 12.6 \text{ million packets}} $$ This justifies why **packet size minimization** (via 6LoWPAN) is the most important design goal of Wireless Internet architectures. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Wireless Internet Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef sensor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000 classDef gw fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef cloud fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000 classDef user fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000 SN1["Sensor Node Mote 1
Temp Humidity CO2"]:::sensor SN2["Sensor Node Mote 2
Temp Humidity CO2"]:::sensor SN3["Sensor Node Mote 3
Temp Humidity CO2"]:::sensor SN4["Sensor Node Mote 4
Temp Humidity CO2"]:::sensor SN5["Sensor Node Mote 5
Temp Humidity CO2"]:::sensor GW["6LoWPAN Border Router
Gateway Sink Node
Protocol Translator"]:::gw NET["Internet Backbone
TCP IP IPv6 MPLS"]:::cloud SRV["Cloud Server
MQTT Broker Database"]:::cloud APP["User Application
Web Dashboard Mobile App"]:::user SN1 -->|IEEE 802.15.4 multi-hop| GW SN2 -->|IEEE 802.15.4 multi-hop| GW SN3 -->|IEEE 802.15.4 multi-hop| GW SN4 -->|IEEE 802.15.4 multi-hop| GW SN5 -->|IEEE 802.15.4 multi-hop| GW GW -->|IPv6 TCP UDP HTTPS| NET NET --> SRV SRV --> APP APP -->|Command Actuation| SRV SRV -->|Reverse path| GW GW -->|Downstream 802.15.4| SN1 ``` ### 4.2 Protocol Stack Comparison: TCP/IP vs. Wireless Internet (WSN) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef app fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef trans fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 classDef net fill:#ccffe6,stroke:#00994d,color:#000 classDef adapt fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#004080,color:#000 classDef phys fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#800000,color:#000 subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL INTERNET STACK"] A1["Application Layer
HTTP FTP SMTP"]:::app A2["Transport Layer
TCP UDP"]:::trans A3["Network Layer
IPv4 IPv6"]:::net A4["Data Link Layer
Ethernet PPP"]:::adapt A5["Physical Layer
Copper Fiber Radio"]:::phys A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 end subgraph WSN["WIRELESS INTERNET STACK FOR WSN"] B1["Application Layer
CoAP MQTT HTTP"]:::app B2["Transport Layer
UDP preferred rarely TCP"]:::trans B3["Network Layer
IPv6 with 6LoWPAN compression"]:::net B4["Adaptation Layer
6LoWPAN fragment mesh dispatch"]:::adapt B5["MAC Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 CSMA CA"]:::adapt B6["Physical Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 2.4 GHz 250 kbps"]:::phys B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 end ``` ### 4.3 The 6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000 PKT["Incoming IPv6 Packet
1280 bytes 40 byte header"]:::in CMP["Header Compression
IPHC RFC 6282
40 bytes to 2 to 4 bytes"]:::proc FRG["Fragmentation Engine
Split into 127 byte frames"]:::proc DIS["Dispatch Byte Insertion
Tag frame type"]:::proc RAD["802.15.4 Radio Driver
CSMA CA CCA backoff"]:::proc AIR["Over the Air Transmission
2.4 GHz O QPSK"]:::out PKT --> CMP --> FRG --> DIS --> RAD --> AIR ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The 6LoWPAN layer is the *translator* sitting between the IPv6 world and the IEEE 802.15.4 world. It performs three jobs: **Compression → Fragmentation → Dispatch tagging**. This is the single most-asked diagram in KTU Module 1 viva and written exams. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Wireless Internet in the context of WSN. List any two challenges in connecting sensor nodes to the Internet.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Wireless Internet refers to the architectural integration of resource-constrained wireless sensor networks with the global TCP/IP-based Internet, allowing bi-directional communication between sensor nodes and remote users/clouds via gateway nodes (6LoWPAN Border Routers). **Two Challenges (2 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Packet Size Mismatch:** IPv6 requires a minimum MTU of 1280 bytes, but IEEE 802.15.4 frames can carry only 127 bytes of payload, requiring complex fragmentation and reassembly. 2. **Resource Constraints:** Sensor nodes have limited RAM (typically 4–128 KB), limited battery power, and low bandwidth (250 kbps), making it difficult to implement a full TCP/IP stack. > *Other acceptable challenges: Address length (128-bit IPv6 vs. limited storage), duty cycling (nodes sleep), and lack of unique global IP addresses without IPv6/6LoWPAN.* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is a 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)? Mention its function.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)** is the gateway device that interconnects an IEEE 802.15.4-based 6LoWPAN network with an external IP network (e.g., the Internet). It is the central node in the Wireless Internet architecture. (1 mark) **Functions (2 marks):** 1. **Protocol Translation:** Converts between compressed 6LoWPAN frames (used inside the WSN) and full IPv6 packets (used on the Internet). 2. **Header Compression/Decompression:** Performs IPHC compression when sending toward nodes, and decompression when receiving from nodes. 3. **Fragmentation/Reassembly:** Handles splitting of large IPv6 packets into 802.15.4-sized frames and vice versa. 4. **Address Translation & Routing:** Maintains routing tables for the 6LoWPAN network and acts as the default gateway for sensor motes. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Modelled after KTU Dec 2023 ESE Paper* **Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of Wireless Internet with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the gateway node in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the 6LoWPAN protocol stack. How does it enable IPv6 communication over IEEE 802.15.4 networks? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Architecture (4 marks):** The Wireless Internet architecture consists of three tiers: 1. **Sensing Tier:** Hundreds to thousands of battery-powered sensor motes form a multi-hop mesh. They sense physical parameters (temperature, pressure, motion) and forward data hop-by-hop using protocols like RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) over IEEE 802.15.4. Communication is *data-centric* — nodes do not have unique identities; queries target attributes (e.g., "all nodes where temperature > 40 °C"). 2. **Gateway/Edge Tier:** A 6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR) acts as the bridge. It runs both an IEEE 802.15.4 interface (toward the WSN) and an Ethernet/Wi-Fi interface (toward the Internet). It performs **protocol translation**, **header compression/decompression**, **packet aggregation**, and **security enforcement** (typically AES-128 at the link layer and DTLS at the application layer). 3. **Application/Cloud Tier:** Standard Internet hosts — web servers, MQTT brokers, time-series databases (InfluxDB, MongoDB), and client applications (browser dashboards, mobile apps) — interact with the WSN using CoAP, MQTT, or HTTP. *(See Section 4.1 diagram for illustration — 1 mark for diagram.)* **Role of the Gateway Node (3 marks):** - **Protocol Translator:** Converts 6LoWPAN ↔ IPv6. - **Data Aggregator:** Merges redundant data from multiple nodes to reduce upstream traffic. - **Security Enforcement:** Acts as the trust boundary; runs firewall and authentication. - **Network Manager:** Configures nodes, assigns IPv6 prefixes, runs RPL root. - **Energy Buffer:** Typically mains-powered, so it shields the WSN from expensive operations like TLS handshake establishment. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **6LoWPAN Protocol Stack (3 marks):** 6LoWPAN is an adaptation layer defined by **RFC 4944** and updated by **RFC 6282** that sits between the IPv6 network layer and the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer. Its purpose is to make IPv6 packets fit into 127-byte 802.15.4 frames. The stack is: - **Application:** CoAP, MQTT-SN - **Transport:** UDP (preferred over TCP due to low overhead) - **Network:** IPv6 (with header compression) - **Adaptation:** 6LoWPAN (this is the *new* layer added) - **MAC:** IEEE 802.15.4 - **PHY:** IEEE 802.15.4 (868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz) *(1 mark for neat diagram — reference Section 4.2.)* **How it Enables IPv6 over 802.15.4 (4 marks):** 1. **Header Compression (IPHC — RFC 6282):** Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header down to 2–4 bytes by eliding fields that can be inferred from link-layer addresses (e.g., source/destination IPv6 addresses derived from 16-bit short addresses). **Compression ratio: typically 40:1.** (1 mark) 2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly:** IPv6 packets larger than the 802.15.4 MTU are split into link-local fragments. The first fragment is tagged with `IPHC` dispatch; subsequent fragments use a `FRAG1` (first) or `FRAGN` (subsequent) dispatch byte. A datagram tag and datagram offset are added. (1 mark) 3. **Mesh Addressing (Mesh Delivery Header):** Supports source-routed multi-hop forwarding *within* the 6LoWPAN, while still using IP at the endpoints. This decouples the IP routing from the link-layer routing. (1 mark) 4. **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC):** Each node derives its 128-bit IPv6 address from its 64-bit IEEE EUI-64 address, eliminating the need for a DHCP server. (1 mark) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **Q. (a)** Explain the challenges of integrating WSN with the Internet. Discuss IPv6 and 6LoWPAN as enabling technologies. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A WSN application needs to transmit a 1280-byte IPv6 packet over an IEEE 802.15.4 link with a 6LoWPAN header of 4 bytes and a mesh-header of 5 bytes on the first fragment. The first-fragment header total is 9 bytes, and continuation-fragment headers are 5 bytes. Calculate: - (i) Number of 802.15.4 frames required. - (ii) Total protocol overhead. - (iii) Effective throughput in bytes-per-frame. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Challenges of WSN-Internet Integration (4 marks):** 1. **Resource Constraints:** Mote-class devices have only 4–128 KB RAM and limited CPU; full TCP/IP is too heavy. 2. **Packet Size Mismatch:** IPv6 MTU (1280 B) >> 802.15.4 payload (127 B). 3. **Duty Cycling:** Nodes sleep to save energy; they are not always IP-reachable. 4. **Address Length:** 128-bit IPv6 addresses are storage-heavy. 5. **Data-Centric vs. Address-Centric:** Internet is host-oriented; WSN is data-oriented. 6. **Security:** Resource-constrained nodes cannot run heavy crypto. **IPv6 as an Enabler (1.5 marks):** - Huge address space (2¹²⁸) → assigns a unique global address to every mote. - Stateless autoconfiguration (SLAAC) → no DHCP server needed. - Built-in IPsec (though usually offloaded to gateway in WSNs). **6LoWPAN as an Enabler (1.5 marks):** - Provides header compression (40 B → 2–4 B). - Handles fragmentation/reassembly. - Defines mesh-under routing. - Allows IPv6 to ride on 802.15.4 links. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $L_{IPv6} = 1280 \text{ B}$ - $L_{max} = 127 \text{ B}$ - $H_{first} = 9 \text{ B}$ (6LoWPAN + mesh) - $H_{cont} = 5 \text{ B}$ (fragment header only) **Step 1: Available payload per fragment (2 marks)** $$ L_1 = 127 - 9 = 118 \text{ B (first fragment)} $$ $$ L_c = 127 - 5 = 122 \text{ B (continuation)} $$ **Step 2: Number of fragments (2 marks)** $$ N = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1280 - 118}{122} \right\rceil = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1162}{122} \right\rceil = 1 + 10 = 11 $$ $$ \boxed{N = 11 \text{ frames}} $$ **Step 3: Total overhead (1.5 marks)** $$ L_{total} = 11 \times 127 = 1397 \text{ B} $$ $$ O = 1397 - 1280 = 117 \text{ B} $$ $$ \boxed{O = 117 \text{ bytes}} $$ **Step 4: Effective throughput (1.5 marks)** $$ \eta = \frac{L_{IPv6}}{N \times 127} \times 100\% = \frac{1280}{1397} \times 100\% $$ $$ \boxed{\eta \approx 91.6\%} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the first-fragment vs. continuation-fragment distinction.** Many students use 118 B for all fragments and get the wrong count. KTU evaluators specifically check this — full 1-mark deduction per wrong fragment. > 2. **Confusing 127 bytes with 128 bytes.** The 802.15.4 *maximum* PHY payload is 127 bytes (the 128th byte is reserved for the FCS). Writing 128 is a 0.5-mark error. > 3. **Omitting the unit "bytes"** in final answers — half-mark deduction in KTU answer scripts. > 4. **Writing "IPHC" without expanding** (IP Header Compression) — KTU expects full-form the first time. > 5. **In Part A (3 marks) questions**, students often give a one-line answer. KTU 2024 scheme mandates **Definition + Two/Three distinct points**.
