One-line answers score at most 1.5/3. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wireless Internet** - ✅ **Wireless Internet** = WSN ↔ Internet integration via a **Gateway / 6LBR**. - ✅ **3-Tier Architecture:** Sensing Field → Gateway → Cloud/Application. - ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4** provides PHY + MAC for WSNs (max payload **127 bytes**, **250 kbps at 2.4 GHz**). - ✅ **IPv6 header = 40 bytes**; 6LoWPAN compresses it to **2–4 bytes** using IPHC (RFC 6282). - ✅ **6LoWPAN** = IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks (RFC 4944). - ✅ **Three 6LoWPAN frame types** are distinguished by **dispatch bytes**: - `01 000001` (0x41) → IPHC compressed - `11 000...` → Mesh delivery - `11 100...` → FRAG1 (first fragment) - `11 110...` → FRAGN (subsequent fragment) - ✅ **Fragmentation formula (must memorize):** $N = 1 + \lceil (L - L_1) / L_c \rceil$ where $L_1$ = first-fragment payload, $L_c$ = continuation-fragment payload. - ✅ **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks, RFC 6550) is the standard IP routing protocol for 6LoWPAN — it builds a **DODAG** (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) rooted at the gateway. - ✅ **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252) is the HTTP-equivalent for WSNs — runs over UDP, uses GET/POST/PUT/DELETE like HTTP. - ✅ **MQTT-SN** (MQTT for Sensor Networks) is an alternative pub/sub protocol optimized for sleeping nodes. - ✅ **UDP is preferred over TCP** in WSNs because TCP's handshake/retransmission overhead is unaffordable for battery-powered motes. - ✅ **Energy per bit:** $E = VIt$. Header compression directly translates to **battery-life extension**. - ✅ **Key buzzwords for KTU viva:** 6LoWPAN, 6LBR, IPHC, RPL, DODAG, CoAP, MQTT-SN, IEEE 802.15.4, dispatch byte, mesh-under, route-over. - ✅ **Why fragmentation is the biggest challenge:** IPv6 minimum MTU (1280 B) is **~10× larger** than 802.15.4 maximum payload (127 B).

topics.title: Sensor Network Scenarios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Network Scenarios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Sensor Network Scenarios** refer to the distinct deployment environments and operational contexts in which Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) are physically installed and functionally executed. Each scenario imposes a unique set of physical, environmental, and protocol-level constraints on the sensor nodes, governing their hardware design, communication mechanisms, energy management, and routing strategies.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, sensor network scenarios are broadly classified into five canonical categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Classification — The Five Standard Scenarios**
> 1. **Terrestrial Sensor Networks (T-WSN)**
> 2. **Underground Sensor Networks (U-WSN)**
> 3. **Underwater Sensor Networks (UW-WSN)**
> 4. **Multi-Media Sensor Networks (M-WSN)**
> 5. **Mobile Sensor Networks (MOB-WSN)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Five Post Offices of a Smart City"**
>
> Imagine a smart city has five different types of post offices, each designed for a unique environment:
> - **Land Post Offices** sit on open ground (Terrestrial — fields, forests, battlefields).
> - **Underground Vaults** operate beneath the soil (Underground — pipelines, mines).
> - **Submarine Stations** are submerged deep under the sea (Underwater — oceans, rivers).
> - **Photography Booths** capture rich media (Multi-media — audio, video, imaging).
> - **Drone Hubs** are moving constantly (Mobile — vehicles, drones, animals).
>
> Just like each post office uses different vehicles, ink, and packaging suited to its medium, each sensor scenario demands tailored hardware (radio vs. acoustic vs. optical) and protocols.

### 1.3 Fundamental Operating Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally applicable across all five scenarios:

- **Operating Frequency Band:** ISM 2.4 GHz, 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz
- **Transmission Range:** 1 m – 100 m (typical motes)
- **Data Rate:** 1 kbps – 250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee baseline)
- **Node Lifetime:** Determined by battery capacity in **Joules (J)** or **milliampere-hours (mAh)**
- **Node Density:** Nodes per unit area (typically 10 – 50 nodes/m³ in dense WSN)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scenario-Wise Detailed Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Terrestrial Sensor Networks (T-WSN)

**Definition:** Sensor nodes are deployed in a known terrestrial environment — on the ground, within buildings, forests, or along roads — using **dense, pre-planned or random (drop-from-air) deployment**.

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Communication medium: **Radio Frequency (RF)** — predominantly 2.4 GHz ISM band.
- Energy source: Typically battery (limited), sometimes solar-augmented.
- Node density: Very high; hundreds to thousands of nodes per square kilometer.
- Routing protocols: SPIN, LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion.

**Why this works:** RF propagates efficiently in air with minimal absorption. Multi-hop forwarding overcomes the limited range of cheap motes.

**Applications:**
- Precision agriculture (soil moisture, temperature)
- Forest fire detection
- Military battlefield surveillance
- Industrial process monitoring

---

#### 2.1.2 Underground Sensor Networks (U-WSN)

**Definition:** Sensor nodes are buried **below the soil, inside rocks, or within concrete structures** to monitor subterranean parameters.

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Communication medium: RF through soil — but with **severe attenuation**.
- Additional hardware: Antenna must be routed to the surface.
- Energy source: Non-replaceable batteries → ultra-low-power design mandatory.
- Challenges: High path loss through dense media, moisture absorption, mechanical stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Underground sensors cannot harvest solar energy easily, so energy efficiency is *the* dominant design constraint.

**Applications:**
- Underground pipeline monitoring (oil, gas, water)
- Landslide prediction
- Mine safety systems
- Structural health monitoring of foundations

---

#### 2.1.3 Underwater Sensor Networks (UW-WSN)

**Definition:** Sensor nodes and sink nodes are deployed **beneath the surface of oceans, lakes, or rivers** for aquatic monitoring.

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Communication medium: **Acoustic waves** (since RF is absorbed rapidly in water).
- Speed of acoustic signal: ≈ **1500 m/s** (vs. 3×10⁸ m/s for RF).
- Latency: Very high due to slow propagation.
- Bandwidth: Severely limited (typically < 30 kHz).
- Energy: Acoustic modems consume orders of magnitude more power than RF motes.

**Applications:**
- Tsunami early warning
- Underwater oil field exploration
- Marine biology (fish migration tracking)
- Harbor security and port surveillance

---

#### 2.1.4 Multi-Media Sensor Networks (M-WSN)

**Definition:** Networks equipped with **sensors that capture rich multimedia content** — scalar (temperature) + scalar data, but more importantly **video, audio, image, and scalar streams**.

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Data rate: High (hundreds of kbps to several Mbps per node).
- Bandwidth demand: Order of magnitude greater than scalar WSN.
- Energy cost: Image/video processing consumes ≈ **10× to 100× more energy** than scalar sensing.
- QoS requirements: Delay-sensitive, jitter-sensitive.
- In-network processing: Compression, feature extraction, distributed coding essential.

**Applications:**
- Surveillance and tracking (CCTV-style)
- Traffic monitoring and enforcement
- Environmental multimedia (volcano, animal behavior video)
- Smart-home gesture recognition

---

#### 2.1.5 Mobile Sensor Networks (MOB-WSN)

**Definition:** Sensor nodes are **not stationary** — they move with attached entities (vehicles, animals, drones, humans) or via controlled mobility (robot-mounted).

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Topology: **Dynamic** — links form and break continuously.
- Routing challenge: Must adapt to mobility (handoffs, route repair).
- Energy harvesting: Often possible (vehicle battery, solar on moving entity).
- Coverage: Improved dynamically compared to static deployments.

**Applications:**
- Vehicular ad hoc sensor networks (VANETs)
- Wildlife tracking (ZebraNet, WhaleNet)
- Search-and-rescue robotic swarms
- Drone-based environmental monitoring

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are core to quantitative analysis in WSN scenario questions. Master them for Part A and Part B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL} &= \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \\
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E_{tx}(k,d) \;=\; E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k \;+\; E_{\text{amp}}\cdot k\cdot d^{n}
$$

$$
E_{rx}(k) \;=\; E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k
$$

$$
T_{\text{prop}} \;=\; \frac{d}{v_{\text{medium}}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; N \cdot \bigl(P_{\text{sense}} + P_{\text{proc}} + P_{\text{comm}}\bigr)
$$

#### Compact Formula Summary Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength of carrier signal | metres (m) |
| $d$ | Distance between Tx and Rx | metres (m) |
| $f$ | Carrier frequency | Hz |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| $E_{\text{elec}}$ | Energy per bit for Tx/Rx circuitry | 50 nJ/bit |
| $E_{\text{amp}}$ | Energy per bit for transmit amplifier | 10 pJ/bit/m² |
| $n$ | Path loss exponent | $n=2$ free-space, $n=3$–$4$ multipath |
| $k$ | Packet size in bits | bits |
| $v_{\text{medium}}$ | Signal propagation velocity | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s RF, $\approx 1500$ m/s acoustic |
| $N$ | Number of sensor nodes | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These scenario classifications directly drive:
- **Hardware selection** (e.g., acoustic modem for underwater vs. CC2420 RF transceiver for terrestrial).
- **MAC protocol selection** (e.g., contention-based CSMA for terrestrial vs. scheduled TDMA for underwater due to long propagation delays).
- **Deployment budget** (underwater nodes cost 10–100× more than terrestrial motes).
- **Energy model selection** for simulations (NS-2, NS-3, COOJA, Castalia).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Consumption in a Multi-Hop Terrestrial Sensor Network

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** Consider a linear chain of **N = 5 sensor nodes**, each separated by **d = 10 m**, transmitting a packet of **k = 2000 bits** to a sink located 50 m away. Compute the **total transmission and reception energy** required assuming single-hop direct transmission versus multi-hop forwarding. Use the standard first-order radio model.

**Given:**
- $k = 2000$ bits
- $N = 5$ nodes
- $d = 10$ m per hop, $D_{\text{total}} = 50$ m
- $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $E_{\text{amp}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² $= 100 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²
- Path loss exponent $n = 2$ (free-space terrestrial)

**Step 1 — Energy to transmit k bits over distance d:**

$$
E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k + E_{\text{amp}}\cdot k\cdot d^{2}
$$

Substituting numerical values for one hop:

$$
E_{tx} = (50\times 10^{-9})(2000) + (100\times 10^{-12})(2000)(10)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx} = (1.0\times 10^{-4}) + (2.0\times 10^{-5}) = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy to receive k bits:**

$$
E_{rx}(k) = E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k = (50\times 10^{-9})(2000) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — Direct single-hop transmission (50 m in one jump):**

$$
E_{tx}^{\text{direct}} = E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k + E_{\text{amp}}\cdot k\cdot (50)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx}^{\text{direct}} = 1.0\times 10^{-4} + (100\times 10^{-12})(2000)(2500)
$$

$$
E_{tx}^{\text{direct}} = 1.0\times 10^{-4} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Multi-hop forwarding energy (each node: 1 tx + 1 rx, except sink):**

Per intermediate hop, total cost:

$$
E_{\text{hop}} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} = 1.2\times 10^{-4} + 1.0\times 10^{-4} = 2.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

For 4 forwarding hops (since the source node only transmits and the final sink only receives):

$$
E_{\text{multi-hop}} = 4 \times (2.2 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

Adding source tx and final sink rx (these are already included in the 4 hops above — let me re-clarify):

- Source node: 1 transmit only → $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- 3 intermediate nodes: each does 1 rx + 1 tx → $3 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-4} = 6.6 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- Sink node: 1 receive only → $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J

$$
E_{\text{multi-hop}}^{\text{total}} = 1.2\times 10^{-4} + 6.6 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0\times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{\text{multi-hop}}^{\text{total}} = 8.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and conclude:**

$$
E_{\text{direct}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{multi-hop}} = 8.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive insight:** Even though multi-hop reduces per-hop transmission energy (because $d^{2}$ shrinks), the **receive energy overhead** at each intermediate node makes direct transmission *more efficient for short chains*. Multi-hop dominates only when $d_{\text{hop}}$ is much smaller than the direct distance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Underwater Acoustic Propagation Delay

**Problem Setup:** Two underwater sensor nodes are separated by **d = 3 km**. Compute the propagation delay if the signal travels via **acoustic waves**.

**Step 1 — Identify propagation velocity:**

$$
v_{\text{acoustic}} \approx 1500 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute propagation delay:**

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{acoustic}}} = \frac{3000 \text{ m}}{1500 \text{ m/s}} = 2 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with RF in air (terrestrial):**

$$
T_{\text{prop}}^{\text{RF}} = \frac{3000}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 10 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of delays:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{prop}}^{\text{acoustic}}}{T_{\text{prop}}^{\text{RF}}} = \frac{2}{10^{-5}} = 2 \times 10^{5}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Acoustic propagation is **200,000× slower** than RF. This is why underwater MAC protocols (e.g., UW-MAC, T-Lohi) use long scheduling windows and TDMA-based reservations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Five Scenarios

| Parameter | Terrestrial | Underground | Underwater | Multi-Media | Mobile |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Medium | Air | Soil / Rock | Water | Air / Indoor | Air |
| Signal type | RF | RF (low freq) | Acoustic | RF + Optical | RF |
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 433 MHz | 10–30 kHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz |
| Range | 10–100 m | 1–10 m | 1–10 km | 10–50 m | Variable |
| Data rate | 250 kbps | 10 kbps | < 30 kbps | > 1 Mbps | 250 kbps |
| Latency | Low | Medium | Very High | Low (jitter) | Variable |
| Topology | Static | Static | Semi-static | Static | Dynamic |
| Energy source | Battery / Solar | Battery only | Battery (rarely solar) | Mains / Battery | Vehicle / Solar |
| Cost per node | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| Key protocol | LEACH, SPIN | Directed Diffusion | UW-MAC, T-Lohi | QoS-aware routing | GEAR, SAR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Architecture Diagram — Sensor Network Scenarios

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef terrestrial fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef underground fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef underwater fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef multimedia fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef mobile fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    baseNode[Wireless Sensor Network]:::base
    tNode[Terrestrial WSN - Air RF 2.4 GHz]:::terrestrial
    uNode[Underground WSN - Soil RF Low Freq]:::underground
    uwNode[Underwater WSN - Acoustic 10 30 kHz]:::underwater
    mNode[Multimedia WSN - RF plus Video Audio]:::multimedia
    mobNode[Mobile WSN - RF with Mobility]:::mobile

    baseNode --> tNode
    baseNode --> uNode
    baseNode --> uwNode
    baseNode --> mNode
    baseNode --> mobNode

    tApp[Agriculture and Forest Fire and Battlefield]:::terrestrial
    uApp[Pipeline and Mine Safety and Landslide]:::underground
    uwApp[Tsunami Warning and Marine Biology]:::underwater
    mApp[Surveillance and Traffic and Smart Home]:::multimedia
    mobApp[VANET and Wildlife Tracking and Drones]:::mobile

    tNode --> tApp
    uNode --> uApp
    uwNode --> uwApp
    mNode --> mApp
    mobNode --> mobApp

    tNode --> sinkT[Base Station Sink]:::base
    uNode --> sinkU[Surface Gateway]:::base
    uwNode --> sinkUW[Surface Buoy Sink]:::base
    mNode --> sinkM[QoS Aware Sink]:::base
    mobNode --> sinkMob[Mobile Sink or Drone]:::base

    sinkT --> internet((Internet Cloud)):::base
    sinkU --> internet
    sinkUW --> internet
    sinkM --> internet
    sinkMob --> internet
```

### 4.2 Energy Model Flow — Multi-Hop Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef energy fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    pktA[Packet k bits]:::data
    srcNode[Source Node S1]:::node
    relayNode2[Relay Node R1]:::node
    relayNode3[Relay Node R2]:::node
    sinkNode[Final Sink BS]:::node

    etx1[Etx Eelec k plus Eamp k d squared]:::energy
    erx1[Erx Eelec k]:::energy
    etx2[Etx Eelec k plus Eamp k d squared]:::energy
    erx2[Erx Eelec k]:::energy
    erx3[Erx Eelec k]:::energy

    pktA --> srcNode
    srcNode --> etx1
    etx1 -.Radio Channel.-> relayNode2
    relayNode2 --> erx1
    relayNode2 --> etx2
    etx2 -.Radio Channel.-> relayNode3
    relayNode3 --> erx2
    relayNode3 --> etx3[Etx Eelec k plus Eamp k d squared]
    etx3 -.Radio Channel.-> sinkNode
    sinkNode --> erx3
```

### 4.3 Scenario Selection Decision Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef dec fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000

    start[Deploy Sensors]:::dec
    q1{Deployment Medium?}:::q

    start --> q1

    q1 -->|Above Ground Static| tChoice[Choose TERRESTRIAL]:::out
    q1 -->|Below Soil| uChoice[Choose UNDERGROUND]:::out
    q1 -->|In Water| uwChoice[Choose UNDERWATER]:::out
    q1 -->|Captures Video or Audio| mChoice[Choose MULTIMEDIA]:::out
    q1 -->|Nodes Move| mobChoice[Choose MOBILE]:::out

    tChoice --> tSig[Use RF at 2.4 GHz]:::out
    uChoice --> uSig[Use Low Frequency RF plus Buried Antenna]:::out
    uwChoice --> uwSig[Use ACOUSTIC Modems]:::out
    mChoice --> mSig[Use High Bandwidth plus QoS MAC]:::out
    mobChoice --> mobSig[Use Mobility Aware Routing]:::out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Terrestrial and Underwater Wireless Sensor Networks in terms of communication medium, propagation speed, and bandwidth.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Terrestrial WSN | Underwater WSN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Communication medium | Radio Frequency (RF) | Acoustic waves |
| Propagation speed | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | $\approx 1500$ m/s |
| Typical bandwidth | 250 kbps (ZigBee) | $< 30$ kbps |
| Latency | Low (microseconds) | Very high (seconds) |
| Node mobility | Mostly static | Drifted by currents |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write *“RF is used underwater”*. This is **wrong** — RF is absorbed within a few centimetres in water. Acoustic signalling is mandatory.

**(3 marks awarded for a clear, correct table with at least 4 distinguishing rows.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three challenges unique to Underground Wireless Sensor Networks.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Severe RF attenuation** through soil and rock — high path loss restricts communication range to 1–10 m.
2. **Non-replaceable energy source** — buried nodes cannot easily harvest solar energy; battery replacement is impractical.
3. **Antenna placement complexity** — antennas must be routed to the surface, increasing hardware cost and risk of mechanical failure.
4. **Moisture-induced corrosion** and soil pressure on sensor packages.
5. **Difficult maintenance** — once buried, debugging or replacement is costly.

**(3 marks for any 3 correctly explained challenges.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **five major sensor network scenarios** with suitable real-world applications for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear WSN has 4 nodes equally spaced 20 m apart, transmitting 4000-bit packets to a sink using multi-hop forwarding. Using the first-order radio model ($E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{\text{amp}} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $n=2$), compute the **total energy consumed**. Compare with **direct single-hop transmission**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Scenarios:**

1. **Terrestrial WSN** — Deployed on land for agriculture, battlefield surveillance, forest fire detection. Uses RF in ISM bands.

2. **Underground WSN** — Buried for pipeline monitoring, mine safety, landslide detection. Uses low-frequency RF with surface antennas.

3. **Underwater WSN** — Submerged in oceans/rivers for tsunami warning, marine biology, port security. Uses acoustic modems due to high RF absorption in water.

4. **Multi-Media WSN** — Equipped with cameras/microphones for surveillance, traffic monitoring, smart-home gesture recognition. Requires high bandwidth and QoS-aware routing.

5. **Mobile WSN** — Nodes move with vehicles, drones, or animals. Used in VANETs, wildlife tracking (e.g., ZebraNet), drone-based environmental monitoring.

*(7 marks — 1.4 marks per scenario with correct application.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $k = 4000$ bits, hop distance $d = 20$ m, $N = 4$ nodes, $D_{\text{direct}} = 60$ m.

**Per-hop transmission energy:**

$$
E_{tx} = (50\times 10^{-9})(4000) + (100\times 10^{-12})(4000)(20)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx} = 2.0\times 10^{-4} + 1.6\times 10^{-4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Per-hop reception energy:**

$$
E_{rx} = (50\times 10^{-9})(4000) = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Direct transmission energy (60 m in one hop):**

$$
E_{tx}^{\text{direct}} = (50\times 10^{-9})(4000) + (100\times 10^{-12})(4000)(60)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx}^{\text{direct}} = 2.0\times 10^{-4} + 1.44\times 10^{-3} = 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

**Multi-hop total energy:**

- Source node: 1 tx → $3.6 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- 2 intermediate nodes: each does 1 tx + 1 rx → $2 \times (3.6\times 10^{-4} + 2.0\times 10^{-4}) = 1.12 \times 10^{-3}$ J
- Sink node: 1 rx → $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J

$$
E_{\text{multi-hop}}^{\text{total}} = 3.6\times 10^{-4} + 1.12 \times 10^{-3} + 2.0\times 10^{-4} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
E_{\text{direct}} = 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \quad \text{vs.} \quad E_{\text{multi-hop}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

> Direct transmission is marginally more efficient here because the **receive-energy overhead** of multi-hop is not yet offset by reduced transmit amplifier cost. Multi-hop would dominate if the chain were longer (> 6 nodes).

*(7 marks — distributed across correct substitution, calculation, and comparative inference.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **challenges of deploying sensor nodes in underwater environments**. Compare **RF and acoustic** signalling for such scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An underwater WSN has two nodes separated by 5 km. Compute the **propagation delay** using acoustic waves. If the same nodes were in a terrestrial environment using RF, what would be the delay? Comment on the implications for MAC protocol design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Underwater Deployment Challenges:**

1. **High propagation delay** — acoustic signals travel at ~1500 m/s, causing latencies of seconds for km-scale distances.
2. **Limited bandwidth** — acoustic channels offer only tens of kHz.
3. **Node mobility due to water currents** — topology becomes dynamic.
4. **Energy constraints** — acoustic modems consume 10–100× more power than RF motes.
5. **Doppler effect** — relative motion between nodes introduces frequency shifts.
6. **Expensive deployment** — underwater hardware must resist corrosion and pressure.

**RF vs Acoustic Comparison:**

| Feature | RF in Water | Acoustic in Water |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Attenuation | Severe (dB/m) | Low (dB/km) |
| Range | < 10 cm usable | Several km |
| Speed | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | 1500 m/s |
| Practical use | Not feasible | Standard |

*(7 marks — challenge list 4 marks + RF/acoustic table 3 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Acoustic delay:**

$$
T_{\text{acoustic}} = \frac{5000 \text{ m}}{1500 \text{ m/s}} = 3.33 \text{ seconds}
$$

**RF delay (terrestrial):**

$$
T_{\text{RF}} = \frac{5000}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 16.67 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{acoustic}}}{T_{\text{RF}}} \approx 2 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Implication for MAC design:**
Because the propagation delay is enormous, **CSMA-based contention** (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4) becomes inefficient — collisions waste channel time across km-scale round-trip delays. Instead, **TDMA-based scheduling with long reservation windows** (e.g., UW-MAC, T-Lohi) is preferred. Handshakes must be designed with awareness of the 3-second one-way latency.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for computation + 4 marks for protocol implication discussion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> - Writing *“RF is used in underwater WSN”* → full 0 for that sub-part. RF is unusable in water; acoustic is mandatory.
> - Forgetting to convert **nJ and pJ to Joules** before summing — examiners deduct 1 mark per unit mismatch.
> - Confusing **propagation delay** with **transmission delay**. Propagation delay uses distance/velocity; transmission delay uses packet-size/bandwidth.
> - In multi-hop energy problems, students often **double-count** the source's transmit energy or forget the sink's reception. Always list *each node's role explicitly*.
> - Do **not** write vague answers like “underground WSN is hard” — always cite **specific physical reasons** (attenuation, antenna, energy, moisture).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Five canonical scenarios:** Terrestrial, Underground, Underwater, Multi-Media, Mobile.
- **Underwater WSN uses acoustic waves**, NOT RF, because RF attenuates within centimetres in water.
- **Acoustic propagation speed ≈ 1500 m/s**, giving propagation delays of seconds over km distances.
- **Underground WSN** suffers from severe RF attenuation in soil; antenna must reach the surface; energy is non-replenishable.
- **Multi-Media WSN** demands high bandwidth (> 1 Mbps), QoS guarantees, and in-network compression.
- **Mobile WSN** has dynamic topology → mobility-aware routing (GEAR, SAR) is essential.
- **First-order radio energy model:** $E_{tx} = E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k + E_{\text{amp}}\cdot k\cdot d^{n}$, $E_{rx} = E_{\text{elec}}\cdot k$.
- **Free-space path loss:** $\text{FSPL} = (4\pi d / \lambda)^{2}$; depends on $d^{2}$ in air.
- **Multi-hop is not always better** — receive overhead can exceed saved transmit energy for short chains.
- **Terrestrial WSN is the default scenario** in KTU numericals unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Underwater MAC protocols** use TDMA/reservation due to long propagation delays; CSMA is inefficient.
- **Standard exam units:** Always express energy in **Joules (J)**, not nJ or pJ, in the final answer.
- **Cost hierarchy (per node):** Underwater > Underground > Multimedia ≈ Mobile > Terrestrial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Goals and Figures of Merit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimization Goals and Figures of Merit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, **Optimization Goals** are the formal, mathematically defined objectives that a network designer seeks to maximize or minimize when architecting protocols, hardware, and deployment strategies. The **Figures of Merit (FoM)** are the quantitative metrics (or KPIs — Key Performance Indicators) used to numerically evaluate how well a given design satisfies these optimization goals.

Together, they form a **design evaluation framework** that translates qualitative engineering aspirations (e.g., "make the network last long") into computable, comparable, and optimizable quantities (e.g., "time until the first node dies, $T_1$").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEECT861 — Module 2):**
> Optimization goals in WSNs represent the multi-objective design criteria (energy, lifetime, coverage, latency, throughput, security) that govern protocol and architecture design. Figures of merit are the measurable scalar/vector quantities that quantify the degree of achievement of these goals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Village"

Imagine you are the **chief engineer designing a solar-powered smart village** with **1000 self-powered streetlamps** scattered across a hilly terrain.

- Your **Optimization Goals** (what you *want*): the lamps should glow *as long as possible* (lifetime), they should light *every corner* of the village (coverage), and the lamps should *communicate* with each other to report failures (connectivity).
- Your **Figures of Merit** (how you *measure* success): the *number of days* before 50% lamps die ($T_{50}$), the *percentage of area* illuminated ($\mathcal{C}$), the *average energy* per lamp per day ($E_{avg}$).

In this analogy:
- A goal is the **"destination"** on a map.
- A figure of merit is the **"speedometer"** that tells you whether you are moving toward or away from the destination.

Every design decision — which protocol to use, where to place gateways, how often to sample — is a *lever* that moves the speedometer. Optimization in WSNs is essentially the art of **finding the best combination of levers** so all speedometers read desirable values simultaneously.

### 1.3 Why Both "Goals" and "Figures of Merit" Coexist

A goal can be **ambiguous** (e.g., "long network lifetime" — long compared to what?). A figure of merit **removes ambiguity** by anchoring the goal to a precise number. The relationship is:

$$
\text{Goal} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Formal Definition} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Figure of Merit (FoM)} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Numerical Evaluation}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to Remember (commonly confused in exams):**
> - **Goal** = *qualitative* aspiration (e.g., "minimize energy").
> - **Figure of Merit** = *quantitative* measurement (e.g., "Joules per bit", "network half-life").
> - **Optimization** = the *process* of finding the design that best satisfies the goals, using the figures of merit as the yardstick.

### 1.4 The Eight Canonical WSN Figures of Merit

The KTU 2024 syllabus, consistent with the seminal works of **Akyildiz et al.** and the **IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee evaluation literature**, lists the following canonical figures of merit:

| # | Figure of Merit | Symbol | Goal Direction |
|---|-----------------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Energy per bit | $E_b$ | Minimize |
| 2 | Network lifetime | $T_{net}$ | Maximize |
| 3 | Sensing coverage | $\mathcal{C}$ | Maximize |
| 4 | Network connectivity | $\kappa$ | Maximize |
| 5 | End-to-end latency | $\tau$ | Minimize |
| 6 | Throughput / Goodput | $\eta$ | Maximize |
| 7 | Fault tolerance | $\mathcal{F}$ | Maximize |
| 8 | Security strength | $\mathcal{S}$ | Maximize |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "list the figures of merit", always present them in this canonical order with units. Examiners award full marks for a complete, ordered list with units and optimization direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Figure of Merit

#### 2.1.1 Energy per Bit ($E_b$)

**Definition:** Average energy consumed by a node to successfully transmit, receive, or process one bit of information.

**Operational Logic:**

- Energy in a WSN is spent across three subsystems: **sensing** ($E_{sen}$), **communication** ($E_{com}$), and **computation** ($E_{cmp}$).
- Communication dominates, often accounting for **>80%** of total energy.

**Energy Model (First-Order Radio Model, Heinzelman et al.):**

To transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

To receive $k$ bits:

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Why $d^n$?** Free-space path loss exponent $n = 2$ for short ranges; multipath fading $n = 4$ for longer ranges with obstacles.

#### 2.1.2 Network Lifetime ($T_{net}$)

**Definition:** Time elapsed until the network can no longer perform its designated function.

**Three Common Definitions:**

$$
T_{net} = T_{1} \quad \text{(time until first node dies — most stringent)}
$$

$$
T_{net} = T_{k} \quad \text{(time until } k \text{% nodes die)}
$$

$$
T_{net} = T_{N} \quad \text{(time until network is partitioned — most lenient)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trap:** Students often write "$T_{net}$ = battery life of one node". This is **wrong**. $T_{net}$ is a *system-level* metric, not a node-level metric.

#### 2.1.3 Sensing Coverage ($\mathcal{C}$)

**Definition:** Fraction of the region of interest (RoI) that is monitored by at least one (or $k$) active sensor.

$$
\mathcal{C} = \frac{\text{Area monitored by sensors}}{\text{Total area of RoI}}
$$

For $k$-coverage (every point is covered by at least $k$ sensors):

$$
\mathcal{C}_k = \Pr[\text{point } p \text{ is covered by } \geq k \text{ sensors}]
$$

#### 2.1.4 Network Connectivity ($\kappa$)

**Definition:** Ability of every node to reach the sink via one or more multi-hop paths.

A necessary condition for full connectivity:

$$
R_c \geq 2 \cdot R_s
$$

where $R_c$ = communication radius, $R_s$ = sensing radius. This is the **critical condition** ensuring coverage implies connectivity.

#### 2.1.5 Latency ($\tau$)

**Definition:** Time delay between event detection at source and reception at sink.

$$
\tau = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop} + \tau_{rx}
$$

#### 2.1.6 Throughput ($\eta$)

**Definition:** Successful bits delivered per unit time across the network.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Number of useful bits received at sink}}{\text{Total observation time}}
$$

#### 2.1.7 Fault Tolerance ($\mathcal{F}$)

**Definition:** Network's ability to maintain functionality despite node/link failures. Often modeled as:

$$
\mathcal{F} = 1 - P_{fail} = 1 - (1-p)^{k}
$$

where $p$ = node failure probability, $k$ = redundancy degree.

#### 2.1.8 Security Strength ($\mathcal{S}$)

**Definition:** Resistance to attacks, often quantified by:

$$
\mathcal{S} = -\log_2(P_{compromise})
$$

where $P_{compromise}$ = probability that an adversary breaks the cryptographic key.

### 2.2 The Multi-Objective Optimization Problem

A real WSN must simultaneously satisfy **multiple goals**, which are often **conflicting**. The canonical formulation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \quad & \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1(\mathbf{x}), \; f_2(\mathbf{x}), \; \ldots, \; f_m(\mathbf{x})]^T \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, J \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, K
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is the vector of design variables (transmission power, sleep duty cycle, routing weights, etc.) and $f_i(\mathbf{x})$ are the individual figures of merit to be optimized.

### 2.3 Pareto Optimality

Because no single design is "best" for all goals, we seek **Pareto-optimal** designs:

A design $\mathbf{x}^*$ is **Pareto-optimal** if there is no other feasible $\mathbf{x}$ that improves at least one $f_i$ without worsening another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The set of all Pareto-optimal designs forms the **Pareto Front** in the objective space. A designer selects a single point on the Pareto Front based on application priorities — this is the essence of WSN design trade-offs.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Figure of Merit | Core Formula | Units | Optimization Direction | Critical Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy/bit | $E_b = E_{tot} / N_{bits}$ | **J/bit** | Minimize | $E_b \leq E_{budget} / N_{bits}$ |
| 2 | Network lifetime | $T_{net} = E_{init} / P_{avg}$ | **seconds / days** | Maximize | $T_{net} \geq T_{mission}$ |
| 3 | Coverage | $\mathcal{C} = A_{cov} / A_{tot}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Maximize | $\mathcal{C} \geq 0.95$ typical |
| 4 | Connectivity | $\kappa = (R_c \cdot \pi) \cdot \rho$ | dimensionless | Maximize | $R_c \geq 2 R_s$ for cov $\Rightarrow$ conn |
| 5 | Latency | $\tau = \sum_{i} (t_{proc,i} + t_{tx,i})$ | **seconds (s)** | Minimize | $\tau \leq \tau_{max,app}$ |
| 6 | Throughput | $\eta = N_{good} / T_{obs}$ | **bps** | Maximize | $\eta \geq \eta_{min,app}$ |
| 7 | Fault tolerance | $\mathcal{F} = 1 - (1-p)^k$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Maximize | $\mathcal{F} \geq 0.99$ for critical apps |
| 8 | Security | $\mathcal{S} = -\log_2 P_{comp}$ | **bits of security** | Maximize | $\mathcal{S} \geq 128$ for AES-128 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **IoT smart agriculture**, the deployment of soil-moisture sensors must satisfy:
- **High coverage** (no blind spots in the field),
- **Long lifetime** (battery replacement is expensive),
- **Low latency** (irrigation decisions are time-sensitive).

The **optimization goal** is a *weighted combination* of these figures of merit, and the **figure of merit framework** allows quantitative comparison of competing protocols (LEACH, PEGASIS, TEEN, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standards Affected:** IEEE 802.15.4e (TSCH), 6LoWPAN, RPL routing, CoAP — all use figures of merit defined here as evaluation criteria in their conformance test suites.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy per Bit in a Multi-Hop Route

**Scenario:** A packet of $k$ bits travels from source node $S$ to sink $D$ over $h$ hops. Each intermediate node receives and retransmits the packet.

**Step 1 — Total transmission energy across $h$ hops:**

$$
E_{Tx,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_i^{n} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Total reception energy at $h-1$ intermediate nodes** (sink does not retransmit):

$$
E_{Rx,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{h-1} E_{elec} \cdot k = (h-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 3 — Total communication energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{com} &= E_{Tx,total} + E_{Rx,total} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_i^{n} \right) + (h-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by assuming equal hop distance $d_i = d_{hop} = d_{total} / h$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{com} &= h \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{hop}^{n} + (h-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k \\
&= (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \left( \frac{d_{total}}{h} \right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy per bit:**

$$
E_b = \frac{E_{com}}{k} = (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \left( \frac{d_{total}}{h} \right)^{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Optimal number of hops (differentiate and set to zero, $n=2$):**

$$
\frac{dE_b}{dh} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{total}^{2} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{h^{2}} - \frac{2}{h^{3}} \right) = 0
$$

Solving for $h$ yields the **optimal hop count** that minimizes $E_b$ — a classic KTU derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** This derivation is **frequently asked in Part B (14 marks)**. Memorize the first-order radio model parameters $E_{elec}$ and $\epsilon_{amp}$ and the path-loss exponent values $n=2$ vs $n=4$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Network Lifetime vs. Reporting Rate

**Setup:** A node has initial energy $E_0$ (Joules), average power consumption $P$ (Watts), and reports at rate $r$ (packets/second).

**Step 1 — Energy per packet** (transmit + idle + sleep fractions):

$$
E_{pkt} = \frac{P_{Tx} \cdot t_{Tx} + P_{idle} \cdot t_{idle} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{1}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifetime (in packets):**

$$
N_{pkt} = \frac{E_0}{E_{pkt}}
$$

**Step 3 — Lifetime (in seconds):**

$$
T_{net} = \frac{N_{pkt}}{r} = \frac{E_0}{r \cdot E_{pkt}}
$$

**Step 4 — Key trade-off:**

$$
T_{net} \propto \frac{1}{r}
$$

Doubling the reporting rate **halves the lifetime** — this is the **energy-latency trade-off** in WSNs.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coverage Probability for Random Poisson Deployment

**Setup:** $N$ sensor nodes are deployed uniformly at random over area $A$ with sensing radius $R_s$.

**Step 1 — Node density:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{N}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that a point $p$ is covered by at least one node:**

For Poisson point process, the probability of zero nodes within distance $R_s$ is:

$$
P_{zero} = e^{-\lambda \pi R_s^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Coverage probability (1-coverage):**

$$
\mathcal{C} = 1 - P_{zero} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi R_s^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — $k$-coverage probability:**

$$
\mathcal{C}_k = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{(\lambda \pi R_s^{2})^{i} \cdot e^{-\lambda \pi R_s^{2}}}{i!}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda$ given target coverage $\mathcal{C}^*$:**

$$
\lambda = -\frac{\ln(1 - \mathcal{C}^*)}{\pi R_s^{2}}
$$

This gives the **required node density** for a given coverage target — a typical design calculation.

### 3.4 Analytical Trade-off Matrix

| Trade-off Pair | Why They Conflict | Resolution Strategy |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Lifetime vs. Latency | Higher reporting rate $\Rightarrow$ shorter life, lower latency | Adaptive duty cycling |
| Coverage vs. Cost | More nodes $\Rightarrow$ higher coverage, higher cost | Optimal density (Poisson analysis) |
| Energy vs. Security | Encryption $\Rightarrow$ more compute, more energy | Lightweight crypto (e.g., SPINS) |
| Throughput vs. Reliability | ARQ retransmissions boost reliability, reduce throughput | FEC + hybrid ARQ |
| Connectivity vs. Power | Higher TX power $\Rightarrow$ better connectivity, shorter life | Multi-hop + power control |

### 3.5 Weighted Aggregate Objective Function

For practical optimization, the multiple goals are combined into a single scalar:

$$
F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} w_i \cdot f_i(\mathbf{x})
$$

with constraints $\sum w_i = 1$, $w_i \geq 0$. The weights reflect application priorities:

- **Environmental monitoring:** $w_{lifetime} = 0.5$, $w_{coverage} = 0.3$, $w_{latency} = 0.2$
- **Battlefield surveillance:** $w_{latency} = 0.4$, $w_{security} = 0.3$, $w_{lifetime} = 0.3$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — WSN Optimization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Requirements] --> B[Optimization Goals]
    B --> G1[Energy Efficiency]
    B --> G2[Network Lifetime]
    B --> G3[Sensing Coverage]
    B --> G4[Connectivity]
    B --> G5[Latency]
    B --> G6[Throughput]
    B --> G7[Fault Tolerance]
    B --> G8[Security Strength]
    G1 --> M1[Energy per Bit]
    G2 --> M2[Time to First Node Death]
    G3 --> M3[Area Coverage Ratio]
    G4 --> M4[Connectivity Index]
    G5 --> M5[End to End Delay]
    G6 --> M6[Bits per Second]
    G7 --> M7[Survival Probability]
    G8 --> M8[Cryptographic Strength]
    M1 --> P[Pareto Optimal Design]
    M2 --> P
    M3 --> P
    M4 --> P
    M5 --> P
    M6 --> P
    M7 --> P
    M8 --> P
    P --> OUT[Deployed WSN System]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trade-off Resolution via Pareto Front

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Conflicting Goals]
        D1[Maximize Lifetime]
        D2[Minimize Latency]
    end
    D1 <--> X[Trade off Coupling]
    D2 <--> X
    X --> PF[Pareto Front]
    PF --> Q1[Design A: Long Life High Latency]
    PF --> Q2[Design B: Balanced]
    PF --> Q3[Design C: Low Latency Short Life]
    Q1 --> SEL[Designer Selects based on App]
    Q2 --> SEL
    Q3 --> SEL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Figure of Merit Evaluator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENS[Sensor Layer] --> ADC[ADC and Sampling]
    ADC --> PROC[Processing and Storage]
    PROC --> RADIO[Radio Subsystem]
    RADIO --> NET[Network Layer Routing]
    NET --> ENM[Energy Meter]
    ENM --> FOM1[Compute Energy per Bit]
    PROC --> LTM[Lifetime Tracker]
    LTM --> FOM2[Compute Network Lifetime]
    SENS --> COV[Coverage Mapper]
    COV --> FOM3[Compute Coverage Ratio]
    RADIO --> CONN[Connectivity Graph Builder]
    CONV[Connectivity Graph] --> FOM4[Compute Connectivity Index]
    NET --> LAT[Latency Timer]
    LAT --> FOM5[Compute End to End Delay]
    FOM1 --> AGG[Aggregator and Weighted Sum]
    FOM2 --> AGG
    FOM3 --> AGG
    FOM4 --> AGG
    FOM5 --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT[Final Figure of Merit Score]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Model Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Node Power Supply] --> ES[E_sense]
    PWR --> EC[E_compute]
    PWR --> ECO[E_communicate]
    ECO --> ETX[E_transmit]
    ECO --> ERX[E_receive]
    ECO --> EID[E_idle]
    ECO --> ESL[E_sleep]
    ETX --> EB[E_per_bit]
    ERX --> EB
    EID --> EB
    ESL --> EB
    ES --> EB
    EC --> EB
    EB --> FB[Final Figure of Merit]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define "Figure of Merit" in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. List any four figures of merit with their units. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Figure of Merit (FoM)** is a *quantitative, measurable metric* used to evaluate the degree to which a WSN design satisfies its optimization goals.

Four figures of merit with units:

| # | Figure of Merit | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy per bit ($E_b$) | **Joules per bit (J/bit)** |
| 2 | Network lifetime ($T_{net}$) | **seconds (s)** or **days** |
| 3 | Sensing coverage ($\mathcal{C}$) | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** |
| 4 | End-to-end latency ($\tau$) | **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)** |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four FoM with units — full marks.)*

---

#### Question 2: What is a "Pareto-optimal" design in WSN optimization? Why is it important? `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A design is **Pareto-optimal** if no other feasible design can improve at least one figure of merit without worsening another. The set of all such designs forms the **Pareto Front** in the multi-dimensional objective space.

**Importance (2 marks):**

- WSNs have **conflicting goals** (e.g., lifetime vs. latency) — no single design is universally best.
- The Pareto Front presents all **non-dominated trade-off solutions**, allowing the designer to **select based on application priorities**.
- It converts a multi-objective problem into a **structured decision-making process**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** Optimization Goals and Figures of Merit in WSNs

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the WSN optimization framework. List and define all eight canonical figures of merit used to evaluate WSN performance. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

The WSN optimization framework is depicted in the Mermaid block-level diagram in Section 4.3 above. It comprises four stages:

1. **Sensing** — physical-to-digital conversion of phenomena.
2. **Processing** — local computation, aggregation, storage.
3. **Communication** — wireless transmission and reception.
4. **Evaluation** — measurement of figures of merit using energy meters, latency timers, coverage mappers, and connectivity analyzers.

**The eight canonical figures of merit (definitions, in 8 sub-bullets):**

1. **Energy per bit ($E_b$):** Joules consumed per successfully transmitted bit.
2. **Network lifetime ($T_{net}$):** Time until the network cannot perform its designated function (often defined as time to first node death).
3. **Sensing coverage ($\mathcal{C}$):** Fraction of the region of interest monitored by active sensors.
4. **Network connectivity ($\kappa$):** Measure of how many nodes can reach the sink via multi-hop paths.
5. **End-to-end latency ($\tau$):** Time delay between event detection and sink reception.
6. **Throughput ($\eta$):** Useful bits delivered per unit time at the sink.
7. **Fault tolerance ($\mathcal{F}$):** Probability that the network continues operating despite $k$ node failures.
8. **Security strength ($\mathcal{S}$):** Computational intractability of breaking the cryptographic key.

*(Block diagram — 2 marks, eight FoM with definitions — 5 marks = 7 marks total.)*

---

**(b)** Derive the energy per bit ($E_b$) for an $h$-hop multi-hop route using the first-order radio model. Show that for $n=2$, the optimal hop count minimizes $E_b$. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**First-Order Radio Model:**

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 1 — Transmission energy over $h$ equal hops of distance $d_{hop}$:**

$$
E_{Tx,total} = h \cdot \left( E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{hop}^{n} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Reception energy at $h-1$ intermediate relays:**

$$
E_{Rx,total} = (h-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 3 — Total communication energy:**

$$
E_{com} = h \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{hop}^{n} + (h-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

$$
E_{com} = (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{hop}^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per bit:**

$$
E_b = \frac{E_{com}}{k} = (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{hop}^{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $d_{hop} = d_{total} / h$ and $n=2$:**

$$
E_b(h) = (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot \left( \frac{d_{total}}{h} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
E_b(h) = (2h - 1) \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{\epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{total}^{2}}{h}
$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to $h$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{dE_b}{dh} = 2 \cdot E_{elec} - \frac{\epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{total}^{2}}{h^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for optimal $h$:**

$$
h^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{total}^{2}}{2 \cdot E_{elec}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Second derivative check** (concavity confirms minimum):

$$
\frac{d^{2}E_b}{dh^{2}} = \frac{2 \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d_{total}^{2}}{h^{3}} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{minimum}
$$

*(5 marks for derivation, 1 mark for optimal $h^*$, 1 mark for second derivative verification = 7 marks total.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** Trade-offs and Pareto Optimization in WSNs

**(a)** Explain the conflict between network lifetime and end-to-end latency in WSNs. Derive the relationship $T_{net} \propto 1/r$ where $r$ is the reporting rate. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Conflict (3 marks):**

- Higher reporting rate $r$ ⇒ more frequent transmissions ⇒ more energy consumption per unit time.
- Lower reporting rate $r$ ⇒ less energy consumption ⇒ longer lifetime, but **higher latency** because events take longer to be reported.
- This is the **fundamental lifetime-latency trade-off** in WSNs.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

A node has initial energy $E_0$ and consumes average power $P_{avg}$.

**Step 1 — Total operating time (lifetime in seconds):**

$$
T_{net} = \frac{E_0}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Reporting rate** $r$ (packets/second) implies total packets delivered:

$$
N_{pkt} = T_{net} \cdot r
$$

**Step 3 — Combining the two equations:**

$$
N_{pkt} = \frac{E_0 \cdot r}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $T_{net}$ in terms of $r$:**

$$
T_{net} = \frac{N_{pkt}}{r}
$$

Or equivalently, if energy per packet is $E_{pkt}$:

$$
T_{net} = \frac{E_0}{r \cdot E_{pkt}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{net} \propto \frac{1}{r}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration:**

If $r$ doubles, $T_{net}$ is halved. If $r$ is reduced by 10x, $T_{net}$ increases by 10x.

---

**(b)** What is Pareto optimality in WSN design? With a 2D diagram of lifetime vs. latency, explain the Pareto front and discuss how a designer selects the operating point. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Pareto Optimality (2 marks):**

A WSN design configuration $\mathbf{x}^*$ is **Pareto-optimal** if there is no other feasible design $\mathbf{x}$ that improves one figure of merit (e.g., lifetime) without worsening another (e.g., latency). The set of all such designs forms the **Pareto Front** in the objective space.

**2D Diagram (Lifelike Description) — drawn on graph paper in exam:**

- X-axis: **End-to-end latency** ($\tau$, seconds) — increasing right.
- Y-axis: **Network lifetime** ($T_{net}$, days) — increasing up.
- **Pareto Front:** A curve sweeping from the top-left (high $T_{net}$, high $\tau$) to the bottom-right (low $\tau$, low $T_{net}$).
- **Dominated region:** Below and to the left of the front — designs in this region are suboptimal.
- **Infeasible region:** Above and to the right of the front — physically unattainable.

**Three Operating Points (3 marks):**

- **Point A (low latency, low lifetime):** Suits **emergency response** applications.
- **Point B (balanced):** Suits **environmental monitoring**.
- **Point C (high lifetime, high latency):** Suits **agricultural sensing**.

**Designer Selection Process (2 marks):**

The designer assigns **weights** $w_i$ to each goal (e.g., $w_{lifetime} = 0.7$, $w_{latency} = 0.3$ for agriculture) and selects the point on the Pareto Front that maximizes the weighted sum $F = w_1 \cdot T_{net} - w_2 \cdot \tau$. This is the **scalarization** of the multi-objective problem.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Optimization & Figures of Merit Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing goal with metric:** Writing "energy is a figure of merit" is vague. Always specify **"energy per bit"** with units **J/bit** (1 mark penalty).
> 2. **Missing the Pareto distinction:** Many students answer multi-objective questions by picking one goal and ignoring others. Always mention **Pareto optimality** (lose 2–3 marks otherwise).
> 3. **Wrong lifetime definition:** "$T_{net}$ = battery life of one node" is incorrect — it is a **system-level** metric, not a node-level one.
> 4. **Skipping units in Part A:** Even in 3-mark questions, **forgetting units** (J/bit, seconds, dimensionless) costs at least 0.5 mark per FoM.
> 5. **First-order radio model parameters:** $E_{elec}$ and $\epsilon_{amp}$ must be **defined explicitly** in derivation questions. Don't write formulas without naming variables.
> 6. **Path-loss exponent confusion:** Use $n=2$ for **free-space** and $n=4$ for **two-ray ground reflection** — mixing them is a common error.
> 7. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Always include a **neat labeled diagram** (e.g., Pareto front) — examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for diagram quality.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Optimization Goals & Figures of Merit**

- [ ] **Eight canonical figures of merit** to memorize: $E_b$, $T_{net}$, $\mathcal{C}$, $\kappa$, $\tau$, $\eta$, $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{S}$ — with units and direction.
- [ ] **First-order radio model:** $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$, $n=2$ (free space) or $n=4$ (multipath).
- [ ] **Energy per bit** for $h$-hop route: $E_b = (2h-1)E_{elec} + h \cdot \epsilon_{amp} \cdot (d_{total}/h)^n$.
- [ ] **Optimal hop count** for $n=2$: $h^* = \sqrt{\epsilon_{amp} d_{total}^{2} / (2 E_{elec})}$.
- [ ] **Network lifetime** $T_{net} = E_0 / P_{avg}$ — system-level, not node-level.
- [ ] **Coverage probability** for Poisson deployment: $\mathcal{C} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi R_s^{2}}$.
- [ ] **Connectivity condition:** $R_c \geq 2 R_s$ ensures coverage implies connectivity.
- [ ] **Lifetime vs. latency trade-off:** $T_{net} \propto 1/r$ (doubling rate halves life).
- [ ] **Pareto optimality:** No design improves one FoM without worsening another.
- [ ] **Pareto Front:** Curve/surface of all non-dominated designs in objective space.
- [ ] **Weighted scalarization:** $F = \sum w_i f_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$ to combine multiple goals.
- [ ] **Application priorities determine weights** (e.g., agriculture vs. battlefield).
- [ ] **WSN design is a multi-objective optimization problem**, not single-objective.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles of WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design Principles of WSNs # 📘 MODULE 2: DESIGN PRINCIPLES OF WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed autonomous network of tiny, resource-constrained sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, motion, or pollutants) and transmit their data through wireless links to a central sink or base station (BS) for further processing and analysis. The **Design Principles of WSNs** refer to the set of fundamental architectural, hardware, protocol-level, and algorithmic guidelines that govern the construction of an efficient, energy-aware, fault-tolerant, and scalable sensor network. These principles are dictated by the severe constraints of the sensor nodes (limited battery, low memory, low bandwidth, small form factor) and the often hostile deployment environment. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System of a City" Imagine a **smart city** monitored not by a few large CCTV cameras (which are like powerful but expensive centralized nodes), but by **thousands of tiny dust-sized sensors** scattered across every streetlight, pothole, pipeline, and building. Each sensor is like a single **nerve ending** in the human body — it can only sense a small stimulus and has very little "energy food" (battery) to send signals. They must collaborate like a **distributed nervous system**: - Each node is **stupid alone** but **intelligent together** (collaborative signal processing). - The **skin of the city** (the sensor field) sends impulses (data) through a **spinal cord** (multi-hop routing) to the **brain** (sink/BS). - Energy is **blood supply** — once a node runs out, it "dies," and the network loses a sensory cell. - Therefore, **design** must focus on **energy economy, self-organization, and survivability**, just as the human nervous system has evolved to do. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU:** WSN design is fundamentally an **energy-centric discipline**. Every design decision — from MAC layer to routing to application layer — is judged primarily by how it **conserves node energy** and **extends network lifetime**. ### 1.2 The Six Core Design Goals of a WSN | S.No | Design Goal | Engineering Meaning | |------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Energy Efficiency** | Maximize operational lifetime on a finite battery. | | 2 | **Scalability** | Network must function seamlessly from 10 to 10,000 nodes. | | 3 | **Fault Tolerance** | Network survives node/link failures via redundancy. | | 4 | **Self-Organization** | Nodes auto-configure topology without human intervention. | | 5 | **Data-Centric Operation** | Queries are addressed to *data*, not to specific node IDs. | | 6 | **Application-Specific Design** | No "one-size-fits-all" WSN — design is tailored to the use case. | ### 1.3 The Three Categories of Design Influences (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > A KTU board question frequently asks: *"List the factors influencing WSN design."* The canonical answer classifies them into three layers: 1. **Hardware Constraints** → small size, limited battery, low memory, restricted processing power, short radio range. 2. **Software / Protocol Constraints** → limited bandwidth, dynamic topology, energy-aware algorithms, security. 3. **Environmental / Deployment Constraints** → unattended operation, hostile conditions, inaccessible terrain, large node density, mobility of nodes/sinks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical WSN Architecture A WSN is structured as a layered hierarchy of five fundamental entities: ### A. Sensor Node (Mote) The fundamental unit. Composed of four sub-units: - **Sensing Unit** — transducer + ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter). - **Processing Unit** — microcontroller + memory (RAM, ROM, Flash). - **Transceiver Unit** — short-range radio (typically 2.4 GHz ISM band, IEEE 802.15.4). - **Power Unit** — battery (often non-rechargeable in field deployment). ### B. Sensor Field The **physical region** where nodes are deployed. Density $\rho$ is a critical parameter: - Low density: $\rho < 0.01$ nodes/m² (sparse monitoring). - High density: $\rho > 10$ nodes/m² (dense coverage with redundancy). ### C. Sink (Base Station / Gateway) The data collection point. May be **static** or **mobile**. Acts as the bridge between the WSN and the outside world (Internet, satellite, etc.). ### D. Task Manager (User) The end-user who issues queries and receives processed information. ### E. Event / Phenomenon The physical event being sensed (e.g., fire, vehicle movement, toxic leak). --- ## 2.2 The WSN Protocol Stack (OSI-Aligned, KTU Standard) | Layer | WSN-Specific Function | KTU Key Protocol Examples | |-------|-----------------------|---------------------------| | **Application Layer** | Application-specific software | Sense-and-respond, SQR-SD (Sensor Query and Data Dissemination) | | **Transport Layer** | Reliable end-to-end delivery | TCP-like protocols (rare), UDP-based variants | | **Network Layer** | Multi-hop routing, addressing | Directed Diffusion, LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN | | **Data Link (MAC) Layer** | Channel access, framing, error control | S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee | | **Physical Layer** | Modulation, encoding, transmission | O-QPSK, BPSK, DSSS, UWB | > [!NOTE] > The **cross-layer design** is unique to WSNs. Unlike traditional networks, WSN protocols are tightly coupled across layers because saving energy at one layer affects the decisions at another. --- ## 2.3 The Three Operating Environments | Environment | Example | Design Implication | |-------------|---------|---------------------| | **Terrestrial** | Open fields, forests, deserts | Short-range radios, line-of-sight issues | | **Underground** | Soil, mines, pipelines | Extremely high attenuation, low bandwidth | | **Underwater** | Oceans, lakes | Acoustic communication (very slow, ~1500 m/s), expensive nodes | | **Multi-media** | Acoustic + seismic + visual | Heterogeneous sensors, high data rates | --- ## 2.4 Hardware Design Constraints — Numerical Specifications > [!IMPORTANT] > These are the **canonical numbers** tested in KTU exams. Memorize them. | Parameter | Typical Value | |-----------|---------------| | **Battery capacity** | 2 AA alkaline cells ≈ **2,200 mAh @ 3V** | | **Microcontroller power** | Active: 10–30 mW, Sleep: < 1 µW | | **Radio power (Tx)** | 20–100 mW | | **Radio power (Rx)** | 15–50 mW | | **Radio range** | 10–100 m (outdoor) | | **Data rate** | 10–250 kbps (typical) | | **Node lifetime target** | Months to years | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas are derived from the **Heinzelman Energy Dissipation Model** (LEACH paper, 2000) — the most tested energy model in KTU exams. ### 🔋 Energy Model (First-Order Radio Model) | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |--------|---------|------| | $E_{Tx}(k,d)$ | Energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ | Joule (J) | | $E_{Rx}(k)$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | Joule (J) | | $E_{elec}$ | Electronics energy (per bit, Tx or Rx) | nJ/bit | | $\epsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier energy | pJ/bit/m² | | $\epsilon_{mp}$ | Multipath amplifier energy | pJ/bit/m⁴ | | $d_0$ | Crossover distance | m | | $E_{DA}$ | Data aggregation energy | nJ/bit/signal | ### 📐 Core Equations **Transmit Energy:** $$ E_{Tx}(k,d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{0} \quad \text{(Free Space)} \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d \ge d_{0} \quad \text{(Multipath)} \end{cases} $$ **Receive Energy:** $$ E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec} $$ **Crossover Distance (the threshold at which the two models are equal):** $$ d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} $$ **Total Energy for One Transmission Round (1-bit, distance $d$):** $$ E_{total}(1,d) = E_{Tx}(1,d) + E_{Rx}(1) = E_{elec}(1 + k) + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The $d^{2}$ vs $d^{4}$ Rule of Thumb:** > - For $d < d_{0}$ → energy grows as **square of distance** (free-space, line-of-sight). > - For $d \ge d_{0}$ → energy grows as the **fourth power of distance** (multipath, obstacles). > - This is why **multi-hop short-distance routing** is vastly more energy-efficient than long-haul single-hop transmission. ### 📡 Path Loss Models **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):** $$ PL_{fs}(d) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) \quad \text{(dB)} $$ **Log-Distance Path Loss (Empirical):** $$ PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10 \cdot n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_{0}}\right) $$ where $n$ is the **path loss exponent**: - $n = 2$ → free space - $n = 3$–$4$ → urban/indoor with obstacles - $n = 4$–$6$ → heavily obstructed ### 📊 Network Performance Metrics **Network Lifetime $T_{net}$:** $$ T_{net} = \min_{i=1 \dots N}\left(\dfrac{E_{initial}(i)}{P_{consumed}(i)}\right) $$ > Network dies when the **first node** exhausts its battery (worst-case definition). **Energy Per Useful Bit Delivered:** $$ E_{bit} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{k_{useful}} \quad \text{(J/bit)} $$ **Data Aggregation Compression Ratio:** $$ CR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Data after aggregation}}{\text{Data before aggregation}} $$ **Node Density (uniform deployment over area $A$):** $$ \rho = \dfrac{N}{A} \quad \text{(nodes/m}^2\text{)} $$ **Coverage Area per Node (assuming circular coverage with sensing radius $r_s$):** $$ A_{cov} = \pi \cdot r_{s}^{2} $$ **k-Coverage Requirement:** $$ N \cdot \pi r_{s}^{2} \ge k \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \ge \dfrac{k \cdot A}{\pi r_{s}^{2}} $$ --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | WSN Design Principle Emphasized | |--------------------|--------------------------------| | **Precision Agriculture** | Energy efficiency + solar harvesting | | **Forest Fire Detection** | Fault tolerance + dense k-coverage | | **Structural Health Monitoring** | High data rate + low-latency | | **Battlefield Surveillance** | Security + stealth (low probability of detection) | | **Healthcare Body Area Networks** | Miniaturization + low power | | **Industrial IoT** | Reliability + real-time guarantees | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Multi-Hop is More Energy-Efficient (The $d^4$ Killer) This is the **single most important derivation** in WSN design. KTU board examiners love to ask this to prove energy efficiency of multi-hop routing. ### 📝 Problem Statement A node must transmit a $k$-bit packet to a sink at distance $D$. Compare: - **Direct (single-hop)** transmission: Node sends the packet straight to the sink. - **Multi-hop** transmission: Node sends to an intermediate node at distance $D/2$, which forwards it to the sink. Use the **multipath** model ($d \geq d_0$) with $n=4$. --- ### Step 1: Direct Transmission Energy For single-hop over distance $D$: $$ E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4} $$ The receiver also expends energy: $$ E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec} $$ So total energy for direct path: $$ E_{direct,total} = 2k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot D^{4} $$ --- ### Step 2: Multi-Hop Transmission Energy (2 hops of $D/2$) Each hop has distance $d_1 = d_2 = D/2$. **Energy at hop 1 (Transmit):** $$ E_{hop1,Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{4} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot \dfrac{D^{4}}{16} $$ **Energy at hop 1 (Receive):** $$ E_{hop1,Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec} $$ **Energy at hop 2 (Transmit to sink):** $$ E_{hop2,Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{4} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot \dfrac{D^{4}}{16} $$ **Total Multi-Hop Energy:** $$ E_{multi,total} = E_{hop1,Tx} + E_{hop1,Rx} + E_{hop2,Tx} $$ Substituting: $$ E_{multi,total} = \left(k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} \dfrac{D^{4}}{16}\right) + k E_{elec} + \left(k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} \dfrac{D^{4}}{16}\right) $$ $$ E_{multi,total} = 3k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot \dfrac{D^{4}}{8} $$ --- ### Step 3: Energy Ratio (Direct ÷ Multi-Hop) $$ \dfrac{E_{direct,total}}{E_{multi,total}} = \dfrac{2k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{3k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} \dfrac{D^{4}}{8}} $$ Factor out $k$: $$ \dfrac{E_{direct,total}}{E_{multi,total}} = \dfrac{2 E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{3 E_{elec} + \dfrac{\epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{8}} $$ Multiply numerator and denominator by 8: $$ \dfrac{E_{direct,total}}{E_{multi,total}} = \dfrac{16 E_{elec} + 8 \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{24 E_{elec} + \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}} $$ --- ### Step 4: Asymptotic Behaviour for Large $D$ For **large $D$**, the term $\epsilon_{mp} D^{4}$ dominates the $E_{elec}$ term: $$ \lim_{D \to \infty} \dfrac{E_{direct,total}}{E_{multi,total}} = \dfrac{8 \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{\epsilon_{mp} D^{4}} = 8 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** For long distances, **multi-hop transmission consumes only $1/8$ the energy** of a direct transmission. This is a factor-of-8 energy saving — a foundational result that justifies the existence of multi-hop routing protocols like LEACH, PEGASIS, and Directed Diffusion. ### Step 5: Optimal Number of Hops Generalizing to $n$ equal hops of distance $D/n$: $$ E_{multi,n} = (n+1) k E_{elec} + n \cdot k \epsilon_{mp} \left(\dfrac{D}{n}\right)^{4} $$ $$ E_{multi,n} = (n+1) k E_{elec} + \dfrac{k \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{n^{3}} $$ Minimizing with respect to $n$ (and assuming $E_{elec}$ term is small): $$ \dfrac{dE_{multi,n}}{dn} \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{opt} \approx \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 k \epsilon_{mp} D^{4}}{k E_{elec}}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Crossover Distance $d_0$ Equating the free-space and multipath models: $$ k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{fs} d^{2} = k E_{elec} + k \epsilon_{mp} d^{4} $$ Cancel $k E_{elec}$ from both sides: $$ \epsilon_{fs} d^{2} = \epsilon_{mp} d^{4} $$ Divide by $d^{2}$ (assuming $d \neq 0$): $$ \epsilon_{fs} = \epsilon_{mp} d^{2} $$ Solve for $d$: $$ d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical example (KTU exam style):** > If $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, then: > > $$ > d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m} > $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSN Design Influences | Design Factor | Direct Impact on | Mitigation Strategy | |---------------|------------------|---------------------| | **Limited battery** | Network lifetime | Energy harvesting, duty cycling, in-network aggregation | | **Limited memory** | Algorithm complexity | Lightweight protocols, hierarchical storage | | **Limited bandwidth** | Data throughput | Compression, aggregation, scheduled MAC | | **Unattended operation** | Maintainability | Self-organization, remote reprogramming | | **Hostile environment** | Fault tolerance | Redundancy, multipath routing, k-coverage | | **Adversarial access** | Security | Encryption, tamper-proofing, trust management | | **Dynamic topology** | Routing overhead | Geographic routing, cluster-based schemes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of a Sensor Node (Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four functional sub-units and their power/data interactions inside a single sensor mote. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSICAL WORLD] -->|Analog Signal| B[SENSING UNIT] B -->|Transducer Interface| C[ADC Converter] C -->|Digital Samples| D[PROCESSING UNIT] D -->|Control Signals| B D -->|Data Packets| E[TRANSCEIVER UNIT] D -->|Power State Mgmt| P[POWER UNIT] E -->|Radio Waves| W[Wireless Channel] W -->|Incoming RF| E P -->|DC Supply| B P -->|DC Supply| D P -->|DC Supply| E MEM[Memory RAM ROM Flash] --- D subgraph Sensor_Node_Block direction TB B C D E P MEM end ``` --- ## 4.2 WSN Protocol Stack (Cross-Layer View) ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[APPLICATION LAYER
Sensing Apps and Queries] L4[TRANSPORT LAYER
Reliable Delivery UDP Variants] L3[NETWORK LAYER
LEACH PEGASIS Directed Diffusion] L2[DATA LINK MAC LAYER
S-MAC IEEE 802.15.4] L1[PHYSICAL LAYER
Modulation O-QPSK BPSK] L5 <-->|Cross Layer Energy Awareness| L1 L4 <-->|Cross Layer| L2 L3 <-->|Cross Layer| L2 L3 <-->|Cross Layer| L1 L2 <-->|Cross Layer| L1 PM[POWER MANAGEMENT PLANE] -.-> L5 PM -.-> L4 PM -.-> L3 PM -.-> L2 PM -.-> L1 TM[TASK MANAGEMENT PLANE] -.-> L5 MM[MOBILITY MANAGEMENT PLANE] -.-> L3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Multi-Hop vs Single-Hop Transmission (Energy Topology) ```mermaid flowchart LR N0[NODE 0
Source] -->|Hop 1: Distance d| N1[NODE 1
Relay] N1 -->|Hop 2: Distance d| N2[NODE 2
Relay] N2 -->|Hop 3: Distance d| BS[BASE STATION SINK] N0 -.->|Direct Path: 3d| BS E1[Energy: E per hop] --- N0 E2[Energy: E per hop] --- N1 E3[Energy: E per hop] --- N2 ED[Energy: 8x E for direct] --- BS ``` --- ## 4.4 WSN Network Architecture (Hierarchical) ```mermaid flowchart TB U[USER / TASK MANAGER] -->|Internet| GW[GATEWAY / BASE STATION] GW -->|Cluster Head CH1| CL1[Cluster 1] GW -->|Cluster Head CH2| CL2[Cluster 2] GW -->|Cluster Head CH3| CL3[Cluster 3] CL1 --> N1A[Mote 1A] CL1 --> N1B[Mote 1B] CL1 --> N1C[Mote 1C] CL2 --> N2A[Mote 2A] CL2 --> N2B[Mote 2B] CL3 --> N3A[Mote 3A] CL3 --> N3B[Mote 3B] CL3 --> N3C[Mote 3C] CL3 --> N3D[Mote 3D] subgraph Sensor_Field CL1 CL2 CL3 end ``` --- ## 4.5 Design Influences Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB A[WSN DESIGN DRIVERS] --> B[HARDWARE CONSTRAINTS] A --> C[PROTOCOL CONSTRAINTS] A --> D[ENVIRONMENTAL CONSTRAINTS] B --> B1[Battery Limited] B --> B2[Memory Limited] B --> B3[Compute Limited] C --> C1[Bandwidth Limited] C --> C2[Dynamic Topology] C --> C3[Energy Aware] D --> D1[Unattended] D --> D2[Hostile] D --> D3[Dense] D --> D4[Possibly Mobile] B1 & B2 & B3 --> OUT[DESIGN GOAL Energy Efficient Self Organizing Fault Tolerant] C1 & C2 & C3 --> OUT D1 & D2 & D3 & D4 --> OUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any four design challenges of WSNs.** **Model Answer:** A Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) is a self-configuring network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and communicate wirelessly to a central sink. **Four Design Challenges:** 1. **Limited Energy** — Battery-powered nodes with no easy recharging. 2. **Limited Hardware Resources** — Small memory, low CPU power, short radio range. 3. **Harsh Deployment Environment** — Unattended, possibly hostile, and inaccessible. 4. **Scalability and Dynamic Topology** — Hundreds to thousands of nodes with frequent changes. > **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Differentiate between data-centric and address-centric networking in WSNs.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Address-Centric (Traditional IP) | Data-Centric (WSN) | |-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------| | **Addressing** | To a specific node IP | To *data* (attribute-value pair) | | **Query style** | "Send to node 17" | "Send all temp > 30°C" | | **Example** | TCP/IP, HTTP | Directed Diffusion, SPIN | | **Robustness** | Fails if node dies | Multiple nodes may answer | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` > > **(a)** Explain the architecture of a wireless sensor node with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each unit. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the energy consumption equation for transmitting $k$ bits over a distance $d$ in the free-space and multipath models. Also derive the expression for crossover distance $d_0$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### 🟢 Solution to Question A #### (a) Architecture of a Sensor Node (7 Marks) A sensor node (also called a *mote*) is composed of **four functional units** and an optional **locator/mobility module**. **1. Sensing Unit** - Comprises **sensors** (temperature, pressure, humidity, acoustic, seismic, etc.) and an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. - Converts physical phenomena into analog electrical signals and then into digital data. - *Example sensor:* MEMS accelerometer consuming ~2 mW active. **2. Processing Unit** - A **microcontroller** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, MSP430) coupled with **memory subsystems**: - **RAM** for runtime data (~10 KB on typical motes). - **ROM/Flash** for program storage (~128 KB – 1 MB). - Runs the embedded OS (e.g., TinyOS, Contiki) and executes routing, aggregation, and security algorithms. **3. Transceiver Unit** - A short-range, low-power **radio** operating in unlicensed ISM bands (433 MHz, 868 MHz, 2.4 GHz). - Modulation schemes: O-QPSK, BPSK, DSSS. - IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee are the most common standards. **4. Power Unit** - Usually **two AA alkaline cells** (≈ 2,200 mAh, 3V). - May include **energy harvesting** modules (solar, vibrational, thermal). - Sleep mode power must be in the order of **µW**. **Block Diagram (Recap):** > Sensing Unit → ADC → Processing Unit ↔ Memory; Processing Unit ↔ Transceiver; Power Unit feeds all three. **Conclusion:** The interplay of these four units is the foundation of all WSN operations; their tight energy coupling dictates the lifetime and capability of every sensor node. --- #### (b) Energy Consumption Equation Derivation (7 Marks) Consider transmitting a packet of $k$ bits over a distance $d$. **Transmitter block consumes energy in two ways:** 1. Running the **radio electronics** (filters, mixers, oscillator) — independent of $d$. 2. **Powering the amplifier** to reach the destination — strongly dependent on $d$.
