```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Yellow blocks are the analog **RF chain**, blue blocks are the **digital baseband**, and green blocks are support systems. Note that the **Frequency Synthesizer (VCO)** feeds both the Tx upconverter and the Rx downconverter — a critical shared resource in low-cost WSN transceivers like the **TI CC2420** or **Atmel AT86RF230**.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Physical Layer State Machine for a WSN Mote

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> WakeUp: Timer Interrupt
    WakeUp --> ChannelAssessment: CCA Check
    ChannelAssessment --> Transmit: Channel Clear
    ChannelAssessment --> Sleep: Channel Busy Backoff
    Transmit --> WaitAck: Send Data Frame
    WaitAck --> Receive: Listen for ACK
    WaitAck --> Sleep: ACK Timeout Retransmit Pending
    Receive --> ProcessRX: Packet Received CRC OK
    Receive --> Sleep: CRC Failed Discard
    ProcessRX --> Transmit: Forward to Next Hop
    ProcessRX --> Sink: Deliver to Application
    Sink --> Sleep: Duty Cycle End
    WaitAck --> Sleep: Max Retries Reached
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Map:** This state diagram illustrates the **PHY-MAC coupling** unique to WSN. Notice that the **dominant state is SLEEP** — a WSN mote spends >99% of its life in sleep to conserve battery. This is the fundamental difference from Wi-Fi (always-on).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List and briefly explain any three physical layer design considerations unique to Wireless Sensor Networks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Energy Efficiency (1 mark):** Sensor motes are battery-powered (often non-rechargeable, e.g., coin cell). The PHY must minimize energy per bit ($E_b$) by using low-duty-cycle, low-power modulations like BPSK or O-QPSK, instead of high-throughput but energy-hungry OFDM used in Wi-Fi.

2. **Low Hardware Cost (1 mark):** WSN deployments may involve thousands of nodes. The PHY transceiver IC must be inexpensive (target < \$5), which forces trade-offs like using single-chip transceivers with shared LO, instead of bulky discrete RF chains.

3. **Robustness to Multipath & Interference (1 mark):** WSNs operate in harsh environments (indoor, industrial, foliage). The PHY must use robust modulation (e.g., DSSS in IEEE 802.15.4) and provide sufficient link margin (typically 20+ dB) to combat fading.

4. *(Bonus, for distinction)* **Compact Form Factor:** Antennas must fit within tiny motes, limiting them to chip/PCB antennas, which constrains the operating frequency and gain.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define receiver sensitivity. How is it calculated for a WSN receiver with bandwidth 2 MHz, noise figure 6 dB, and minimum SNR requirement of 12 dB?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Receiver sensitivity ($S_{\min}$) is the **minimum received signal power** (in dBm) that a receiver can detect and demodulate with an acceptable bit error rate (BER).

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\min}(\text{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + \text{NF} + \text{SNR}_{\min}
$$

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(2\times 10^6) + 6 + 12
$$
$$
S_{\min} = -174 + 63.01 + 6 + 12 = -92.99 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Answer:** $S_{\min} \approx -93$ dBm.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **Friis free-space path loss equation** in detail. Explain each term and show its conversion to the logarithmic (dB) form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN node transmits at **$P_t = +5$ dBm** at **2.4 GHz** to a receiver located **50 m** away. Both antennas have a gain of **1.5 dBi**. System losses (cables, connectors) total **2 dB**. Determine: (i) the received power, and (ii) the link margin if the receiver sensitivity is **$-90$ dBm**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Friis Free-Space Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Isotropic Power Density (1.5 marks):**
For an ideal isotropic radiator with transmit power $P_t$, the power flux density at distance $d$ is uniform on a sphere of surface $4\pi d^2$:

$$
S_d = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad (\text{W/m}^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Aperture (1.5 marks):**
The effective collecting area of an antenna with gain $G_r$ operating at wavelength $\lambda = c/f$ is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Combining (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_r = S_d \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}
$$

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to dB (1.5 marks):**
Taking $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$ of both sides:

$$
P_r(\text{dBm}) = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) + 147.55
$$

Which collapses to the field-friendly form:

$$
L_{fs}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation (1 mark):**
Doubling distance ⇒ 6 dB extra loss; doubling frequency ⇒ 6 dB extra loss. This is why **sub-GHz bands travel farther** than 2.4 GHz for the same antenna size — critical for long-range WSNs like LoRa.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Link Budget (7 Marks)**

**Given (1 mark):**
$P_t = +5$ dBm, $f = 2400$ MHz, $d = 50$ m = 0.05 km, $G_t = G_r = 1.5$ dBi, $L_{\text{sys}} = 2$ dB, $S_{\min} = -90$ dBm.

**Step 1 — Compute Path Loss (2 marks):**

$$
L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.05) + 20\log_{10}(2400)
$$
$$
L_{fs} = 32.44 + (-26.02) + 67.60 = 74.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Received Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs} - L_{\text{sys}}
$$
$$
P_r = 5 + 1.5 + 1.5 - 74.02 - 2 = -68.02 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Link Margin (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Link Margin} = P_r - S_{\min} = -68.02 - (-90) = 21.98 \text{ dB}
$$

**Conclusion (marks awarded as final sentence):**
The link is **healthy with ~22 dB margin**, sufficient to combat indoor fading and multipath.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **log-distance path loss model** for WSNs. Discuss the role of the **path loss exponent $n$** and **shadow fading $X_\sigma$** with suitable examples for indoor, urban outdoor, and factory environments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN uses **O-QPSK modulation** at **2.4 GHz** with chip rate **2 Mchips/s**, data rate **250 kbps**, and transmit power **0 dBm**. (i) Calculate the energy per bit if the transmitter draws **17.4 mA** from a **3 V** source while transmitting. (ii) Comment on how modulation choice and transmit power affect overall node lifetime. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Log-Distance Path Loss Model (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Equation (2 marks):**

$$
P_r(d)\,[\text{dBm}] = P_r(d_0) - 10\,n\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma
$$

- $d_0$ = reference distance (typically 1 m), measured empirically.
- $n$ = **path loss exponent** (rate of decay with distance).
- $X_\sigma$ = **shadow fading** (zero-mean Gaussian, standard deviation $\sigma$ in dB).

**Step 2 — Role of $n$ (2 marks):**
$n$ captures the propagation environment. Higher $n$ ⇒ faster signal decay. Typical values:

- **Indoor LOS (hallway):** $n = 1.6$ to $1.8$, $\sigma = 3$ dB — almost free-space.
- **Urban outdoor (sensor on streetlight):** $n = 3.0$ to $4.0$, $\sigma = 6$ to $8$ dB.
- **Factory floor (metal clutter, machinery):** $n = 5.0$ to $6.0$, $\sigma = 10$ to $12$ dB.

**Step 3 — Role of $X_\sigma$ (2 marks):**
Shadow fading models large-scale obstructions (walls, machinery, human bodies). For $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, the **outage probability** at the cell edge is:

$$
P_{\text{outage}} = Q\!\left(\frac{\text{Link Margin}}{\sigma}\right)
$$

For 95% coverage reliability with $\sigma = 8$ dB, designer must add **~13 dB margin** — driving the WSN to use higher transmit power or better modulation.

**Step 4 — WSN Design Implication (1 mark):**
WSN simulations (e.g., in NS-2, Castalia) require accurate $n$ and $\sigma$ values per environment to predict packet delivery ratio (PDR) realistically.

#### **Part (b) — Energy per Bit & Lifetime (7 Marks)**

**Given (1 mark):** $R_b = 250$ kbps, $V = 3$ V, $I_{\text{avg,tx}} = 17.4$ mA, $P_t = 0$ dBm.

**Step 1 — Transmit Power in Watts (1 mark):**

$$
P_{\text{elec,tx}} = V \cdot I = 3 \times 17.4 \times 10^{-3} = 52.2 \text{ mW} = -12.83 \text{ dBm}
$$

(Note: most power is consumed by the RF chain, not the radiated 0 dBm.)

**Step 2 — Time per Bit (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^3} = 4\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy per Bit (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_b = P_{\text{elec,tx}} \cdot T_{\text{bit}} = 52.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 208.8 \text{ nJ/bit}
$$

**Step 4 — Lifetime Calculation (1.5 marks):**
Assume a 2 × AA battery pack = 2000 mAh @ 3 V = 6000 J.

$$
\text{Continuous Tx lifetime} = \frac{6000}{52.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 114{,}942 \text{ s} \approx 31.9 \text{ hours}
$$

This is why **duty cycling** is essential — with 1% duty cycle, lifetime extends to ~133 days.

**Step 5 — Modulation/Power Comment (1 mark):**
- Switching from O-QPSK to **BPSK** reduces energy per bit at the same BER by ~3 dB.
- Reducing $P_t$ by 3 dB halves radiated power, but does **not** halve battery consumption, because the PA may still consume quiescent current — a key WSN design subtlety.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing units in Friis formula** (km vs. m, MHz vs. GHz) — *lose 1–2 marks*.
> 2. **Using SNR directly instead of $E_b/N_0$** in BER equations — *lose 2 marks*.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract system losses** ($L_{\text{sys}}$) in link budget — *lose 1 mark*.
> 4. **Not converting to dB** before subtracting — common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Confusing path loss exponent $n$** with the "Friis exponent 2" — examiners reward students who distinguish free-space vs. real environments.
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer** — always end with the units (dBm, dB, dBi).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Physical Layer Role:** Frequency selection, modulation, encoding, transmit/receive.
- [ ] **ISM Bands:** 433/868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz — license-free.
- [ ] **IEEE 802.15.4:** Uses O-QPSK with DSSS at 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps.
- [ ] **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$; doubles distance ⇒ **+6 dB loss**.
- [ ] **Log-Distance Model:** $P_r(d) = P_0 - 10n \log(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$; WSN simulation standard.
- [ ] **Path Loss Exponents:** $n=2$ (free space), 3–4 (urban), 5–6 (factory).
- [ ] **Receiver Sensitivity:** $-174 + 10\log_{10}B + \text{NF} + \text{SNR}_{\min}$.
- [ ] **BER (BPSK):** $\frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ — lowest power for given BER.
- [ ] **BER (BFSK/OOK):** $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ — simpler hardware.
- [ ] **Link Budget:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs} - L_{\text{sys}}$ (all dB).
- [ ] **Link Margin:** Always > 15–20 dB for robust WSN deployment.
- [ ] **Energy per Bit:** $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot T_{\text{bit}}$ — minimize via duty cycling.
- [ ] **Duty Cycling:** Mote sleeps >99% of the time — the core WSN power-saving strategy.
- [ ] **Hardware Examples:** Mica2 (433 MHz BFSK), MicaZ/TelosB (2.4 GHz O-QPSK, CC2420).
- [ ] **Engineering Trade-off:** Lower data rate ⇒ lower energy per bit but higher latency.
- [ ] **Memory Aid:** **"WSN PHY = low power + low cost + low duty cycle + robust modulation."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Ad Hoc Networks and Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobile Ad Hoc Networks and Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) — Formal Definition

A **Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices (nodes) connected by wireless links. According to the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) MANET Working Group**, each node acts both as a host (running user applications) and as a router (forwarding packets for other nodes), with the topology changing dynamically as nodes move arbitrarily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A MANET is an autonomous collection of mobile nodes that communicate over bandwidth-constrained wireless links. Because no fixed infrastructure (e.g., base stations or access points) is required, the network must **self-organize** and **self-configure**, and routing must adapt continuously to mobility-induced topology changes.

### 1.2 Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) — Formal Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed network of autonomous, tiny, low-power sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants) and pass their data through the network to a main location called the **sink** (or base station).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** A WSN is fundamentally a **data-centric** network — the user queries phenomena (e.g., "What is the temperature in zone A?") rather than specific node addresses. This is a critical paradigm shift from traditional address-centric networks.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**MANET Analogy — The "Disaster Rescue Walkie-Talkie Team":**
Imagine a search-and-rescue team entering a collapsed building where all cellular towers are destroyed. Each rescuer carries a walkie-talkie that can both speak AND relay messages from other rescuers. As they move through the rubble, the chain of communication constantly rewires itself. This is a **MANET** — mobile humans, no towers, dynamic paths.

**WSN Analogy — The "Smart Vineyard":**
Picture thousands of tiny battery-powered soil-moisture sensors scattered across a 100-acre vineyard. Each sensor measures soil wetness a few times per hour, then whispers that data to its neighbor, who passes it onward, hop-by-hop, until it reaches the farmer's central computer. Nobody worries about the sensors' exact location — the farmer only cares *where the dry patches are*. This is a **WSN** — many tiny, energy-starved, data-collecting nodes.

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **MANET** node count: typically **10–100 nodes**, highly mobile (speeds up to **\sim 30 m/s**).
- **WSN** node count: typically **hundreds to thousands**, mostly **stationary or quasi-static**.
- **WSN energy budget**: often a single non-rechargeable battery for **2–5 years**.
- **WSN per-node cost target**: under **USD 1** for mass deployment.
- **MANET primary design goal**: maintain **end-to-end connectivity** despite mobility.
- **WSN primary design goal**: maximize **network lifetime** under strict energy constraints.

> [!TIP]
> The easiest one-line exam memory aid: *"MANETs = routing-centric, WSNs = data-centric."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Characteristics of MANETs

A MANET exhibits the following operational traits, each of which directly influences protocol design:

- **Dynamic Topology:** Nodes move arbitrarily, causing frequent link formation and breakage. Routing tables must be updated continuously.
- **Bandwidth-Constrained Links:** Wireless channels are shared and limited. Effective throughput is often significantly lower than the raw radio rate due to collisions, fading, and contention.
- **Energy-Constrained Operation:** Mobile devices rely on batteries; power-aware routing is essential (though less severe than WSNs).
- **Limited Physical Security:** Wireless transmission is inherently open to eavesdropping, jamming, and spoofing.
- **Multi-hop Routing:** Because transmission range is limited, packets traverse intermediate nodes acting as routers.
- **No Centralized Administration:** Self-organization eliminates single points of failure but complicates coordination.

### 2.2 Operational Characteristics of WSNs

WSNs differ from MANETs in important ways:

- **Severe Energy Constraints:** Energy is the **single most critical resource**; protocol design is fundamentally shaped by power consumption.
- **Densely Deployed Nodes:** Often hundreds to thousands in a small region, requiring **scalable** algorithms.
- **Data-Centric Paradigm:** Queries target phenomena (e.g., "any temperature > 40 °C?"), not specific sensor IDs.
- **Application-Specific Design:** Hardware, OS, and protocols are tailored to the sensing task.
- **Limited Computational Power:** Tiny microcontrollers (8- or 16-bit), small RAM (typically **2–10 KB**), modest clock rates.
- **Unreliable Sensor Nodes:** Nodes can fail due to battery exhaustion, hardware faults, or environmental damage; protocols must be **fault-tolerant**.
- **Frequent Topology Change (logical):** Even if nodes are static, logical topology changes due to node death, energy depletion, and link unreliability.

### 2.3 MANET Routing Protocol Classification

| Protocol Class | Mechanism | Representative Protocols | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proactive (Table-Driven)** | Maintain fresh routes to all nodes at all times | **DSDV**, OLSR | High control overhead; fast delivery |
| **Reactive (On-Demand)** | Discover routes only when needed | **AODV**, **DSR** | Low overhead; higher initial latency |
| **Hybrid** | Combine both approaches | **ZRP** | Balances overhead and latency |
| **Hierarchical** | Cluster-based routing | **CBRP**, LEACH (in WSN) | Scalable; complex cluster maintenance |
| **Geographic / Position-Based** | Use node location to route | **GPSR** | Energy-efficient; requires localization |

### 2.4 WSN Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

The WSN protocol stack consists of five layers plus three management planes:

- **Application Layer:** User applications (e.g., habitat monitoring, target tracking).
- **Transport Layer:** Reliable data delivery, congestion control (e.g., PSFQ, RMST).
- **Network Layer:** Routing of sensed data (e.g., SPIN, LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion).
- **Data Link Layer:** MAC protocol (e.g., S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4), error control.
- **Physical Layer:** Frequency selection, modulation, signal detection, encryption.
- **Power Management Plane:** Manages node power consumption.
- **Mobility Management Plane:** Detects and registers node movement (less critical in WSNs).
- **Task Management Plane:** Schedules sensing tasks among nodes.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU ESE and module tests. Units in parentheses indicate standard SI units unless otherwise noted.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P_t$ = transmit power (W), $P_r$ = received power (W), $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), $d$ = distance (m) |
| 2 | **Two-Ray Ground Reflection Model** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \dfrac{h_t^{2} h_r^{2}}{d^{4}}$ | $h_t, h_r$ = antenna heights (m); valid for large $d$ |
| 3 | **Energy to Transmit 1 bit (free space, distance $d$)** | $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ | $E_{elec}$ = electronics energy/bit (**50 nJ/bit** typical), $\varepsilon_{fs}$ = amplifier energy (**10 pJ/bit/m²**) |
| 4 | **Energy to Transmit 1 bit (multipath, distance $d$)** | $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$ | $\varepsilon_{mp}$ = multipath amplifier energy (**0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴**); used when $d \geq d_0$ |
| 5 | **Crossover Distance $d_0$** | $d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$ | Switch point between free-space and multipath models (typically **\sim 87 m**) |
| 6 | **Energy to Receive 1 bit** | $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Receiver electronics energy/bit |
| 7 | **Total Energy for Multi-hop over $n$ equal hops** | $E_{total} = n \cdot \left( E_{Tx}(d/n) + E_{Rx} \right)$ | For comparing with direct transmission energy |
| 8 | **Network Lifetime (definition)** | $T_{net} = \min_{i \in V} t_{i,death}$ | Time until first node exhausts its battery |
| 9 | **Throughput over a shared channel** | $S = \dfrac{\text{useful packet delivery rate}}{\text{channel capacity}}$ | Dimensionless; used in MAC analysis |
| 10 | **Average Energy per Useful Bit** | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\text{Number of bits successfully delivered}}$ | Key WSN efficiency metric (J/bit) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** All absolute-value and fraction bars have been replaced with `\vert` or `\dfrac` to preserve KTU markdown rendering integrity. Do **not** write raw `|` symbols inside KTU answer scripts' tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MANETs** are deployed in **military tactical networks (SINCGARS)**, **disaster response**, **vehicular ad hoc networks (VANETs)** for intelligent transport systems, and **rover swarms in space exploration**.
- **WSNs** underpin the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, **precision agriculture**, **structural health monitoring of bridges and dams**, **wildfire detection**, and **remote patient monitoring** in healthcare.
- The **differences in design philosophy** between MANETs (routing-centric) and WSNs (data/energy-centric) influence virtually every protocol layer, making the comparison one of the **most frequently tested topics in KTU Module 3**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Direct Transmission vs. Multi-Hop Transmission Energy

One of the **highest-yield KTU derivations** is the energy comparison between sending a $k$-bit packet directly to a sink at distance $d$ versus sending it through $n$ equal hops.

**Step 1 — Define the energy model.**

A sensor node expends energy on the **transmit electronics**, the **transmit amplifier**, and the **receive electronics**. Let:
- $k$ = packet size in bits.
- $E_{elec}$ = energy dissipated per bit by transmitter or receiver electronics.
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = energy dissipated per bit per $m^{2}$ (free-space) or per $m^{4}$ (multipath) in the transmit amplifier.
- $d$ = distance between source and sink (direct).

**Step 2 — Write the energy for DIRECT transmission.**

In free-space conditions, the transmit amplifier cost dominates at a quadratic rate:

$$
E_{direct} = E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the energy for MULTI-HOP transmission over $n$ hops.**

In an $n$-hop chain, each hop covers distance $d/n$. The total transmit energy is the sum across $n$ hops, plus $n$ reception events (one at each intermediate node):

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot \left( E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{n}\right)^{2} \right) + n \cdot \left( E_{elec} \cdot k \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify.**

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}}{n} + n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

$$
E_{multi} = 2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}}{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare $E_{direct}$ and $E_{multi}$.**

For large $d$ and $n \geq 2$, the term $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}}{n}$ decreases with $n$, while $2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k$ grows linearly. Differentiating with respect to $n$ and setting to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d(E_{multi})}{dn} = 2 E_{elec} \cdot k - \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}}{n^{2}} = 0
$$

$$
n^{2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}}{2 E_{elec}}
$$

$$
n_{opt} = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{2 E_{elec} / \varepsilon_{fs}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the engineering insight.**

> [!TIP]
> For a fixed source-sink distance $d$, there exists an **optimal hop count $n_{opt}$** that minimizes total energy. Multi-hop transmission is always more energy-efficient than direct transmission when $d$ is large relative to the electronics-to-amplifier energy ratio. This is the **foundational justification** for hop-by-hop routing in WSNs.

### 3.2 Derivation: Friis Free-Space Equation (for WSN Link Budget)

**Step 1 — Start from the isotropic radiator assumption.**

The power density at distance $d$ from an isotropic radiator of transmit power $P_t$ is:

$$
S = \dfrac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the effective aperture of the receiving antenna.**

The effective aperture of a lossless antenna is $A_{e} = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \cdot G_r$. So:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4 \pi} \cdot G_r
$$

**Step 3 — Include both antenna gains $G_t$ and $G_r$:**

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to path-loss form (in dB).**

The free-space path loss is:

$$
L_{fs}(dB) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

(when $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz, the constant 32.44 accounts for unit conversion).

**Step 5 — Implication for WSN design.**

A doubling of distance $d$ incurs a **6 dB** (factor of 4) path loss penalty. This is why **multi-hop short-range communication** is preferred over long-range direct links in energy-constrained WSNs.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MANET vs WSN

This matrix is the **single most important KTU comparison** for this topic.

| Parameter | **MANET** | **WSN** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Design Goal | Maintain end-to-end **connectivity** | Maximize **network lifetime** |
| Routing Paradigm | **Address-centric** (route from node A to B) | **Data-centric** (route to/from "phenomenon") |
| Node Count | 10 – 100 | 100 – 10 000+ |
| Mobility | **High** (vehicular, pedestrian) | **Low** (mostly stationary) |
| Energy Constraint | Moderate (rechargeable) | **Severe** (non-rechargeable) |
| Node Cost | High (smartphones, laptops) | **Very low** (\textless \$1 target) |
| Computational Power | High (GHz CPUs) | **Very low** (8/16-bit MCUs) |
| Data Flow | Peer-to-peer, symmetric | Many-to-one (sensors to sink) |
| Traffic Pattern | Interactive, real-time | Periodic, event-driven |
| Redundancy | Low (each node unique) | **High** (spatial redundancy by design) |
| Security Threat Model | Eavesdropping, spoofing, DoS | Node capture, physical tampering, replay |
| Typical Routing Protocols | AODV, DSR, OLSR, ZRP | SPIN, LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion |
| Addressing | **IP-style** (node IDs) | **Attribute-based** (data names) |
| Failure Model | Link breakage due to mobility | Node death due to battery depletion |
| Topology Change Frequency | **Continuous** (mobility) | Episodic (node death, environmental) |
| Localization Importance | Moderate | **High** (data tagged with location) |

### 3.4 Derivations: Network Lifetime Estimation

**Energy consumed per round by a node in LEACH-like clustering:**

$$
E_{round} = k \cdot \left( 2 n \cdot E_{elec} + n \cdot E_{DA} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} \right)
$$

where $E_{DA}$ is the data-aggregation energy per bit, and $d_{toBS}$ is the cluster-head-to-base-station distance.

**Total rounds until death:**

$$
L_{round} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}}
$$

where $E_{initial}$ is the initial energy of the node (typically **0.5 J to 2 J** per node in Mica-class motes).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensor Node Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Sensing Unit\nSensors plus ADC] --> nodeB[Processing Unit\nMicrocontroller plus Memory]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Transceiver Unit\nRadio plus Antenna]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Power Unit\nBattery plus DC-DC]
    nodeA -.power.-> nodeD
    nodeB -.power.-> nodeD
    nodeC -.power.-> nodeD
    nodeE[External Trigger\nor Timer Wakeup] --> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram shows the **four classical subunits** of a sensor mote (sensing, processing, communication, power) as per Akyildiz et al.'s foundational WSN architecture. The dotted lines indicate **power rails**, while the solid arrows show **data/signal flow**.

### 4.2 WSN Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer5[Application Layer\nUser Queries and Reports] --> layer4[Transport Layer\nReliable Delivery]
    layer4 --> layer3[Network Layer\nMulti Hop Routing]
    layer3 --> layer2[Data Link Layer\nMAC plus Error Control]
    layer2 --> layer1[Physical Layer\nRadio plus Modulation]
    plane1[Power Management Plane] -.-> layer1
    plane1 -.-> layer2
    plane1 -.-> layer3
    plane2[Mobility Management Plane] -.-> layer2
    plane2 -.-> layer3
    plane3[Task Management Plane] -.-> layer5
    plane3 -.-> layer3
```

### 4.3 MANET vs WSN Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MANET_BOX[MANET Domain]
        m1[Mobility is High]
        m2[Routing is Address Centric]
        m3[Energy is Moderate]
        m4[Goal: End to End Connectivity]
    end
    subgraph WSN_BOX[WSN Domain]
        w1[Mobility is Low]
        w2[Routing is Data Centric]
        w3[Energy is Severe]
        w4[Goal: Maximize Lifetime]
    end
    m1 --- w1
    m2 --- w2
    m3 --- w3
    m4 --- w4
```

### 4.4 Deployment Topology of a Typical WSN

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sinkA[Sink or Base Station\nGateway to Internet] --> hop1[Sensor Node 1\nCluster Head]
    hop1 --> node1[Sensor Node A]
    hop1 --> node2[Sensor Node B]
    hop1 --> node3[Sensor Node C]
    sinkA --> hop2[Sensor Node 2\nCluster Head]
    hop2 --> node4[Sensor Node D]
    hop2 --> node5[Sensor Node E]
    hop2 --> node6[Sensor Node F]
    node1 -.multi hop to sink.-> sinkA
    node4 -.multi hop to sink.-> sinkA
```

### 4.5 MANET Routing Protocol Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA[MANET Routing Protocols] --> br1[Proactive Table Driven]
    rootA --> br2[Reactive On Demand]
    rootA --> br3[Hybrid]
    rootA --> br4[Hierarchical]
    rootA --> br5[Geographic Position Based]
    br1 --> leaf1[DSDV OLSR]
    br2 --> leaf2[AODV DSR TORA]
    br3 --> leaf3[ZRP]
    br4 --> leaf4[CBRP]
    br5 --> leaf5[GPSR]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: WSN Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Physical Phenomenon\nTemperature Humidity Vibration] --> capA[Sensor Transduction\nAnalog Signal]
    capA --> capB[Analog to Digital\nConversion]
    capB --> capC[Local Processing\nAggregation and Filtering]
    capC --> capD[Multi Hop Wireless\nCommunication]
    capD --> capE[Sink Node]
    capE --> capF[User Application\nDatabase Web Portal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare the design objectives of Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) and Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs). Which resource constraint is the most critical in each?**

**Model Answer:**

- **MANET design objective:** Maintain **end-to-end connectivity** between mobile nodes despite arbitrary topology changes. Routing efficiency, mobility management, and Quality of Service (QoS) are primary goals.
- **WSN design objective:** Maximize **network lifetime** under extreme energy constraints, while reliably delivering sensed data to the sink. Fault tolerance and data aggregation are primary goals.
- **Most critical resource in MANET:** **Bandwidth** and **mobility handling**, since the wireless channel is shared and routes change rapidly.
- **Most critical resource in WSN:** **Energy (battery power)**, since nodes are typically deployed in inaccessible locations with non-rechargeable batteries.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for MANET objective, 1 mark for WSN objective, 1 mark for the critical resource comparison.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any four challenges unique to Wireless Sensor Networks.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Severe energy constraints** — non-rechargeable batteries and energy-harvesting limitations force ultra-low-power protocol design.
2. **Limited hardware resources** — tiny memory (KB range), low CPU speeds, simple radios.
3. **Harsh deployment environments** — nodes may face temperature extremes, humidity, physical damage, leading to high **node failure rates**.
4. **Scalability** — protocols must support thousands of nodes with minimal overhead.
5. *(Optional additional point)* **Data-centric addressing** — query-based data retrieval rather than ID-based routing.
6. *(Optional additional point)* **Localization** — sensed data must typically be tagged with location.

**Marks Distribution:** 4 valid points × 0.75 marks each ≈ 3 marks; usually 4 clearly stated points suffice for full credit.

---

### **PART B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical sensor node. List the responsibilities of each unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the energy consumption model for a sensor node transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$ in free space, and show that multi-hop transmission is more energy-efficient than direct transmission for large $d$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The architecture of a typical sensor node consists of **four main units**:

1. **Sensing Unit:**
   - Comprises one or more physical sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, pressure) and an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
   - The sensor produces a continuous analog signal proportional to the observed phenomenon; the ADC samples and digitizes it.
   - Responsibility: convert physical phenomena into digital data.

2. **Processing Unit:**
   - Includes a **microcontroller** (or microprocessor) plus limited **memory** (RAM + ROM/Flash).
   - Responsible for local data processing, aggregation, scheduling, and executing communication protocols.
   - Often runs a lightweight **Operating System** such as TinyOS or Contiki.

3. **Transceiver Unit:**
   - A short-range radio (typically operating in 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz, or 2.4 GHz ISM bands).
   - Responsible for **wireless transmission and reception** of packets.
   - Modes: transmit, receive, idle, sleep (each with very different power draws).

4. **Power Unit:**
   - Usually a **battery** (e.g., 2× AA alkaline = ~2.7 Ah at 3 V) plus a **DC-DC converter** to supply regulated voltages.
   - May include **energy-harvesting** elements (solar, vibration, thermal) in advanced designs.
   - Responsibility: provide energy to all other units.

> **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the visual representation.)*

**Marks Distribution (a):** 2 marks for the diagram, 1 mark for sensing unit, 1 mark for processing unit, 1 mark for transceiver, 1 mark for power unit, 1 mark for responsibilities summary.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Energy model for transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$ (free space):**

The transmit energy has two components: **electronics energy** and **amplifier energy**:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy model for receiving $k$ bits:**

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 3 — Direct transmission energy over distance $d$:**

$$
E_{direct} = E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multi-hop transmission energy over $n$ equal hops of length $d/n$:**

Total transmit energy across $n$ hops, plus reception at each of the $n$ intermediate nodes:

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot E_{Tx}\!\left(k, \dfrac{d}{n}\right) + (n-1) \cdot E_{Rx}(k)
$$

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot \left(E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{n}\right)^{2}\right) + (n-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
E_{multi} = 2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k - E_{elec} \cdot k + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}}{n}
$$

For large $n$, the $-E_{elec} \cdot k$ term is negligible:

$$
E_{multi} \approx 2n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}}{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare $E_{direct}$ and $E_{multi}$:**

Since $E_{multi}$ has a term that **decreases as $1/n$** (the amplifier term) and a term that **increases linearly with $n$** (the electronics term), an optimum $n$ exists. For $d$ large enough, $E_{multi} < E_{direct}$ even for $n = 2$. The amplifier energy saved by halving the distance (which quarters the free-space loss) outweighs the additional reception cost.

**Conclusion:** For sufficiently large source-sink distances, multi-hop transmission is more energy-efficient than direct transmission, justifying hop-by-hop routing in WSNs.

**Marks Distribution (b):** 1 mark for energy model, 1 mark for direct TX, 2 marks for multi-hop derivation, 2 marks for comparison and justification, 1 mark for final conclusion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Define MANET. Explain its key characteristics and list the major challenges in MANET design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare MANET and WSN using a detailed table across at least 8 parameters. State at least three application areas unique to WSNs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** A **Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices that communicate over wireless links. Each node acts as both a host (running applications) and a router (forwarding packets for others). There is no central base station or access point.

**Key Characteristics:**

1. **Dynamic topology** — nodes move arbitrarily, leading to frequent link formation and breakage.
2. **Multi-hop communication** — packets traverse intermediate nodes since transmission range is limited.
3. **Bandwidth-constrained links** — wireless channels are shared and have limited capacity.
4. **Energy-constrained operation** — mobile devices rely on batteries (less severe than WSNs).
5. **No infrastructure** — the network must self-organize and self-configure.
6. **Limited physical security** — wireless transmission is open to eavesdropping and attacks.

**Major Challenges:**

- **Routing under mobility** — maintaining accurate routes when topology changes constantly.
- **QoS provisioning** — guaranteeing delay, jitter, and bandwidth for real-time applications.
- **Security** — defending against malicious nodes, wormholes, black holes, and Sybil attacks.
- **Power management** — extending battery life without sacrificing performance.
- **Scalability** — protocols must scale to hundreds of nodes.
- **Mobility models** — designing realistic mobility patterns for testing.

**Marks Distribution (a):** 1 mark for definition, 3 marks for characteristics (½ mark each × 6), 3 marks for challenges (½ mark each × 6).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Comparison Table (MANET vs WSN):**

| Parameter | MANET | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | End-to-end connectivity | Network lifetime maximization |
| Routing Paradigm | Address-centric | Data-centric |
| Node Count | 10 – 100 | 100 – 10 000+ |
| Mobility | High | Low / quasi-static |
| Energy Constraint | Moderate | Severe |
| Node Cost | High (\$100–\$1000) | Very low (\textless \$1) |
| Traffic Pattern | Interactive, real-time | Many-to-one, periodic, event-driven |
| Fault Tolerance | Low (each node is unique) | High (spatial redundancy) |
| Localization | Not critical | Critical (data tagged with location) |
| Data Aggregation | Optional | Common (essential) |
| Typical Protocols | AODV, DSR, OLSR, ZRP | SPIN, LEACH, PEGASIS, DD |

**Three application areas unique to WSNs:**

1. **Precision Agriculture** — soil moisture, pH, and crop health monitoring across vast fields.
2. **Structural Health Monitoring** — detecting strain, vibration, and cracks in bridges, dams, and high-rise buildings.
3. **Environmental Monitoring** — tracking forest fires, glacier melting, air pollution, and wildlife habitats.
4. *(Optional additional)* **Remote Healthcare** — body-area sensor networks monitoring patient vitals.
5. *(Optional additional)* **Industrial Process Control** — monitoring temperature, pressure, and flow in chemical plants.

**Marks Distribution (b):** 4 marks for the comparison table (½ mark per parameter × 8), 3 marks for unique WSN applications (1 mark each × 3).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating MANETs and WSNs** — Many students write "both are wireless" and lose marks. The KTU key specifically wants the **data-centric vs address-centric** distinction and the **energy-severity** difference.
> 2. **Missing units in energy formulas** — Writing $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ **without stating units** (J, nJ/bit, pJ/bit/m²) loses a half mark.
> 3. **Skipping the multi-hop derivation** — Just stating "multi-hop is better" is insufficient; the KTU key requires **deriving the optimal $n$** or at least showing the comparison algebraically.
> 4. **Forgetting the $d_{0}$ crossover distance** — When asked about energy models, students who only give the free-space model lose marks if the question mentions "long-distance" or "multipath."
> 5. **Not labelling the Mermaid-style diagrams clearly** — In handwritten exams, the **sensing, processing, transceiver, and power** units must each be **boxed and named** to get full marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MANET** = Mobile + Ad Hoc + No infrastructure + Self-organizing.
- ✅ **WSN** = Many small sensor motes + Data-centric + Energy-starved.
- ✅ **Key difference:** MANETs are **routing-centric**; WSNs are **data/energy-centric**.
- ✅ **Sensor node architecture:** Sensing + Processing + Transceiver + Power (4 units).
- ✅ **WSN protocol stack** has 5 layers + 3 management planes (Power, Mobility, Task).
- ✅ **Energy for TX (free space):** $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d^{2}$.
- ✅ **Energy for TX (multipath):** $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{mp} k d^{4}$.
- ✅ **Crossover distance:** $d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87$ m.
- ✅ **Multi-hop > Direct** for large $d$ because of quadratic (or quartic) loss.
- ✅ **Optimal hop count exists** to minimize total network energy.
- ✅ **Network lifetime** = time until the first node dies (strict) or until a fraction $k$ of nodes die (relaxed).
- ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit; $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²; $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴; $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit.
- ✅ **MANET routing categories:** Proactive, Reactive, Hybrid, Hierarchical, Geographic.
- ✅ **AODV** = Reactive, uses RREQ/RREP/RERR; **DSDV** = Proactive, uses sequence numbers.
- ✅ **WSN-specific protocols:** SPIN, LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion.
- ✅ **WSN applications:** Agriculture, healthcare, environment, industry, military, structural monitoring.
- ✅ **Friis equation:** $P_{r} = P_{t} G_{t} G_{r} (\lambda / 4\pi d)^{2}$ — must know for link-budget questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Buses and Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Field Bus — The Formal Definition In industrial automation, the term **Fieldbus** refers to a family of **industrial computer network protocols** used for **real-time distributed control**. Formally, a fieldbus is a *bidirectional, multidrop, serial-bus digital communication system* that replaces the traditional point-to-point analog wiring (4–20 mA current loops) between field-level sensors/actuators and the controller level (PLCs, DCS, SCADA). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** > A **Fieldbus** is the *layer-1 (physical) and layer-2 (data-link) industrial networking standard* that interconnects field devices (transmitters, valves, motor drives) with controllers using a *single shared digital bus*, governed by deterministic protocols such as **PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus, DeviceNet, CAN, and Modbus**. The original purpose of a fieldbus is to: - Eliminate the **analog point-to-point cabling** between sensor and controller. - Carry **both control data and power** (intrinsically safe two-wire operation). - Provide a **deterministic, time-bounded delivery** of process variables. --- ## 1.2 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) — The Industrial Context A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed **autonomous sensor motes** that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions and forward the sensed data through **multi-hop wireless links** to a central sink (base station). In the context of **Module 3**, WSN is positioned as the *wireless evolution* of the fieldbus paradigm — replacing the shared copper medium with RF transceivers while preserving the same functional goal: real-time process data exchange at the field level. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy: The "Digital Telephone Party-Line"** > > Imagine a 1980s office where every desk (sensor) had its **own dedicated phone line** (4–20 mA wire) to the manager's cabin (controller). Hundreds of copper pairs ran through the plant. A **fieldbus** is the upgrade where all desks share *one digital conference line* — each desk gets a tiny time slot (token / TDMA slot) to speak, and the manager hears everyone. > > A **Wireless Sensor Network** is the *cordless Bluetooth headset version* of that conference line. Each desk has a tiny wireless microphone (mote). The microphones relay whispers to neighbours until the message reaches the manager (sink), with batteries instead of power cables. --- ## 1.4 Why Fieldbus Evolved into WSN — The Motivation The transition from wired fieldbus to wireless field networks (industrial WSNs) is driven by the following engineering realities: | # | Industrial Problem | Wired Fieldbus Limitation | WSN Solution | |---|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------| | 1 | Rotating machinery instrumentation | Cable wrap, slip-ring wear | Battery-powered radio motes | | 2 | Hazardous zone (Zone-1, Zone-0) cabling | Explosion-proof conduit cost | Intrinsically safe wireless | | 3 | Retro-fit on legacy plants | Trench excavation cost | Non-invasive deployment | | 4 | Mobility of assets (cranes, AGVs) | Cable tether | Mobile wireless nodes | | 5 | Temporary monitoring campaigns | Fixed install overkill | Quick-deploy motes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly bridges *legacy fieldbus* (PROFIBUS, HART, Modbus) with *modern industrial WSN standards* (WirelessHART, ISA100.11a, ZigBee PRO, 6LoWPAN). The exam often asks a 14-mark comparison question between the two paradigms. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics to Remember - **Industrial operating temperature**: −40 °C to +85 °C. - **Cable-based fieldbus bandwidth**: **31.25 kbps** (H1, FOUNDATION Fieldbus) to **12 Mbps** (PROFIBUS DP, EtherCAT). - **Industrial WSN typical data rate**: **250 kbps** (IEEE 802.15.4 @ 2.4 GHz DSSS, O-QPSK). - **ISM band**: **2.4 GHz** (global, license-free) — used by **WirelessHART, ZigBee, ISA100.11a**. - **Latency target (process automation)**: ≤ **1 second** end-to-end (per ISA100.11a). - **Mote battery life target**: **5 to 10 years** on 2× AA lithium thionyl chloride. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The OSI Layer Mapping of a Fieldbus A traditional fieldbus typically implements only **three of the seven OSI layers** to keep stack overhead low and deterministic: $$ \text{Fieldbus OSI Subset} \;=\; \{ \text{Layer-1 Physical},\ \text{Layer-2 Data-Link},\ \text{Layer-7 Application} \} $$ | OSI Layer | Fieldbus Implementation | Example Protocol | |-----------|--------------------------|-------------------| | Layer 7 — Application | Process variable dictionary, function blocks | FOUNDATION Fieldbus FF | | Layer 2 — Data Link | Token passing / producer-consumer | PROFIBUS, CAN | | Layer 1 — Physical | Two-wire differential, Manchester / NRZ | RS-485, IEC 61158-2 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why three layers?** Industrial control loops run at 10–100 Hz; the 4-layer TCP/IP overhead would dominate. By stripping the stack, fieldbus guarantees **deterministic bounded latency**. --- ## 2.2 Major Wired Fieldbus Protocols (Detailed) ### 2.2.1 PROFIBUS (Process Field Bus) - **Physical:** RS-485 differential, 2-wire, half-duplex. - **Topologies:** Line (bus) with repeaters; max segment **32 nodes**, max length **1200 m** at **93.75 kbps**. - **Variants:** **PROFIBUS-DP** (Decentralised Periphery, fast I/O, ≤ 12 Mbps) and **PROFIBUS-PA** (Process Automation, intrinsic safety, 31.25 kbps). - **Access method:** Token passing between masters + master-slave polling. - **Cable type:** Shielded twisted pair, characteristic impedance **150 Ω**. ### 2.2.2 FOUNDATION Fieldbus (FF) - **Physical H1 layer:** IEC 61158-2, **31.25 kbps**, Manchester-coded, two-wire. - **Power on bus:** 9–32 V DC carrying both signal and 24 V device supply. - **HSE (High Speed Ethernet):** 100 Mbps backbone for upper-level control. - **Function blocks:** PID, AI, AO, DI, DO — distributed across devices. ### 2.2.3 CAN (Controller Area Network) / DeviceNet - **Original purpose:** Automotive (Bosch 1986). - **Bit rates:** **1 Mbps** at 40 m, scaling to **50 kbps** at 1000 m. - **Frame:** 11-bit or 29-bit ID, 0–8 byte payload, CSMA/CA + bit-stuffing. - **DeviceNet:** CAN-based industrial adaptation by Allen-Bradley. ### 2.2.4 Modbus (Modicon / Schneider) - **Variants:** Modbus RTU (RS-485), Modbus TCP (Ethernet). - **Simplicity:** Single function code → register read/write, master-slave only. - **Use:** Low-cost, legacy retrofits. ### 2.2.5 HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer) - **Hybrid protocol:** Digital signal **superimposed on 4–20 mA** loop using Bell-202 FSK (1200/2200 Hz). - **Backward compatibility** with analog instrumentation — extremely common in brown-field plants. - **WirelessHART (2007):** IEEE 802.15.4 @ 2.4 GHz, 16 channels, TSMP (Time-Synchronized Mesh Protocol), 10 ms time slots. --- ## 2.3 Industrial WSN Protocols — The Modern Layer Industrial WSN standards adopt a *thicker* protocol stack but ensure determinism through **time synchronization** rather than *token passing*. ### 2.3.1 IEEE 802.15.4 (Radio Foundation) - **Modulation:** O-QPSK (2.4 GHz) or BPSK (868/915 MHz). - **Data rate:** 250 kbps (2.4 GHz), 20/40 kbps (sub-GHz). - **Max packet:** 127 bytes (MAC frame). - **Channel access:** Slotted CSMA/CA with GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots). - **TX power:** typically 0 dBm (1 mW) to 8 dBm. ### 2.3.2 ZigBee PRO - **Network layer:** Mesh routing, AODV (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector). - **Profiles:** ZigBee PRO 2007 (energy harvesting, security). - **Limit for industrial use:** Best-effort QoS, no hard determinism. ### 2.3.3 WirelessHART (IEC 62591) - **Time sync:** All motes sync to ±10 µs via IEEE 1588 timestamping. - **Channel hopping:** 16 channels, channel offset rotated every **30 ms** for FTDMA reliability. - **Latency:** Multi-hop end-to-end ≈ **100 ms to 2 s**. ### 2.3.4 ISA100.11a - **Flexibility:** Supports native IPv6 via 6LoWPAN tunneling. - **Two-tier latency:** Class 1 ≤ 100 ms (control), Class 5 ≤ 1 s (monitoring). - **Backbone:** Connects to legacy fieldbus via gateway. --- ## 2.4 Core Engineering "Why" Behind Each Layer Choice > **Why Manchester coding in FOUNDATION Fieldbus?** > Each bit has a guaranteed *mid-bit transition*, so a constant DC level (e.g., bus fault, short) is detected instantly — critical for intrinsically safe (IS) instrumentation. > **Why TDMA + channel hopping in WirelessHART?** > Industrial RF is hostile (multipath, motors, drives). By rotating channel every 30 ms, narrow-band interference statistically affects only 1/16 of transmissions, giving redundancy without retransmission overhead. > **Why CSMA/CA in ZigBee?** > ZigBee targets building automation where latency tolerance is high; CSMA/CA saves mote energy (no idle listening between slots). --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Boundary / Value | |---|---------|------------------|------|------------------| | 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | 2.4 GHz ISM, B = 2 MHz | | 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | d in km, f in MHz | | 3 | Bit Energy per Noise | $\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R}$ | dB | O-QPSK needs ≥ 5 dB | | 4 | Battery Lifetime (ideal) | $T_{\text{life}} = \frac{Q_{\text{mAh}} \cdot V_{\text{avg}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | hours | AA LiSOCl₂ ≈ 2400 mAh | | 5 | WirelessHART slot duration | $T_{\text{slot}}$ | ms | 10 ms (typical) | | 6 | Token Rotation Time (PROFIBUS) | $T_{\text{TRT}} = \sum_i T_{i,\text{msg}}$ | ms | ≤ 50 ms (control) | | 7 | CSMA Backoff (IEEE 802.15.4) | $\text{backoff} = \text{rand}(0, 2^{\text{BE}} - 1)$ | slots | BE = 3…8 | | 8 | Hop Count Delay (multi-hop) | $D_{\text{total}} = n \cdot D_{\text{single-hop}}$ | s | ≈ 5–50 ms per hop | | 9 | Fieldbus Cable Delay | $t_{\text{cable}} = \frac{L}{v_p \cdot c}$ | s | $v_p \approx 0.66 c$ | | 10 | WSN Mote TX Energy (per bit) | $E_{\text{tx}} = V \cdot I_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{bit}}$ | J/bit | ≈ 50 nJ/bit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** When asked "compare latency of PROFIBUS vs WirelessHART," give **numerical values** (PROFIBUS ≈ 1 ms single-frame @ 12 Mbps; WirelessHART ≈ 100–500 ms end-to-end). Marks are awarded for *quantitative* comparison, not vague statements. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fieldbus vs Industrial WSN This is the **most high-yield KTU 14-mark module topic**. The exam routinely asks students to tabulate these eight parameters side-by-side. | # | Parameter | Wired Fieldbus (PROFIBUS / FF) | Industrial WSN (WirelessHART / ISA100.11a) | |---|-----------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------------| | 1 | **Medium** | Copper twisted pair / fibre | RF @ 2.4 GHz ISM | | 2 | **Topology** | Bus (linear), tree, star | Mesh (multi-hop), star-of-stars | | 3 | **Max nodes per segment** | 32 (PROFIBUS), 240 (FF) | 100+ typical mesh | | 4 | **Max distance (no repeater)** | 1200 m @ 93.75 kbps | ≈ 100 m per hop (LOS); 30 m (NLOS plant) | | 5 | **Bandwidth** | 31.25 kbps – 12 Mbps | 250 kbps (shared, lower effective) | | 6 | **Determinism** | Token-passing / scheduled | TDMA + time-sync (WirelessHART) | | 7 | **Power** | Bus-powered (2-wire IS) | Battery 5–10 yr | | 8 | **Installation cost (retro-fit)** | High (cable tray, conduit) | Low (clamp-on / magnetic mount) | | 9 | **EMI immunity** | Excellent (galvanic isolation) | Moderate (use channel hopping, DSSS) | | 10 | **Latency (control loop)** | 1–10 ms | 50–500 ms | | 11 | **Standards body** | IEC 61158, IEC 61784 | IEC 62591, ISA100, IEEE 802.15.4 | | 12 | **Cyber-security** | Physical separation = implicit security | AES-128 mandatory (WirelessHART) | --- ## 3.2 Latency Derivation for WirelessHART Multi-Hop Path This is the type of *worked numerical problem* KTU often sets in 7-mark sub-questions. **Problem Statement:** A WirelessHART network has a path of **$n = 4$ hops** between a field sensor and the gateway. Each hop occupies **one TDMA slot of $T_{\text{slot}} = 10$ ms**, and the packet occupies **two slots** (one TX, one RX). Compute the *single-direction minimum latency* and *round-trip worst-case latency* given a *retransmission probability of $p_r = 0.1$* with up to **2 retransmissions**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Single-hop slot consumption** $$ T_{\text{hop}} = T_{\text{TX}} + T_{\text{RX}} = 2 \times 10 \text{ ms} = 20 \text{ ms} $$ *Reason:* Each mote must transmit in its slot and the receiver must ACK in the next slot. This is the **half-duplex TDMA handshake**. **Step 2 — Multi-hop latency for $n$ hops** $$ T_{\text{path}} = n \cdot T_{\text{hop}} = 4 \times 20 \text{ ms} = 80 \text{ ms} $$ *Reason:* Each hop is sequential; the packet cannot "jump" two hops in a single slot — this is the fundamental cost of *multi-hop mesh*. **Step 3 — Average retransmission delay** Expected number of retransmissions: $$ E[r] = p_r \cdot 1 + p_r^2 \cdot 2 + \ldots = \sum_{k=1}^{K} k \cdot p_r^k $$ For $p_r = 0.1$ and $K = 2$: $$ E[r] = (0.1)(1) + (0.01)(2) = 0.10 + 0.02 = 0.12 \text{ retransmissions/hop} $$ **Step 4 — Total retransmission delay per hop** $$ T_{\text{re-tx,hop}} = E[r] \cdot 2 T_{\text{slot}} = 0.12 \times 20 \text{ ms} = 2.4 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 5 — Total single-direction latency** $$ T_{\text{sd}} = T_{\text{path}} + n \cdot T_{\text{re-tx,hop}} = 80 + 4 \times 2.4 = 89.6 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 6 — Round-trip worst-case (with $2$ retries on every hop)** $$ T_{\text{rt,wc}} = 2 \cdot \left[ n \cdot T_{\text{hop}} + n \cdot 2 \cdot 2T_{\text{slot}} \right] $$ $$ T_{\text{rt,wc}} = 2 \cdot \left[ 80 + 4 \cdot 40 \right] = 2 \cdot 240 = 480 \text{ ms} $$ **Conclusion:** This worst-case 480 ms round-trip satisfies the **ISA100.11a Class 2 latency budget (≤ 250 ms one-way)** *only marginally* — explaining why WirelessHART disallows control loops deeper than 3 hops in practice. --- ## 3.3 Battery Life Derivation for an Industrial Mote A Crossbow / Dust Networks mote (e.g., **MicaZ**) operates at $V_{\text{DD}} = 3.0$ V with: - **TX current**: $I_{\text{TX}} = 17.4$ mA (at 0 dBm, O-QPSK) - **RX current**: $I_{\text{RX}} = 19.7$ mA - **Sleep current**: $I_{\text{sl}} = 5 \ \mu\text{A}$ - **Duty cycle**: $1\%$ TX, $1\%$ RX, $98\%$ sleep Compute battery life on **2 × AA LiSOCl₂** ($Q = 2400$ mAh). **Step 1 — Effective average current** $$ I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{\text{TX}} \cdot t_{\text{TX}} + I_{\text{RX}} \cdot t_{\text{RX}} + I_{\text{sl}} \cdot t_{\text{sl}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}} $$ With $t_{\text{TX}} = t_{\text{RX}} = 0.01 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}$: $$ I_{\text{avg}} = (0.01)(17.4) + (0.01)(19.7) + (0.98)(0.005) \text{ mA} $$ $$ I_{\text{avg}} = 0.174 + 0.197 + 0.0049 = 0.3759 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical battery life** $$ T_{\text{life}} = \frac{Q}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{2400}{0.3759} \approx 6384 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to years** $$ T_{\text{life,years}} = \frac{6384}{24 \times 365} \approx 0.73 \text{ years} $$ **Step 4 — Apply practical derate factor (self-discharge, temperature, voltage drop) of 0.6** $$ T_{\text{life,real}} = 0.73 \times 0.6 \approx 0.44 \text{ years} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Industrial vendors quote "5–10 year battery" because they ship **energy-harvesting motes** or use **lithium-thionyl chloride with 14 Ah capacity**, not 2.4 Ah AA cells. Always state the battery model in numerical problems. --- ## 3.4 FSPL Quick Calculation for 2.4 GHz Indoor Industrial Link For a wireless mote **30 m** away in a metal-rich plant at **2.45 GHz**, with cable/connector losses of 1.5 dB and antenna gains of 2 dBi each: $$ f_{\text{MHz}} = 2450,\quad d_{\text{km}} = 0.030 $$ $$ \text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(0.030) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.44 $$ $$ = (-30.46) + (67.78) + 32.44 = 69.76 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \text{Link budget} = P_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{TX}} - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{cable}} + G_{\text{RX}} $$ $$ = 0 + 2 - 69.76 - 1.5 + 2 = -67.26 \text{ dBm} $$ The mote receiver sensitivity is typically **−95 dBm @ 250 kbps**. The link margin is: $$ M_{\text{link}} = -95 - (-67.26) = 27.74 \text{ dB} $$ This is **excellent** margin for a 30 m industrial link — explaining why WirelessHART works reliably in congested plants. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Protocol Stack Comparison — Fieldbus vs WSN ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FB["WIRED FIELDBUS STACK (e.g., FOUNDATION Fieldbus)"] FB_L7["Layer 7 - Application
Function Blocks AI AO PID"] FB_L2["Layer 2 - Data Link
Token Passing / Producer"] FB_L1["Layer 1 - Physical
IEC 61158-2 Manchester"] FB_MED["MEDIUM
Shielded Twisted Pair 31.25 kbps"] end subgraph WS["INDUSTRIAL WSN STACK (WirelessHART / ISA100.11a)"] WS_APP["Application Layer
Process Variables Mapping"] WS_TP["Transport Layer
UDP over 6LoWPAN"] WS_NW["Network Layer
Mesh Routing 802.15.4"] WS_MAC["MAC Layer
TSMP TDMA 10 ms slots"] WS_PHY["PHY Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 O-QPSK 2.4 GHz"] WS_RF["MEDIUM
Radio RF 2.4 GHz 250 kbps"] end FB_L7 --> FB_L2 --> FB_L1 --> FB_MED WS_APP --> WS_TP --> WS_NW --> WS_MAC --> WS_PHY --> WS_RF style FB fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style WS fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style FB_MED fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412 style WS_RF fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af ``` ## 4.2 Hybrid Plant Architecture — Fieldbus + WSN Co-existence ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FIELD["FIELD LEVEL - Wired Fieldbus"] S1[Smart Transmitter FF H1] S2[Pressure Sensor PROFIBUS PA] S3[Valve Positioner FF] B1["Fieldbus Cable 2-wire IS"] end subgraph WSNN["FIELD LEVEL - Wireless WSN"] W1[Wireless Mote 1 Temp] W2[Wireless Mote 2 Vibration] W3[Wireless Mote 3 Corrosion] WSNGW[WSN Gateway WirelessHART] end subgraph MID["CONTROL LEVEL"] PLC[PLC DCS Controller] SCADA[SCADA HMI Server] end S1 --> B1 S2 --> B1 S3 --> B1 W1 -. Radio .-> WSNGW W2 -. Radio .-> WSNGW W3 -. Radio .-> WSNGW B1 --> PLC WSNGW --> PLC PLC --> SCADA style FIELD fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style WSNN fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style MID fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 ``` ## 4.3 WirelessHART Mesh — Channel-Hopping TDMA Operation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MESH["WIRELESSHART MESH NETWORK"] D1[Field Device Mote A] D2[Field Device Mote B] D3[Field Device Mote C] D4[Field Device Mote D] D5[Field Device Mote E] AP[WirelessHART Gateway Access Point] end D1 -. ch1 30ms .-> D2 D2 -. ch5 30ms .-> D3 D3 -. ch9 30ms .-> AP D4 -. ch3 30ms .-> D2 D5 -. ch11 30ms .-> D3 D1 -. ch7 backup .-> AP D4 -. ch13 redundant .-> AP style MESH fill:#ecfeff,stroke:#155e75 style AP fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Fieldbus-to-WSN Migration Path ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Stage 1 - All Wired Fieldbus
4-20 mA Loops PROFIBUS PA"] -->|Retro-fit Phase| B["Stage 2 - Hybrid Deployment
Critical loops: Wired, Monitoring: WSN"] B -->|Greenfield Phase| C["Stage 3 - Wireless-First
WirelessHART backbone, FF H1 only at ESD valves"] C -->|Industry 4.0| D["Stage 4 - IIoT Convergence
OPC UA over 6LoWPAN to Cloud"] style A fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The mermaid block in 4.1 shows the *three-layer* fieldbus stack versus the *five-layer* WSN stack. The extra layers (transport, network) exist solely because WSN must *emulate* the connection-oriented guarantees of the wired bus on top of a *best-effort* RF medium. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. Define a Fieldbus. List any four popular fieldbus standards used in industrial automation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **Fieldbus** is a *bidirectional, multidrop, digital serial communication network* used to interconnect field-level instrumentation (sensors, actuators, valves) with controllers (PLCs, DCS) in real-time process automation, replacing the conventional 4–20 mA point-to-point analog wiring with a *single shared digital bus*. Four popular fieldbus standards: 1. **PROFIBUS** (Process Field Bus) — IEC 61158, RS-485 physical layer, 12 Mbps. 2. **FOUNDATION Fieldbus (FF)** — IEC 61158-2, 31.25 kbps H1 intrinsically safe variant. 3. **CAN / DeviceNet** — Bosch CAN protocol, 1 Mbps, dominant in automotive-derived systems. 4. **Modbus** — Modicon legacy, simple master-slave RTU over RS-485. *(Acceptable also: HART, INTERBUS, ControlNet, AS-Interface, EtherCAT.)* --- ### **Q2. What is the difference between a wireless sensor network (WSN) and a wired fieldbus in terms of latency?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Wired Fieldbus (e.g., PROFIBUS) | Wireless Sensor Network (e.g., WirelessHART) | |--------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------| | Determinism | Token-passing / TDMA; bounded | TDMA + channel hopping; bounded but slower | | Typical Control-Loop Latency | **1 – 10 ms** (single segment, 12 Mbps) | **50 – 500 ms** (multi-hop, 250 kbps) | | Reason for Difference | Copper propagation $\approx 2/3 \cdot c$; no retransmission | RF contention, time-sync handshakes, mesh forwarding | > *Key takeaway:* WSN trades latency for *cable-free deployment*; for sub-10 ms control loops, wired fieldbus is still preferred. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > - Many students *forget to state the data rate unit explicitly* (kbps vs Mbps) — 1 mark lost per occurrence. > - In comparison questions, **both** columns must be tabulated; bullet-only answers get partial credit at best. > - For WirelessHART, students often write *"TDMA"* only — the examiner wants **"TDMA + 10 ms slot + channel hopping + time sync"** as a single bundle (1 mark each). > - Do *not* confuse **HART** (hybrid 4-20 mA analog + digital FSK) with **WirelessHART** (IEEE 802.15.4 radio). Losing 2 marks. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a) Explain the architecture of a FOUNDATION Fieldbus (FF) system. Discuss the function of each layer in its protocol stack.** `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(b) Discuss the major industrial wireless sensor network protocols. Compare IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, WirelessHART, and ISA100.11a.** `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` ### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) FOUNDATION Fieldbus Architecture (7 marks)** **1. Two-Tier Network Hierarchy:** - **H1 Fieldbus:** 31.25 kbps, 2-wire, intrinsically safe (IEC 60079-11 compatible). Distance up to 1900 m per segment; max 32 devices per segment; supports bus-powered field devices. - **HSE (High Speed Ethernet):** 100 Mbps, standard IEEE 802.3u, used as the *backbone* linking multiple H1 segments via linking devices and gateways. **2. Protocol Stack Layers:** | Layer | Function | |-------|----------| | **Physical (Layer 1)** | IEC 61158-2 Manchester coding; 10 mA pk-pk signal at 9–32 V DC; bus-powered | | **Data Link (Layer 2)** | LAS (Link Active Scheduler) controls token circulation; CD (Compel Data) and DT (Data Transfer) services; deterministic bus arbitration | | **Application (Layer 7)** | Function blocks (PID, AI, AO, DI, DO), device description (DD), resource blocks for diagnosis | **3. Function Blocks:** Blocks reside *distributed* across field devices. A PID block may run in a *valve positioner*, an AI block in a *pressure transmitter* — the loop is closed over the bus. **4. LAS Mechanism:** The Link Active Scheduler grants "tokens" to specific devices; cyclic and acyclic communication are scheduled with deterministic phase durations. > **Why this matters:** FF's distributed control philosophy (DCS) lets loop-computation happen at the *edge*, eliminating analog point-to-point re-transmission delays. --- **(b) Major Industrial WSN Protocols — Comparison (7 marks)** | Parameter | IEEE 802.15.4 | ZigBee PRO | WirelessHART | ISA100.11a | |-----------|---------------|------------|--------------|------------| | **Standard Body** | IEEE | ZigBee Alliance | HART CF | ISA | | **Frequency** | 868/915/2450 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz only | 2.4 GHz | | **Data Rate** | 20–250 kbps | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | | **Modulation** | BPSK / O-QPSK | O-QPSK | O-QPSK + DSSS | O-QPSK | | **MAC** | Slotted CSMA/CA + GTS | CSMA/CA | TSMP TDMA | TSMP TDMA + CSMA | | **Topology** | Star, peer | Mesh, tree | Full mesh | Mesh + star | | **Determinism** | Low (best-effort) | Low (best-effort) | High (±10 µs sync) | High | | **Security** | AES-128 (optional) | AES-128 | AES-128 mandatory | AES-128 | | **Industrial Use** | Foundation only | Building automation | Process automation | Process automation | **Key comparative statements:** - *IEEE 802.15.4 is a radio only* — it specifies **PHY + MAC** but no network/application layers. - *ZigBee* stacks 802.15.4 + a *network* layer (AODV mesh) — suitable for *non-critical* monitoring. - *WirelessHART* stacks 802.15.4 + *TSMP* (Time-Synchronized Mesh Protocol) — the only standard **IEC 62591 certified** for process automation. - *ISA100.11a* allows IPv6/6LoWPAN integration — bridges to the **IIoT** world natively. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a) Explain the transition from wired fieldbus to wireless sensor networks in industrial plants. What are the key engineering drivers?** `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(b) Design a WirelessHART network for a refinery tank farm of 60 tanks, with each tank requiring temperature, pressure, and level monitoring. Justify topology, redundancy, and battery sizing.** `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` ### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Transition from Wired Fieldbus to WSN (7 marks)** **Engineering Drivers:** 1. **Cable Cost Reduction:** A 1000-instrumentation-point plant incurs ≈ 30% of CAPEX in cabling + cable trays + conduit. WSN reduces this to ≈ 5%. 2. **Retro-fit Feasibility:** Adding a sensor in a wired system requires excavation; a wireless mote is *clamp-on* — installed in minutes. 3. **Rotating / Mobile Assets:** Cranes, kilns, AGVs cannot be wired economically. 4. **Hazardous Areas (Zone-1, Zone-0):** Certified wireless motes eliminate explosion-proof conduit. 5. **Diagnostic-rich Maintenance:** Wireless enables vibration / corrosion monitoring on unreachable pipework. **Transition Path (Industry Standard):** - **Stage 1** — All-wired H1 FF (greenfield). - **Stage 2** — Hybrid: critical loops wired, monitoring wireless. - **Stage 3** — Wireless-first except for safety-instrumented ESD (Emergency Shutdown) loops. - **Stage 4** — IIoT convergence with OPC UA over 6LoWPAN. **Constraints that limit WSN adoption:** - *Latency-bound:* ESD loops need < 10 ms; WSN cannot guarantee this. - *EMI-rich plants:* Wireless may fail near VFDs, large motors. - *Cyber-security concerns:* Wireless is a higher attack surface. - *Battery maintenance* in 1000+ mote deployments. --- **(b) WirelessHART Tank-Farm Network Design (7 marks)** **1. Instrumentation Count:** $$ N_{\text{instruments}} = 60 \text{ tanks} \times 3 \text{ sensors/tank} = 180 \text{ wireless motes} $$ **2. Topology Choice — Full Mesh:** - **Why:** A linear/cluster tree cannot survive a single node failure; refinery safety demands redundant paths. WirelessHART mandates **mesh with at least 2 neighbours per mote**. - **Number of gateways:** For 180 motes with $\approx 30$ motes per AP (industry rule of thumb), we need **6 gateways** for redundancy. $$ N_{\text{AP}} = \lceil 180 / 30 \rceil \times 2_{\text{redundancy}} = 6 \times 2 = 6 \text{ gateways (2 hot-standby)} $$ Actually, applying the 2× redundancy rule: $N_{\text{AP}} = 12$ gateways, grouped into 2 redundant pairs across 3 process zones (each zone: 20 tanks = 60 motes). **3. Hop Count Planning:** The tank-farm is typically 200 m × 300 m. With motes on tank walls 30 m apart, **max hop count to nearest AP** ≈ **4 hops**. **4. Latency Verification:** $$ T_{\text{path}} = 4 \times 20 \text{ ms} = 80 \text{ ms} \;\; \text{(see derivation §3.2)} $$ This satisfies the **ISA100.11a Class 2 (control loop) budget of ≤ 100 ms** — design approved. **5. Battery Sizing:** Assuming **D-cell LiSOCl₂ ($Q = 14$ Ah, $V = 3.6$ V)** with $I_{\text{avg}} = 0.376$ mA (derived in §3.3): $$ T_{\text{life}} = \frac{14000}{0.376} \approx 37234 \text{ hours} \approx 4.25 \text{ years} $$ With **0.6 derate factor**: $$ T_{\text{life,real}} \approx 2.55 \text{ years} $$ To achieve the **industry-standard 5-year battery life**, use **D-cell with sleep-mode aggressive duty cycling** or **energy harvesting from vibration** on pump manifolds. **6. Channel Hopping Plan:** Configure all 180 motes on the default 16-channel schedule; rotate channel offset every 10 slots. **7. Security:** Enable AES-128 with join key provisioned in the WirelessHART handheld at commissioning. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MODULE-3 REVISION BULLET LIST — FIELD BUS & WSN** > > ✅ A **fieldbus** is a *shared digital bus* replacing 4–20 mA point-to-point wiring, deterministic and intrinsically safe. > ✅ The "Big-3" fieldbus families: **PROFIBUS (DP/PA), FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Modbus**; plus **CAN/DeviceNet** and **HART**. > ✅ H1 fieldbus speed = **31.25 kbps Manchester**, bus-powered. > ✅ PROFIBUS DP speed = up to **12 Mbps** RS-485. > ✅ HART is **hybrid** (1200/2200 Hz FSK over 4-20 mA) — *do not confuse with WirelessHART*. > ✅ A **Wireless Sensor Network** in industry replaces the copper with RF; same functional purpose. > ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4** defines PHY + MAC only; ZigBee/WirelessHART/ISA100 stack on top. > ✅ **WirelessHART** (IEC 62591) = TDMA + 10 ms slots + channel hopping + AES-128, **mandatory** for process automation. > ✅ **ISA100.11a** supports native IPv6/6LoWPAN — gateway to IIoT. > ✅ Typical industrial mote: **2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, O-QPSK, 0 dBm**, AES-128. > ✅ FSPL at 2.45 GHz, 30 m indoor ≈ **69.8 dB**; mote sensitivity ≈ **−95 dBm**; link margin typically > 25 dB. > ✅ Multi-hop latency = $n \cdot 2 T_{\text{slot}}$ ≈ **80 ms for 4 hops**; *round-trip worst case* ≈ 480 ms. > ✅ Battery sizing — D-cell LiSOCl₂ gives ≈ 4 years realistic at 1% duty cycle. > ✅ WirelessHART mesh, ZigBee mesh, FF bus — *star, tree, linear* are fieldbus topologies. > ✅ ESD (Emergency Shutdown) loops **must remain wired**; wireless is *not* yet SIL-3 rated for safety in most regions. > ✅ Migration path: *all-wired → hybrid → wireless-first → IIoT*. > ✅ For KTU: always quote **numerical values with units**; never write "high" or "low" without a number. > ✅ Common 14-mark question: *"Compare fieldbus and WSN"* — use the **Analytical Matrix §3.1** verbatim. ---

topics.title: Enabling Technologies for Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Enabling Technologies for Wireless Sensor Networks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Enabling Technologies" in WSN? **Enabling Technologies for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** refer to the interdisciplinary convergence of hardware, software, communication, and energy subsystems that collectively make the vision of large-scale, autonomous, untethered sensing a practical reality. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, this topic establishes the **engineering foundation** upon which every protocol, routing scheme, and application of Module 3 and beyond is built. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** > Enabling technologies for WSNs encompass the underlying physical and logical components that permit a sensor node to *sense*, *process*, *communicate*, and *sustain* itself. These include **wireless communication technologies (RF, ZigBee, Bluetooth LE, 6LoWPAN)**, **MEMS-based sensing elements**, **embedded microcontrollers/processors**, **energy sources (batteries & energy harvesting)**, and **networked software platforms (TinyOS, ContikiOS)**. --- ## 1.2 The Four Functional Pillars of a WSN Node A WSN node is essentially a **"miniaturized, battery-powered, wirelessly-connected data acquisition computer."** Every enabling technology feeds into one of four functional pillars: | Pillar | Function | Enabling Technology | |---|---|---| | **Sensing** | Convert physical phenomena → electrical signals | MEMS sensors, biosensors, chemical sensors | | **Processing** | Interpret, aggregate, store data | Embedded microcontrollers (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M) | | **Communication** | Wireless transmission/reception | RF transceivers (CC2420, CC2530), antennas | | **Power** | Sustain node lifetime | Batteries, solar/thermal/vibration energy harvesting | > [!NOTE] > **Design Reality Check:** In any commercial WSN node (e.g., MicaZ, TelosB, IRIS), the **radio** typically consumes **~50–80%** of total energy, while sensing and processing together consume **~10–30%**. This single statistic drives almost every protocol design choice in WSN literature. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Forest Watchtower" Model Imagine a **dense forest** that must be monitored 24/7 for fires, animal movement, and soil moisture. - **Traditional Approach:** Hire thousands of human watchmen (expensive, unsustainable, slow). - **WSN Approach:** Scatter thousands of **tiny, cheap, battery-powered "watchtowers"** (sensor motes). Each tower has: - A **microphone/smoke detector** (sensor) → *"the senses"* - A **small embedded chip** (microcontroller) → *"the brain"* - A **walkie-talkie antenna** (RF transceiver) → *"the voice"* - A **rechargeable battery + a tiny solar panel** (energy subsystem) → *"the stomach"* - A **pre-loaded rulebook** (TinyOS / ContikiOS) → *"the etiquette of when to talk"* Just as enabling technologies (electricity, telephone, paved roads) made modern cities possible, the **enabling technologies for WSNs** make the vision of an *"internet of things embedded in the physical world"* a practical reality. --- ## 1.4 Why These Technologies are "Enabling" — Three Decisive Properties 1. **Low Cost per Node (≪ 1 USD at scale):** Made possible by CMOS-MEMS co-integration. 2. **Ultra-Low Power (mW range):** Achieved through duty-cycling, low-power RF ICs, and energy harvesting. 3. **Ad-Hoc, Self-Organizing Operation:** Enabled by embedded network stacks (ZigBee, RPL over 6LoWPAN, BLE Mesh). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Favourite Question Cue:** Examiners frequently ask — *"List the four subsystems of a sensor node and give an example of each."* Memorize the four pillars above; the rest of the module is elaboration. --- ## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics to Know by Heart The following are the **canonical metrics** you must commit to memory for the KTU exam: - **Operating Frequency Bands:** 433 MHz (ISM), 868/915 MHz (ISM), **2.4 GHz (ISM — most common)** - **Typical Data Rate:** 20–250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4) - **Node Lifetime Target:** 1–5 years on 2× AA batteries - **Transmission Range:** 10–100 m (indoor), 100–1000 m (outdoor with high-gain antenna) - **Duty Cycle:** < 1% for energy-constrained deployments - **Node Cost Target:** < 10 USD (academic), < 1 USD (industrial vision) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sensor Node Architecture — Block-Level Decomposition A WSN node is engineered as a layered stack. We will analyze **each layer** with its operational logic, dominant technology, and KTU-relevant details. ### Layer 1 — Sensing Subsystem - **Component:** MEMS sensor + signal conditioning (ADC, amplifier, filter). - **Operating Principle:** Physical quantity (temperature, pressure, acceleration) deflects a microfabricated mechanical structure; the deflection is converted to a capacitance/resistance change, which is then digitized. - **Power Profile:** Microwatts to milliwatts.
