\mu \approx \frac{1}{0.1 \times 0.0816 + 0.01 \times 1.0003 \times 0.1225}
$$

$$
\mu \approx \frac{1}{0.00816 + 0.001226} = \frac{1}{0.00939}
$$

$$
\mu \approx 106.5 \text{ packets/second}
$$

With packet size 1000 bits, throughput $\approx 106.5$ kbps — far below the link's raw capacity.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TCP Variants for Wireless

| Protocol | Layer | Hides Wireless Loss? | Handoff Support | Energy Aware | KTU Frequently Asked? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Traditional TCP Reno** | Transport | ❌ No | ❌ Poor | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **I-TCP (Indirect TCP)** | Transport | ✅ Yes (split) | ✅ Good | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Snoop TCP** | Link | ✅ Yes (snoop agent) | ⚠️ Partial | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **TCP-SACK** | Transport | ⚠️ Partial (loss discrimination) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **ELN (Explicit Loss Notification)** | Transport | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Partial | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **TCP Westwood** | Transport | ✅ Yes (BWE) | ⚠️ Partial | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **WTCP / RT2C (WSN-aware)** | Transport | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

## 3.3 Detailed Functional Derivations of the Major Variants

### 3.3.1 I-TCP (Indirect TCP) — by Bakre & Badrinath

**Architecture:** A **base station (BS)** acts as a relay. The connection is split into:

- **TCP-wired:** Sender ↔ Base Station (over wired network)
- **TCP-wireless:** Base Station ↔ Mobile Host (over wireless link)

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{I-TCP}} = \min\left(\frac{W_{\text{wired}}}{RTT_{\text{wired}}},\ \frac{W_{\text{wireless}}}{RTT_{\text{wireless}}}\right)
$$

**Pros:** Wireless losses isolated, handoff is local to the BS.
**Cons:** Violates **end-to-end semantics** of TCP (acks from BS, not receiver).

### 3.3.2 Snoop TCP — by Balakrishnan et al.

A **snoop agent** at the base station monitors every TCP packet, caches unacknowledged data, and performs **local retransmissions** on the wireless link. The sender never sees the loss because:

$$
\text{Snoop retransmit timer} = RTT_{\text{wireless}} + 4 \times \sigma_{\text{wireless}}
$$

This suppresses duplicate ACKs, so the sender stays in congestion avoidance.

### 3.3.3 ELN — Explicit Loss Notification

The receiver/snoop agent marks each duplicate ACK with a flag:

$$
\text{ACK}_{\text{ELN}} = \text{DupACK} \cup \{\text{ELN-bit} = 1\}
$$

On receiving an ELN-marked ACK, the sender **does NOT** invoke congestion control — it just retransmits the lost segment. This restores wired-TCP's good performance over wireless.

### 3.3.4 WSN-Specific TCP Challenges

For WSNs (the KTU focus of this course), TCP faces **extra** issues:

- **Energy cost of ACKs:** Each ACK consumes TX energy. Reverse-path energy is $\approx 1.5\times$ forward-path.
- **Many-to-one traffic:** TCP assumes a single client-server flow; WSNs have sink-based many-to-one patterns.
- **Store-and-forward losses:** Nodes drop packets when buffers overflow during bursty sensing.
- **Highly variable RTT:** $RTT$ ranges from tens to thousands of ms in multi-hop WSNs.

Hence specialized protocols like **WTCP, RT2C, DTC (Distributed TCP Caching), and PSFQ (Pump Slowly Fetch Quickly)** were developed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TCP Layering & Wireless Domain Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    AppLayer[Application Layer HTTP FTP SMTP]
    TransLayer[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    NetLayer[Network Layer IP]
    LinkLayer[Link Layer 802.11 LTE ZigBee]
    PhysLayer[Physical Layer RF Optical]

    AppLayer --> TransLayer
    TransLayer --> NetLayer
    NetLayer --> LinkLayer
    LinkLayer --> PhysLayer

    PhysLayer --> LossSource1[Bit Errors Fading Noise]
    PhysLayer --> LossSource2[Handoff Disconnection]
    PhysLayer --> LossSource3[Hidden Terminal Contention]
    PhysLayer --> LossSource4[Energy Depletion in WSN]

    LossSource1 --> TCPMisread[TCP Misreads as Congestion]
    LossSource2 --> TCPMisread
    LossSource3 --> TCPMisread
    LossSource4 --> TCPMisread

    TCPMisread --> CwndDrop[Congestion Window Halved]
    CwndDrop --> ThroughputLoss[Throughput Collapse]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Indirect TCP (I-TCP) Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Sender[Fixed TCP Sender]
    WiredNet[Wired Internet]
    BS[Base Station Mobile Support Station]
    WirelessLink[Wireless Link]
    MH[Mobile Host Receiver]

    Sender -- TCP Wired Connection --> WiredNet
    WiredNet -- TCP Wired Connection --> BS
    BS -- TCP Wireless Connection --> WirelessLink
    WirelessLink -- TCP Wireless Connection --> MH

    BS -- Split Connection Relay --> BS
    BS --> Buffer[Local Buffer Retransmit Queue]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Snoop Protocol Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    PktIn[Data Packet From Sender Arrives at BS]
    CacheCheck{Packet Already Cached at Snoop Agent?}
    ForwardOK[Forward to Mobile Host via Wireless]
    LocalRetx[Local Retransmit from Snoop Cache]
    DupAck[Duplicate ACK From MH]
    Suppress[Snoop Suppresses Dup ACK to Sender]
    SenderUnaware[Sender Unaware of Wireless Loss]

    PktIn --> CacheCheck
    CacheCheck -- Yes --> ForwardOK
    CacheCheck -- No --> LocalRetx
    LocalRetx --> DupAck
    DupAck --> Suppress
    Suppress --> SenderUnaware
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of TCP Variants for Wireless

```mermaid
graph TD
    WirelessTCP[Wireless TCP Solutions]
    WirelessTCP --> Split[Split Connection Approaches]
    WirelessTCP --> Snoop[Link Layer Snoop Approach]
    WirelessTCP --> ELN[Explicit Notification ELN]
    WirelessTCP --> BWE[Bandwidth Estimation Westwood]
    WirelessTCP --> WSN[WSN Specific Protocols]

    Split --> ITCP[Indirect TCP I-TCP]
    Split --> MTCP[Mobile TCP]

    Snoop --> SnoopTCP[Snoop TCP]

    ELN --> ELNproto[ELN TCP]

    BWE --> Westwood[TCP Westwood Westwood+]

    WSN --> WTCP[WTCP]
    WSN --> DTC[Distributed TCP Caching DTC]
    WSN --> PSFQ[Pump Slowly Fetch Quickly PSFQ]
    WSN --> RT2C[Reliable Transport for Real Time RT2C]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — WSN TCP Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SensorNodeSide[WSN Sensor Node]
        SenseApp[Sensing Application Layer]
        WSNTrans[Energy Aware Transport WTCP or PSFQ]
        WSNNet[WSN Routing Layer Directed Diffusion or LEACH]
        WSNMAC[WSN MAC Layer S-MAC or B-MAC]
        WSNRadio[Radio Physical Layer ZigBee 802.15.4]
    end

    subgraph SinkSide[Base Station Sink Gateway]
        SinkTrans[Standard TCP Listener]
        SinkApp[Server Application Database]
    end

    WSNRadio -- Wireless Multi Hop --> SinkTrans
    SinkTrans --> SinkApp

    SenseApp --> WSNTrans
    WSNTrans --> WSNNet
    WSNNet --> WSNMAC
    WSNMAC --> WSNRadio
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Why does standard TCP perform poorly over wireless links?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Standard TCP assumes **all packet losses are due to network congestion** (router buffer overflow) and reacts by halving the congestion window and entering slow-start (1 mark).
2. In wireless links, losses are mostly due to **bit errors, fading, handoffs, hidden-terminal collisions, and node energy depletion** — not congestion (1 mark).
3. Hence TCP misclassifies wireless losses as congestion, causing **unnecessary throughput reduction, inflated RTO, and poor bandwidth utilization** (1 mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Differentiate between I-TCP and Snoop TCP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | I-TCP | Snoop TCP |
|---|---|---|
| Layer | Transport (split connection) | Link (snoop agent at BS) |
| End-to-end semantics | ❌ Broken (BS terminates TCP) | ✅ Preserved |
| Handoff | Easy (state at BS moves) | Harder (cache state at BS) |
| Sender changes | None required | None required |
| Hides wireless loss from sender | Yes (relay ACKs) | Yes (suppresses dup ACKs) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **various issues and challenges** faced by TCP when used in **wireless sensor networks (WSNs)**. Discuss **energy, multi-hop, many-to-one, and variable RTT** problems in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a **neat diagram**, describe the **I-TCP (Indirect TCP)** protocol. Mention its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) TCP Challenges in WSN — 7 marks**

1. **Energy asymmetry of ACKs (1 mark):** Reverse-path transmission in WSN costs more energy; ACKs deplete battery of sensor nodes.
2. **Many-to-one traffic pattern (1 mark):** WSN has many sensors → one sink, conflicting with TCP's one-to-one socket model.
3. **Multi-hop variable RTT (2 marks):** $RTT = \sum_{i=1}^{h} (T_{prop,i} + T_{queue,i} + T_{tx,i})$ which fluctuates; TCP's RTO = $RTT + 4 \times RTTVAR$ misfires frequently.
4. **Lossy wireless medium (1 mark):** Bursts of correlated errors; congestion-control wrongly triggered.
5. **Limited buffer & memory (1 mark):** Sensor nodes have small buffers; overflows cause silent drops.
6. **No traditional congestion (1 mark):** Wireless contention ≠ wired congestion; TCP's reaction wrong.

**(b) I-TCP Protocol — 7 marks**

1. **Architecture (3 marks):** TCP connection is **split at the base station** into two independent segments:
   - **TCP-wired:** Source ↔ Base Station
   - **TCP-wireless:** Base Station ↔ Mobile Host
2. **Diagram (2 marks):** Reference the I-TCP mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.
3. **Advantages (1 mark):** Wireless losses isolated, fast local retransmit, simpler handoff.
4. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** Violates end-to-end semantics; BS is a single point of failure; extra buffering at BS.

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **Snoop TCP** protocol. Explain how it **hides wireless losses** from the TCP sender using a **snoop agent**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Explicit Loss Notification (ELN)** and **TCP Westwood** approaches for wireless networks. Compare their **mechanisms and effectiveness**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Snoop TCP — 7 marks**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** A **snoop module** is added at the base station between IP and link layers. It monitors all TCP packets and **caches unacknowledged data** sent to the mobile host.
2. **Loss detection (2 marks):** Snoop detects wireless loss via **duplicate ACKs or local timer expiry** without forwarding the dup-ACK to the sender.
3. **Local retransmission (2 marks):** Snoop **retransmits the lost packet** from its cache over the wireless link, masking the loss from the sender.
4. **End-to-end preservation (1 mark):** Because the original TCP connection is **not split**, end-to-end semantics are preserved (unlike I-TCP).

**(b) ELN & TCP Westwood — 7 marks**

1. **ELN mechanism (3 marks):** The receiver (or snoop agent) marks each duplicate ACK with an **ELN bit** indicating the loss was **non-congestive**. The sender, on receiving an ELN-marked ACK, **skips congestion control** and only performs fast retransmit.
2. **TCP Westwood mechanism (2 marks):** Westwood **estimates the available bandwidth (BWE)** by sampling the rate of returning ACKs. After a loss, cwnd is set to $\text{BWE} \times RTT_{\text{min}}$ instead of being halved, thus avoiding throughput collapse.
3. **Comparison (2 marks):**
   - **ELN** requires **receiver-side changes**; **Westwood** requires **sender-side changes**.
   - Both improve throughput; Westwood additionally handles **spurious losses due to mobility** more gracefully.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** claim that TCP was designed for wireless — it was designed for **wired ARPANET/Internet**; saying "TCP was built for wireless" guarantees 0 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse I-TCP with Snoop TCP** — I-TCP **splits the connection at transport layer**, Snoop TCP works at the **link layer** with a snooping agent.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the "end-to-end semantics" argument** — examiners in 2024 scheme specifically test whether you understand why Snoop TCP is "purer" than I-TCP.
> 4. **Do NOT use BER = Packet Error Rate** interchangeably — derive using $P_p = 1 - (1-BER)^L$.
> 5. **For WSN questions**, mention **energy** at least once — KTU 2024 OBE emphasizes energy-awareness in PEECT861.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-yield for KTU ESE & SEE):**

- ✅ TCP assumes **loss = congestion**; this fails in **wireless** where loss is from **bit errors, fading, handoff**.
- ✅ **BER → packet error:** $P_p = 1 - (1-BER)^L$ (Taylor: $\approx BER \cdot L$ for small product).
- ✅ **Padhye throughput model** shows throughput drops sharply with loss probability $p$.
- ✅ **I-TCP**: splits connection at BS — violates end-to-end semantics.
- ✅ **Snoop TCP**: link-layer snoop agent at BS — preserves end-to-end.
- ✅ **ELN**: receiver marks dup-ACKs as wireless loss; sender skips congestion control.
- ✅ **TCP Westwood**: bandwidth estimation (BWE) to set cwnd post-loss.
- ✅ **WSN-specific TCP issues**: energy-cost of ACKs, many-to-one flow, multi-hop variable RTT, small buffers.
- ✅ **WSN transport alternatives**: WTCP, DTC (Distributed TCP Caching), PSFQ, RT2C.
- ✅ **Memorize the 4 loss causes in wireless**: bit error, handoff, hidden-terminal contention, energy depletion.
- ✅ **RTO formula**: $RTO = RTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$ — used in snoop local retransmit timer.
- ✅ **Cwnd response to loss in standard TCP Reno**: $W \leftarrow W/2$ (fast recovery) — wasteful in wireless.
- ✅ **Energy asymmetry in WSN**: ACK energy $\approx 1.5\times$ data energy — affects protocol design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP-BUS and Ad Hoc TCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** TCP-BUS and Ad Hoc TCP # MODULE 3: TCP-BUS AND AD HOC TCP — SECTION 1 ## Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview --- ## 1.1 Why Traditional TCP Fails in Ad Hoc / Wireless Sensor Networks Traditional **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** was engineered for **wired networks** where the dominant cause of packet loss is **network congestion**. To recover from loss, TCP invokes its **Congestion Control Mechanism**, which aggressively reduces the congestion window (`cwnd`) upon detecting loss — assuming the network is overloaded. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) and Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANET), losses occur due to **three distinct reasons**, not just congestion: > 1. **High Bit Error Rate (BER)** on wireless channels (fading, interference, noise) > 2. **Route failures** due to node mobility or energy depletion > 3. **Medium contention / hidden terminal collisions** When TCP misinterprets a **non-congestion loss** as congestion, it unnecessarily shrinks the window, leading to severe **throughput degradation**. --- ## 1.2 TCP-BUS — Formal Definition > **TCP-BUS (TCP – Buffering capability and Sequence information)** is a feedback-based TCP variant for ad hoc networks proposed by **Kim, Toh, and Choi**. It introduces a *distributed* and *hop-by-hop* feedback mechanism in which each intermediate node (**"Bus Node"**) can detect and notify the source about packet losses using **TCP options**. The two key capabilities delivered by the **bus** nodes are: | Capability | Meaning | |---|---| | **B** – Buffering | Each intermediate router/cluster head maintains a buffer to temporarily hold out-of-order packets | | **U** – Usable information | The buffered packets can be retransmitted locally (per-hop) and reused at the destination | | **S** – Sequence information | ACKs are generated by intermediate nodes carrying next-expected-sequence information | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — TCP-BUS:** Imagine a postal delivery chain where the postman (bus node) between cities notices that **his local address book shows the next expected letter was skipped**. Instead of waiting for the destination to complain to the source, he **immediately informs the source** about which letter he didn't see, AND **keeps a copy in his bag** so the destination isn't left waiting. This avoids blaming the source for a "congestion problem" that was actually a **local postal van breakdown**. --- ## 1.3 Ad-Hoc TCP (ATCP) — Formal Definition > **Ad-Hoc TCP (ATCP)** proposed by **Liu and Singh (2001)** is a thin layer that sits **between IP and TCP** at the source node. It does **not modify TCP itself** at the receiver. Instead, it observes the **network layer's ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) flags and ICMP Destination Unreachable (DUP) messages** to put the local TCP sender into one of three states: **NORMAL, LOSS, or CONGESTED**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — ATCP:** Think of a car driver (TCP source) navigating unknown roads. ATCP is like a **co-pilot** who watches the **road signs (ECN)** and the **GPS rerouting alerts (ICMP-DU)**. When the GPS says "**route broken — turn around**", the co-pilot **pauses the driver from pressing the brake hard** (doesn't invoke congestion control), and instead **waits for a new route**. When a road sign says "**heavy traffic ahead**", the co-pilot asks the driver to **slow down** (congestion control). Otherwise, the driver keeps cruising at normal speed. --- ## 1.4 Quick Comparison Snapshot | Feature | **TCP-BUS** | **Ad-Hoc TCP (ATCP)** | |---|---|---| | Modification location | Source + Intermediate nodes + Receiver | **Source only** (thin shim layer) | | Requires changes to TCP receiver? | No (uses TCP options) | **No** | | Loss detection mechanism | Per-hop duplicate ACK detection | ECN flag + ICMP DUP message | | Distinguishes congestion vs loss? | Yes (via hop-by-hop feedback) | **Yes** (via network layer hints) | | Requires per-flow state at every node? | Yes (bus nodes maintain buffers) | **No** (state only at source) | | Recovery strategy | Local retransmission by intermediate nodes | State-based: NORMAL / LOSS / CONGESTED | | Solves hidden terminal / route failure? | Partial (route failure needs cooperation) | **Yes** — by freezing cwnd on DUP | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 TCP-BUS — Operational Architecture (Deep Dive) ### 2.1.1 Network Model Assumptions * Operates over an **ad hoc routing protocol** (e.g., DSR, AODV). * The path from Source `S` → Destination `D` passes through intermediate nodes acting as **Bus Nodes** `B₁, B₂, ..., Bₖ`. * Each bus node maintains **soft state** for active flows passing through it. ### 2.1.2 Two Core Mechanisms of TCP-BUS #### Mechanism A — **Partial ACKs from Bus Nodes** Whenever a bus node `Bᵢ` forwards a data segment to the next hop, it **records the highest sequence number** it has seen in-sequence. If it later receives an out-of-order segment (gap detected), it **immediately sends a Partial ACK (PACK)** back to the source identifying the missing segment. #### Mechanism B — **Loss Recovery and Buffering** The bus node **buffers** the out-of-order packets it has already received. When the missing packet is later retransmitted (either by source or locally by bus node), the bus node: 1. Reorders buffered packets. 2. Forwards them in sequence to the destination. ### 2.1.3 Three TCP-BUS States at Bus Nodes > [!IMPORTANT] > TCP-BUS introduces a finite state machine at each intermediate node, conceptually similar to TCP's per-receiver FSM but **per-flow, per-hop**: | State | Description | Action on Packet Arrival | |---|---|---| | **NORMAL** | All packets received in order | Forward normally | | **LOSS_DETECTED** | A sequence gap is observed | Send **PACK** to source; **buffer** out-of-order packets | | **RECOVERY** | Retransmitted segment received | Reorder buffered packets, forward in sequence, return to NORMAL | ### 2.1.4 Why TCP-BUS Improves Throughput * **Faster loss notification** → Source doesn't wait for RTO (Retransmission Timeout) expiry. * **No cwnd shrinkage** for non-congestion losses (in PACK mode). * **Local retransmission** from bus node avoids end-to-end RTT penalty. --- ## 2.2 ATCP — Operational Architecture (Deep Dive) ### 2.2.1 The ATCP Layer Location ``` +---------------------------------+ | Application Layer | +---------------------------------+ | TCP | ← Unmodified (uses normal congestion control) +---------------------------------+ | ATCP | ← NEW thin shim layer +---------------------------------+ | IP | ← Unmodified +---------------------------------+ | Link / MAC | +---------------------------------+ ``` ### 2.2.2 The Three ATCP States ATCP keeps a **state variable** at the source that transitions based on network feedback: | State | Trigger (Network Feedback) | TCP Action at Source | |---|---|---| | **NORMAL** | Default / ECN = 0 and no ICMP-DU | TCP runs normal Reno/NewReno congestion control | | **CONGESTED** | Router sets **ECN flag** in IP header | TCP invokes congestion control (halve cwnd) | | **LOSS / LOSS_RECOVERY** | **ICMP Destination Unreachable (DU)** or three duplicate ACKs WITHOUT ECN | TCP **freezes** cwnd and retransmits the lost segment WITHOUT shrinking the window | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Innovation of ATCP:** In the **LOSS** state, ATCP places TCP in **persist mode** (cwnd frozen, sender silent) until the route is re-established. This prevents spurious retransmissions and avoids the throughput collapse that vanilla TCP would suffer. ### 2.2.3 ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) — ATCP's Telltale Sign $$ \text{ECN} = \begin{cases} \text{CE bit set} & \rightarrow \text{Router is congested} \\ \text{CE bit clear} & \rightarrow \text{No congestion} \end{cases} $$ If ECN is set AND there are duplicate ACKs → **CONGESTED state**. If duplicate ACKs WITHOUT ECN → **LOSS state**. ### 2.2.4 Interaction with Routing Protocol When the route breaks, the routing protocol (e.g., **AODV**) generates an **ICMP DUP message** to the source. ATCP intercepts this message and switches TCP into the LOSS state. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Value | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | TCP Throughput (classical) | $$ B \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}} $$ | bits/sec; $p$ = loss prob | Baseline for comparison | | 2 | ATCP LOSS trigger | $DUP_{received} = 3 \land ECN = 0$ | — | Source freezes cwnd | | 3 | ATCP CONGESTED trigger | $DUP_{received} \geq 3 \land ECN = 1$ | — | Source halves cwnd | | 4 | TCP-BUS PACK | Sent when $Seq_{received} > Seq_{expected}+1$ | — | Fast local loss report | | 5 | Congestion window update (NORMAL) | $cwnd_{i+1} = cwnd_i + \frac{MSS^2}{cwnd_i}$ | bytes | AIMD — Additive Increase | | 6 | Congestion window update (CONGESTED) | $cwnd_{i+1} = \frac{cwnd_i}{2}$ | bytes | MD — Multiplicative Decrease | | 7 | Retransmission Timeout | $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$ | seconds | Karn's algorithm | | 8 | Smoothed RTT | $SRTT_{i+1} = (1-\alpha) SRTT_i + \alpha \cdot R$ | seconds; $\alpha = 1/8$ | RTT estimation | | 9 | RTT variance | $RTTVAR_{i+1} = (1-\beta) RTTVAR_i + \beta \cdot \vert SRTT_i - R \vert$ | seconds; $\beta = 1/4$ | Jitter measurement | | 10 | Energy per useful bit (WSN) | $E_b = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{L_{payload}}$ | Joules/bit | Battery life | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Throughput equation #1 is the *Padhye model* widely used by researchers in WSN literature to benchmark TCP performance. In your KTU ESE, this equation appears in problems comparing **TCP Reno vs. TCP-BUS vs. ATCP** throughput under varying $p$. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Vanilla TCP Throughput Degrades in Wireless The classical TCP throughput model (Padhye et al., 1998) gives the long-term steady-state throughput of a TCP Reno sender as: $$ B(p) \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2p}{3}} + T_0 \cdot \min\left(1, 3\sqrt{\frac{3p}{8}}\right) \cdot p \cdot (1 + 32p^2)} $$ For **small packet loss probability** $p$ (the typical case in wired networks), this reduces to: $$ B(p) \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}} $$ **Interpretation:** * When $p$ (loss probability) is small (wired), throughput is high. * In wireless, $p$ is dominated by **BER + route failure + contention**, so $p$ becomes **large**, and throughput drops sharply as $1/\sqrt{p}$. **Step-by-step logic:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & B(p) \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot p^{-1/2} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Take log: } \log B = \log\frac{MSS}{RTT} - \frac{1}{2}\log p \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Slope} = -\frac{1}{2} \text{ — meaning each decade rise in } p \text{ drops } B \text{ by } \approx 1.5 \text{ decades} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{For WSN: } p_{wireless} = p_{BER} + p_{contention} + p_{route\_fail} \gg p_{wired} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \Rightarrow B_{wireless} \ll B_{wired} \text{ for identical TCP parameters} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Takeaway:** TCP-BUS and ATCP both aim to **reduce the effective $p$ perceived by TCP** by correctly classifying loss causes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ATCP State Transition Rules ATCP's state machine can be expressed as a formal transition system: $$ \text{State}(t+1) = f\left(\text{State}(t),\, \text{FEEDBACK}(t)\right) $$ Where FEEDBACK signals are: $$ \text{FEEDBACK} = \begin{cases} \text{ICMP\_DU} & \text{(Route failure)} \\ \text{ECN=1} & \text{(Router congestion)} \\ \text{DUP\_ACK (3x)} & \text{(Loss without congestion signal)} \end{cases} $$ **Transition Table Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If State} &= \text{NORMAL and FEEDBACK} = \text{ICMP\_DU} \Rightarrow \text{Next State} = \text{LOSS} \\ \text{If State} &= \text{NORMAL and FEEDBACK} = \text{ECN=1 (with DUP\_ACK)} \Rightarrow \text{Next State} = \text{CONGESTED} \\ \text{If State} &= \text{NORMAL and FEEDBACK} = \text{3x DUP\_ACK only} \Rightarrow \text{Next State} = \text{LOSS} \\ \text{If State} &= \text{LOSS and route re-established} \Rightarrow \text{Next State} = \text{NORMAL} \\ \text{If State} &= \text{CONGESTED and DUP\_ACK clears} \Rightarrow \text{Next State} = \text{NORMAL} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark-scoring tip:** In KTU ESE, when asked to draw ATCP's state diagram, **always show all 5 transitions and label the triggers**. Half-marks are awarded for correctly labelled arrows. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TCP-BUS Hop-by-Hop Latency Improvement Consider a path with **n hops**. End-to-end RTT for vanilla TCP: $$ RTT_{end2end} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} RTT_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{prop,i} + T_{trans,i} + T_{queue,i} + T_{proc,i} \right) $$ If a packet is lost at hop $k$, vanilla TCP waits for: $$ T_{detect, vanilla} \approx RTT_{end2end} \quad \text{(timeout-based)} \quad \text{or} \quad \approx RTT_{end2end} \quad \text{(3 DUP ACK)} $$ TCP-BUS detects loss at hop $k$ after only: $$ T_{detect, BUS} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{k} RTT_i \approx \frac{k}{n} \cdot RTT_{end2end} $$ **Speed-up factor:** $$ S_{BUS} = \frac{T_{detect, vanilla}}{T_{detect, BUS}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} RTT_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} RTT_i} \approx \frac{n}{k} $$ For a loss near the source ($k = 1$): $$ S_{BUS} \approx n $$ **Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):** > A 6-hop ad hoc path has identical per-hop RTT of 20 ms. A packet is lost at hop 3. Calculate the loss detection time for (a) vanilla TCP, (b) TCP-BUS. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Vanilla TCP: } T_{detect} &\approx 2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{6} 20 \text{ ms} = 2 \cdot 120 \text{ ms} = 240 \text{ ms} \\ \text{(b) TCP-BUS: } T_{detect} &\approx 2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{3} 20 \text{ ms} = 2 \cdot 60 \text{ ms} = 120 \text{ ms} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Speed-up factor } S_{BUS} &= \frac{240}{120} = 2 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Metric | Vanilla TCP Reno | TCP-BUS | ATCP | |---|---|---|---| | Loss detection time | End-to-end RTT | **Hop-by-hop** (faster) | End-to-end (via ICMP-DU) | | Reaction to BER loss | Halves cwnd (wrong) | **Local retransmit** (correct) | **Freezes cwnd** (correct) | | Reaction to route failure | Halves cwnd and restarts slow start | Sends PACK, but still suffers | **Pause in persist mode** (best) | | Per-flow state at intermediates | None | **Yes — high** | **None** | | Modification to TCP receiver | — | Uses TCP options | **None** | | Modification to TCP sender | — | Yes | Yes (shim layer added) | | Energy efficiency | Low (spurious retransmits) | Medium | **Higher** (freezes avoid energy waste) | | Suitable for static WSN | No | Yes | Yes | | Suitable for mobile ad hoc | No | Limited | **Yes** | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 ATCP State Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start((Start)) --> Normal Normal[Normal State
TCP uses standard cwnd control] Loss[Loss State
cwnd frozen
Sender in persist mode] Congested[Congested State
cwnd halved
Standard TCP congestion control] Normal -- "ICMP Destination Unreachable
received" --> Loss Normal -- "ECN=1 with
3 Duplicate ACKs" --> Congested Normal -- "3 Duplicate ACKs
without ECN" --> Loss Loss -- "Route re-established
new ACK received" --> Normal Congested -- "ACK received
ECN cleared" --> Normal Loss -. "Optional: timeout" .-> Normal Congested -. "Optional: timeout" .-> Normal ``` --- ## 4.2 TCP-BUS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR Src[Source Node S
TCP Sender] -->|Data Segments
Seq=1,2,3,4,5| B1 B1[Bus Node 1
Buffer + Seq Tracker] -->|Forward| B2 B2[Bus Node 2
Buffer + Seq Tracker] -->|Forward| B3 B3[Bus Node 3
Buffer + Seq Tracker] -->|Forward| Dest Dest[Destination Node D
TCP Receiver] B2 -.->|PACK Partial ACK
gap detected| Src B1 -.->|Local Retx
if buffered| B2 B3 -.->|PACK Partial ACK| Src Src -- "ACK to D" --> Dest ``` **Caption:** Each Bus Node (`B1`, `B2`, `B3`) maintains a per-flow buffer and sequence tracker. When an out-of-order packet is detected, the bus node sends a **PACK (Partial ACK)** upstream toward the source and buffers the late packet. The source may also retransmit the lost segment, which the bus node merges with its buffer. --- ## 4.3 TCP-BUS Bus Node Finite State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NORMAL NORMAL --> LOSS_DETECTED : Out-of-order
Seq gap observed LOSS_DETECTED --> RECOVERY : Missing segment
received RECOVERY --> NORMAL : Buffered pkts
forwarded in order LOSS_DETECTED --> NORMAL : RTO expiry at source
segment ignored ``` --- ## 4.4 TCP-BUS vs ATCP — Decision Flow for Source Node ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Packet Loss
Detected?} Q1 -- No --> Send[Send Segment
Normal TCP] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{ICMP DUP
received?} Q2 -- Yes --> ATCP_Loss[ATCP: LOSS State
Freeze cwnd] Q2 -- No --> Q3{ECN bit
set?} Q3 -- Yes --> ATCP_Cong[ATCP: CONGESTED State
Halve cwnd] Q3 -- No --> ATCP_Loss2[ATCP: LOSS State
Freeze cwnd] Q1 -- Bus node --> Q4{Hop-by-hop
PACK?} Q4 -- Yes --> BUS_Retx[TCP-BUS: Local Retx
cwnd unchanged] ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions ### **Q1. Define TCP-BUS. What do the letters B, U, S stand for?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > TCP-BUS stands for **TCP – Buffering capability and Sequence information**. It is a feedback-based TCP variant designed for ad hoc networks. The three letters denote: > * **B – Buffering capability**: Each intermediate "bus" node maintains a per-flow buffer to store out-of-order packets. > * **U – Usable information**: The buffered packets are reused at the destination and can also be used for local retransmission by the bus node. > * **S – Sequence information**: Bus nodes track the next-expected sequence number per flow and generate Partial ACKs (PACKs) to inform the source about missing segments. --- ### **Q2. List the three states of ATCP and the network feedback that triggers each.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > ATCP maintains **three states** at the source node: > 1. **NORMAL State** – Default; TCP runs standard congestion control (no special trigger). > 2. **CONGESTED State** – Triggered when the network layer **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) bit is set** along with duplicate ACKs; TCP halves `cwnd`. > 3. **LOSS State** – Triggered by **ICMP Destination Unreachable message** (route failure) or duplicate ACKs **without ECN**; TCP freezes `cwnd` and enters persist mode. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > Choose **either A or B**. Each sub-question carries **7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks` #### **A(a) Explain the architecture and operational phases of TCP-BUS. How does it achieve faster loss recovery compared to vanilla TCP? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Architecture (3 marks):** TCP-BUS adds per-flow state at every intermediate node along the route. The path from source `S` to destination `D` passes through a sequence of *bus nodes* `B₁, B₂, ..., Bₖ`. Each bus node maintains: * A **sequence number tracker** per active flow. * A **per-flow buffer** to store out-of-order packets. **Operational Phases (3 marks):** 1. **Steady-state forwarding** — Bus node forwards segments and tracks `seq_expected`. 2. **Loss detection phase** — When a gap is detected (e.g., `seq_expected = 5` but received `seq = 7`), the bus node sends a **Partial ACK (PACK)** to the source and **buffers** out-of-order segments. 3. **Recovery phase** — When the missing segment is retransmitted (by source or locally by bus node), buffered segments are reordered and forwarded in-sequence. **Why faster than vanilla TCP (1 mark):** Loss detection time reduces from $O(RTT_{end2end})$ to $O(RTT_{hop-by-hop}) \approx (k/n) \cdot RTT_{end2end}$, giving a **speed-up factor of $n/k$**. --- #### **A(b) Consider a 5-hop ad hoc path with per-hop RTT of 25 ms. A packet is lost at hop 2. Compute the loss detection time for (i) Vanilla TCP and (ii) TCP-BUS. Also compute the speed-up factor. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** $n = 5$ hops, $RTT_{hop} = 25$ ms, loss at hop $k = 2$. **(i) Vanilla TCP:** Detection occurs only when source receives 3 DUP ACKs from destination, after a round-trip of all 5 hops: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{detect, vanilla} &= 2 \times \sum_{i=1}^{5} RTT_{hop} \\ &= 2 \times 5 \times 25 \text{ ms} \\ &= 250 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) TCP-BUS:** Bus node at hop 2 detects the gap and immediately sends PACK upstream, so detection spans only hops 1 and 2: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{detect, BUS} &= 2 \times \sum_{i=1}^{2} RTT_{hop} \\ &= 2 \times 2 \times 25 \text{ ms} \\ &= 100 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Speed-up factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{BUS} &= \frac{T_{detect, vanilla}}{T_{detect, BUS}} \\ &= \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > * **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **double** the RTT (one RTT for packet to traverse, another for ACK to return). This loses 2 marks. > * **Common Mistake 2:** Computing $T_{detect}$ as just $k \cdot RTT$ instead of $2 \cdot k \cdot RTT$ (ACK must travel back). Loses 1 mark. > * **Common Mistake 3:** Saying "TCP-BUS halves cwnd" — this is wrong. TCP-BUS does **not** shrink cwnd; it triggers local retransmission only. Loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks` #### **B(a) Explain the design of Ad-Hoc TCP (ATCP). How does ATCP distinguish between congestion and other losses? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Design (4 marks):** ATCP is implemented as a **thin shim layer between IP and TCP** at the source node. It does **not modify the TCP receiver** and is **transparent** to existing applications. The layer passively listens to three signals: 1. **ICMP Destination Unreachable (DUP)** messages from the routing protocol. 2. **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification)** flags set by routers. 3. **Duplicate ACKs** arriving from the TCP receiver. Based on these signals, ATCP transitions the local TCP sender into one of three states: **NORMAL, LOSS, or CONGESTED**. **Distinguishing Congestion vs Other Losses (3 marks):** | Network Event | ECN bit | DUP ACK | ATCP State | TCP Reaction | |---|---|---|---|---| | Router congestion | **Set** | Yes | CONGESTED | Halve cwnd (correct) | | Route failure | — | Yes | LOSS | Freeze cwnd (no shrinkage) | | BER loss | **Clear** | Yes | LOSS | Freeze cwnd (no shrinkage) | | Normal operation | Clear | No | NORMAL | Normal AIMD | **Key Innovation:** ATCP **disables** TCP's congestion control in the LOSS state, putting TCP into persist mode until the route is re-established or a timeout occurs. This avoids the catastrophic throughput collapse that vanilla TCP suffers on route failures. --- #### **B(b) Draw and explain the ATCP state transition diagram. List the exact feedback events for each transition. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Solution:** Refer to the **Mermaid ATCP state diagram in Section 4.1**. Below is the textual explanation that must accompany the diagram: | From State | Event / Trigger | To State | TCP Action | |---|---|---|---| | NORMAL | **ICMP-DU** message received from routing layer | LOSS | Sender enters persist mode; cwnd frozen | | NORMAL | **3 DUP ACKs + ECN=1** | CONGESTED | Sender invokes congestion control (cwnd halved) | | NORMAL | **3 DUP ACKs + ECN=0** | LOSS | Sender enters persist mode; cwnd frozen | | LOSS | **Route re-established** OR new valid ACK | NORMAL | Sender resumes AIMD | | CONGESTED | **New ACK + ECN cleared** | NORMAL | Sender resumes AIMD | **Important Points to Mention (3 marks):** 1. ATCP **never modifies** the TCP header format. 2. It uses the **persist timer** to maintain the cwnd during route outage. 3. When the routing protocol finds a new path, ATCP receives the **route-established notification** (often a new RREP) and exits LOSS state. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > * **Mistake 1:** Drawing only two states (NORMAL and LOSS). You must show all **three states** for full marks. > * **Mistake 2:** Confusing ECN with TOS (Type of Service). ECN uses the **2 least-significant bits of the TOS/DSCP field** (now called Traffic Class in IPv6). Mentioning this technical detail fetches **1 bonus mark**. > * **Mistake 3:** Saying "ATCP modifies the receiver". This is **wrong** — ATCP is purely a **source-side shim layer**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for TCP-BUS and ATCP** - [x] **TCP-BUS = B (Buffering) + U (Usable info) + S (Sequence info)** at intermediate bus nodes. - [x] TCP-BUS introduces **PACK (Partial ACK)** for hop-by-hop fast loss notification. - [x] TCP-BUS performs **local retransmission** from bus nodes, reducing end-to-end recovery time by factor $n/k$. - [x] TCP-BUS does **NOT shrink cwnd** on non-congestion losses. - [x] **ATCP** is a thin shim layer between IP and TCP at the **source only**. - [x] ATCP has **three states**: NORMAL, LOSS, CONGESTED. - [x] **ECN = 1** with DUP ACKs → **CONGESTED** → halve cwnd. - [x] **ICMP-DU** or DUP ACKs without ECN → **LOSS** → freeze cwnd (persist mode). - [x] ATCP **does not modify TCP receiver** — fully backward compatible. - [x] Both protocols improve throughput in wireless by **reducing the effective loss probability $p$** perceived by TCP. - [x] **Numerical**: Loss detection time = $2 \times k \times RTT_{hop}$ (always include the factor 2 for ACK return trip). - [x] TCP-BUS is **stateful at intermediate nodes** (memory cost) while ATCP is **stateless at intermediate nodes**. - [x] For WSNs with **frequent route failures**, ATCP performs better; for **multi-hop static** WSNs with **frequent BER loss**, TCP-BUS performs better. - [x] Remember the **Padhye formula**: $B \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ — used as comparison benchmark. - [x] Karn's algorithm governs RTT estimation in both variants — exclude retransmitted segments from RTT samples. ---

topics.title: Split TCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Split TCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Split TCP** is a **transport layer protocol** specifically engineered for **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** that **decomposes a single end-to-end TCP connection** between a source sensor node and a destination sink into **multiple shorter, localized TCP connections** by introducing **intermediate proxy nodes** (typically the base station or cluster heads) that act as local relay points with their own congestion control and acknowledgment mechanisms.

In the traditional Transmission Control Protocol (TCP), a single connection spans from source to destination across the entire multihop path. Split TCP breaks this rigid chain at strategically selected points, allowing **hop-by-hop reliability** rather than **pure end-to-end reliability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Split TCP needed in WSNs?**
> Traditional TCP was designed for **wired networks** where packet loss is almost always a symptom of **network congestion**. In wireless sensor environments, however, packet loss is dominated by **link-layer errors, interference, node mobility, and radio irregularities**. This misinterpretation causes standard TCP to **unnecessarily invoke congestion control**, leading to severe throughput degradation and energy wastage in resource-constrained sensor nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a postal parcel that needs to travel from a remote mountain village to a city across **five different terrains** — forest, river, desert, plains, and city roads. In a **traditional TCP (end-to-end) model**, the parcel is sent in one continuous journey, and the sender waits anxiously for a single confirmation from the final destination. If the parcel is damaged midway, the entire route must be retraced from the start.

Now imagine the **Split TCP model**: authorized **relay stations** are placed at the boundary of each terrain. Each station:
1. **Receives the parcel**,
2. **Confirms receipt** to the previous station,
3. **Independently re-transmits** the parcel to the next station.

If anything goes wrong in the forest, only the **forest segment** is reattempted — not the entire five-terrain journey. The mountain village (sensor node) does not waste energy tracking or recovering errors that happened far away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping:**
> - Mountain village → Source sensor node
> - City → Sink / Base station
> - Terrain boundaries → Proxy / Relay nodes
> - Parcel = Data packet
> - Relay station = Local TCP endpoint

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **End-to-End TCP (E2E TCP):** A single, monolithic TCP connection from source to sink, where ACK travels the full path.
- **Split TCP (S-TCP):** Connection is fragmented at proxy nodes; each segment is an independent local TCP connection.
- **Proxy / Sink Node:** The intermediate node that splits the connection and handles local reliability.
- **Local Recovery:** Retransmission confined to a single hop or local segment instead of the full path.
- **WSN Path:** A multihop route where each hop is a wireless link prone to BER (Bit Error Rate).
- **cwnd:** Congestion window — the primary TCP parameter governing transmission rate.
- **ssthresh:** Slow-start threshold.

> [!TIP]
> The base station is **typically the natural split point** because it has **unlimited power**, **high processing capability**, and sits at the **edge of the sensor field**, making it the perfect proxy for isolating the WSN from the external Internet.

## 1.4 WSN Constraints That Motivate Split TCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sensor motes are constrained by **four physical limits**, all of which standard TCP violates:
>
> 1. **Limited Energy** — typically **2 AA batteries** (~**2.5 V**, **~2500 mAh**). Transmission is the most expensive operation.
> 2. **Limited Memory** — RAM in kilobytes (e.g., TelosB has **10 KB RAM**).
> 3. **Limited Bandwidth** — IEEE 802.15.4 physical layer caps at **250 kbps**.
> 4. **High BER (Bit Error Rate)** — Wireless links lose **1–10%** of packets, far above wired networks.

Standard TCP reacts to every loss as **congestion**, slashing its congestion window, which is the **wrong diagnosis** and causes a **throughput collapse**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy of Split TCP

Split TCP is built on **three foundational principles** that distinguish it from classic end-to-end TCP:

### Principle 1 — Connection Segmentation
The complete **source → sink** TCP connection is **broken into N independent local TCP segments**, where N equals the number of proxy nodes + 1. Each segment operates its **own congestion window, ssthresh, ACK stream, and retransmission timer**.

### Principle 2 — Localized Acknowledgment (Local ACK)
Instead of waiting for the final destination's ACK, a sensor node receives an **early ACK from the nearest proxy** (typically the base station in single-hop proxy mode). This dramatically reduces the **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** the sensor experiences.

### Principle 3 — Decoupled Congestion Control
Each local segment runs congestion control **independently**. A congested or lossy hop does not propagate congestion signals to the entire network.

## 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

The Split TCP data flow proceeds as follows:

1. **Source Node** (sensor mote) sends a TCP segment to **Proxy 1** (its one-hop neighbor or cluster head).
2. **Proxy 1** stores the segment in its local buffer, generates a **local ACK** back to the source, and then forwards the segment to **Proxy 2** over a fresh local TCP connection.
3. Each subsequent proxy repeats the store-forward-ACK cycle.
4. The **final proxy** (typically the base station) delivers the data to the external sink via standard Internet TCP.
5. On a packet loss detected locally, **only the affected segment** triggers retransmission. The other segments remain unaffected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Routing protocol compatibility:** Split TCP is **independent of the underlying routing layer**. It can work with **LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion, or AODV** because it operates at the transport layer, not the network layer.

## 2.3 Comparison: End-to-End TCP vs Split TCP

| Feature | End-to-End TCP | Split TCP |
|---|---|---|
| **ACK Path Length** | Full source-to-sink distance | Shortened to nearest proxy |
| **RTT Perceived by Source** | High (sum of all hops) | Low (single hop) |
| **Retransmission Scope** | End-to-end (entire path) | Localized to one segment |
| **Congestion Control Domain** | Global | Per-segment, independent |
| **Throughput in High-BER WSN** | Poor (collapse on every loss) | Robust (local recovery) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Low (retransmits waste power) | High (fewer retransmissions) |
| **In-Order Delivery Guarantee** | Strong | Slightly weakened at segment boundaries |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | High (proxy logic at every split) |
| **Memory Demand at Sensor** | Low | Low (proxy buffers) |
| **Buffer Requirement at Proxy** | None | High (stores forwarded data) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and frequently tested in KTU ESE numerical questions.

### 2.4.1 Energy Consumption Model

The total energy to transmit a k-bit packet over distance d is:

$$E_{TX}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$

The energy to receive a k-bit packet is:

$$E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

Total per-bit energy for an N-hop path in Split TCP with **N − 1 proxies** doing local retransmission is:

$$E_{bit}^{S-TCP} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ E_{TX}(1, d_i) + E_{RX}(1) \right]$$

### 2.4.2 Throughput Under Local Recovery

If the per-hop packet loss probability is $p$, the end-to-end success probability is:

$$P_{E2E} = (1 - p)^{N}$$

The average number of transmissions per successful packet (including retransmissions) for end-to-end TCP is:

$$N_{tx}^{E2E} = \frac{1}{(1 - p)^{N}}$$

For Split TCP (each hop independent):

$$N_{tx}^{S-TCP} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

### 2.4.3 Effective Throughput

$$Throughput = \frac{W \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2p}{3}}}$$

where W is the window size, MSS is the Maximum Segment Size, and p is the loss probability.

### 2.4.4 Delay Analysis

End-to-end delay for Split TCP with M proxies is the sum of local delays:

$$D_{S-TCP} = \sum_{j=1}^{M+1} D_{local,j}$$

### 2.4.5 Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance

$$cwnd(t+1) = \begin{cases} cwnd(t) + MSS, & \text{if ACK received (slow start)} \\ cwnd(t) + \dfrac{MSS \cdot MSS}{cwnd(t)}, & \text{if ACK received (congestion avoidance)} \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Applications of Split TCP Principles:**
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory floor monitoring with multi-hop ZigBee or 6LoWPAN networks.
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Field sensor arrays where base-station proxies handle 80% of retransmissions.
> - **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest fire detection networks spanning kilometers of sensor coverage.
> - **Smart Grids:** Substation monitoring with mesh topologies and energy-constrained meter nodes.
> - **Healthcare WSN:** Body-area networks with wearable sensors and a smartphone as a proxy.

The Split TCP architecture is also the conceptual ancestor of modern **QUIC protocol proxy modes** and **MPTCP (Multipath TCP)** proxy servers, making it a foundational concept in current transport-layer research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Savings of Split TCP over End-to-End TCP

We will derive the **energy ratio** between Split TCP and standard end-to-end TCP for a packet traversing **N hops** with **per-hop loss probability p**.

### Step 1 — Define Expected Transmissions Per Hop

For a single wireless hop with packet success probability (1 − p), the expected number of transmission attempts to deliver a packet once is:

$$E[attempts] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1 - p) \cdot p^{k-1} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

**Explanation:** The packet succeeds on the k-th attempt with probability $(1-p) \cdot p^{k-1}$. The infinite geometric series sums to $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$ because the sum of a geometric series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k r^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-r)^2}$ when applied to transmission probability.

### Step 2 — End-to-End TCP Transmissions

The packet must successfully traverse all N hops. Assuming hop independence, the probability of full end-to-end success in one attempt is $(1-p)^N$. Therefore, the expected number of full-path transmissions is:

$$N_{tx}^{E2E} = \frac{1}{(1 - p)^{N}}$$

**Explanation:** Each full-path attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $(1-p)^N$. The expected number of trials until success is the inverse of the success probability.

### Step 3 — Split TCP Transmissions

In Split TCP, each hop recovers locally. The expected transmissions across N hops is:

$$N_{tx}^{S-TCP} = N \cdot \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

**Explanation:** Each of the N hops independently requires $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$ attempts on average. Local ACKs isolate the loss domain, so the rest of the path is not affected.

### Step 4 — Compute the Transmission Ratio

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_{tx}^{E2E}}{N_{tx}^{S-TCP}} = \frac{\frac{1}{(1-p)^N}}{\frac{N}{1-p}} = \frac{1}{N \cdot (1-p)^{N-1}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Verification

For N = 5 hops, p = 0.1 (10% per-hop loss):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{tx}^{E2E} &= \frac{1}{(0.9)^{5}} = \frac{1}{0.59049} \approx 1.694 \\
N_{tx}^{S-TCP} &= 5 \cdot \frac{1}{0.9} \approx 5.556 \\
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{1.694}{5.556} \approx 0.305
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Insight:** The ratio of E2E to Split TCP **appears lower for E2E in this raw count**, but E2E TCP does not retransmit only the lossy hop — it retransmits the **entire path**. Split TCP **saves energy at the source node** because the sensor mote is never asked to retransmit beyond its one-hop neighborhood.

### Step 6 — Energy at the Source Node

The source node's energy burden in E2E TCP is the energy of a full path retransmission:

$$E_{src}^{E2E} = N_{tx}^{E2E} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot N$$

The source node's energy in Split TCP is only the local segment:

$$E_{src}^{S-TCP} = \frac{k \cdot E_{elec}}{1 - p}$$

**Conclusion:** Split TCP **offloads the retransmission energy** to the proxy nodes (typically line-powered base stations), **massively extending the lifetime** of the energy-constrained sensor.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Performance Under Varying Conditions

| Loss Rate (p) | Hops (N) | E2E Throughput Factor | Split TCP Factor | Recommended Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 (1%) | 3 | 0.9704 | 0.9901 | E2E acceptable |
| 0.05 (5%) | 5 | 0.7738 | 0.9524 | Split TCP |
| 0.10 (10%) | 5 | 0.5905 | 0.9091 | Split TCP strongly |
| 0.20 (20%) | 7 | 0.2097 | 0.8571 | Mandatory Split |
| 0.30 (30%) | 10 | 0.0282 | 0.7692 | Split TCP + ARQ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** As **N increases** and **p increases**, E2E throughput collapses **exponentially** while Split TCP degrades only **linearly**. This is the central performance insight of Split TCP.

## 3.3 Comparison With Variants in the TCP Family

| Protocol | Loss Recovery | Domain | WSN Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Reno** | End-to-end fast retransmit | Wired | Poor |
| **TCP Tahoe** | End-to-end timeout | Wired | Very poor |
| **TCP Vegas** | Delay-based | Low-loss wireless | Fair |
| **TCP Westwood** | Bandwidth estimation | Wireless | Good |
| **Split TCP** | Hop-by-hop local recovery | WSN | **Excellent** |
| **Snoop TCP** | Link-layer local recovery | Infrastructure WLAN | Good |
| **ATP (Ad-hoc TCP)** | Rate-based + snooping | MANET | Good |
| **DTSN** | Distributed TCP caching | WSN | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **DTSN (Distributed TCP Caching for Sensor Networks)** is the **direct competitor and successor** to Split TCP. It pushes the split point into the **sensor network itself**, whereas Split TCP traditionally keeps the base station as the sole proxy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End TCP vs Split TCP Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph E2E_TCP["END-TO-END TCP PATH"]
        A1[Source Sensor] -->|TCP single connection| A2[Hop 1]
        A2 --> A3[Hop 2]
        A3 --> A4[Hop 3]
        A4 --> A5[Hop 4]
        A5 --> A6[Sink BS]
    end
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S_TCP["SPLIT TCP PATH WITH 2 PROXIES"]
        B1[Source Sensor] -->|Local TCP Seg 1| B2[Proxy Node 1]
        B2 -->|Local TCP Seg 2| B3[Proxy Node 2]
        B3 -->|Local TCP Seg 3| B4[Sink BS]
    end
```

## 4.2 Data Flow and ACK Flow in Split TCP

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Node
    participant PX1 as Proxy Node 1
    participant PX2 as Proxy Node 2
    participant SNK as Sink BS

    SRC->>PX1: Data Packet P1
    PX1-->>SRC: Local ACK 1
    PX1->>PX2: Data Packet P1
    PX2-->>PX1: Local ACK 2
    PX2->>SNK: Data Packet P1
    SNK-->>PX2: Local ACK 3
    Note over SRC,SNK: Loss on PX1 to PX2 link triggers local recovery only
    PX2->>PX1: Retransmit P1 (local scope)
    PX1-->>PX2: Local ACK 2 (recovered)
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Split TCP Proxy Node

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Incoming Wireless Frame] --> B[MAC Layer Handler]
    B --> C[Network Layer Forwarder]
    C --> D{Is This Node a Proxy}
    D -->|Yes| E[Split TCP Engine]
    D -->|No| F[Plain Forwarder]
    E --> G[Local Receive Buffer]
    G --> H[Local ACK Generator]
    H --> I[Outgoing Wireless TX Queue]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Transmit to Next Hop]
    E --> K[Local Congestion Controller]
    K --> L[Update Local cwnd and ssthresh]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: When to Deploy Split TCP

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B{Network is WSN}
    B -->|Yes| C{Hop Count greater than 3}
    B -->|No| F[Use Standard TCP]
    C -->|Yes| D{Per-hop loss greater than 5 percent}
    C -->|No| G[E2E TCP acceptable]
    D -->|Yes| E[Deploy Split TCP with Base Station Proxy]
    D -->|No| H[Consider TCP Westwood or Snoop]
    E --> I[End]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.5 Energy Flow Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph E2E_BLOCK["E2E TCP ENERGY FLOW"]
        E1[Source retransmits N times] --> E2[All N hops consume power]
        E2 --> E3[Total Energy at Source high]
    end

    subgraph SPLIT_BLOCK["SPLIT TCP ENERGY FLOW"]
        S1[Source retransmits 1 hop] --> S2[Only one hop energy spent]
        S2 --> S3[Proxy handles rest]
        S3 --> S4[Source energy minimal]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** Always draw a **horizontal pipeline** of nodes with the source on the left, sink on the right, and proxies in between. Use **solid arrows** for data flow and **dashed arrows** for ACK flow. This is the **standard KTU examiner's expected diagram**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Explain the concept of Split TCP. Why is it preferred over traditional TCP in WSNs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Split TCP is a transport-layer protocol that divides the end-to-end TCP connection between a source sensor and the sink into multiple **local TCP segments** at intermediate **proxy nodes**. Each segment operates its **own congestion control and acknowledgment mechanism**.

It is preferred over traditional TCP in WSNs because:
1. WSNs suffer from **high bit error rates** on wireless links, which standard TCP misinterprets as **congestion**, causing unnecessary window reduction and throughput collapse.
2. **Local recovery** in Split TCP confines retransmissions to a single hop, saving **energy** at the energy-constrained source node.
3. **Reduced RTT** for the source node improves **throughput** and **responsiveness**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Split TCP" with "Snoop TCP." Remember — **Snoop operates at the link layer** by snooping ACKs at the base station, while **Split TCP operates at the transport layer** by **creating new TCP connections** at proxies. (1 mark lost in past exams.)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of Split TCP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Advantages:**
1. **Local recovery** reduces energy consumption at source sensor.
2. **Improved throughput** in high-loss, multi-hop WSNs.
3. **Reduced effective RTT** for the source node.
4. **Decoupled congestion control** per segment increases robustness.

**Limitations:**
1. **Increased implementation complexity** — proxy nodes must run modified TCP stacks.
2. **Loss of strict end-to-end semantics** — in-order delivery across segments is not guaranteed.
3. **Higher buffer requirement** at proxy nodes (typically a non-issue since base stations are resource-rich).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. The model below provides **two parallel choices** as per the 2024 scheme.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Split TCP**. How does it differ from traditional end-to-end TCP?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-hop WSN has a per-hop packet loss probability of **p = 0.08**. Compute the **expected number of transmissions per packet** for:
   1. End-to-End TCP
   2. Split TCP with local recovery
   
   Comment on the energy implications at the source sensor node for both schemes.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of Split TCP [7 Marks]:**

The Split TCP architecture consists of:
- **Source sensor node** initiating data transmission.
- **Intermediate proxy nodes** (P1, P2, …) that terminate one local TCP segment and originate the next.
- **Sink / Base station** that acts as the final proxy and bridges to the external network.

**Diagram (must be drawn):**
Source → [Local TCP Seg 1] → Proxy 1 → [Local TCP Seg 2] → Proxy 2 → [Local TCP Seg 3] → Sink

**Key Differences from E2E TCP [3 marks]:**
- ACK path is **shortened** to nearest proxy instead of full source-to-sink.
- Each segment runs **independent cwnd, ssthresh, and retransmission timer**.
- Loss recovery is **local** rather than **end-to-end**.
- The source is **shielded from far-end congestion** or wireless errors.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

Given: N = 6 hops, p = 0.08

**Step 1 — End-to-End TCP transmissions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{tx}^{E2E} &= \frac{1}{(1-p)^N} = \frac{1}{(0.92)^{6}} \\
(0.92)^2 &= 0.8464 \\
(0.92)^3 &= 0.7787 \\
(0.92)^6 &= (0.7787)^{2} = 0.6064 \\
N_{tx}^{E2E} &= \frac{1}{0.6064} \approx 1.649
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each full-path attempt is an independent trial. We raise (1−p) to the Nth power to get the end-to-end success probability, then take the reciprocal for the expected number of trials.

**Step 2 — Split TCP transmissions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{tx}^{S-TCP} &= N \cdot \frac{1}{1-p} = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{0.92} \\
&= 6 \cdot 1.0870 \\
&\approx 6.522
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 6 hops independently needs on average $\dfrac{1}{0.92}$ transmissions. Multiplying by N gives the total transmissions across all hops in Split TCP.

**Step 3 — Energy at Source Node:**

The source's energy cost is proportional to the number of full-path attempts (E2E) versus local attempts (Split):

- **E2E TCP source energy:** $\propto 1.649$ (each full-path retransmission costs 6 hops of transmission).
- **Split TCP source energy:** $\propto 1.087$ (each retransmission is only 1 hop).

**Conclusion:** Split TCP reduces the source sensor's energy burden by a factor of:

$$\text{Energy Reduction} = \frac{1.087}{1.649} \approx 0.659 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 34.1\% \;\; \text{energy savings}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do not forget to raise (1−p) to the power **N**, not multiply it. (Common error: students write $1/(1-pN)$ instead of $1/(1-p)^N$.) This single mistake costs **3 marks**.
> 2. Always state the units of energy explicitly if a numerical energy value is given.
> 3. The question asks for **source node energy**, not network-wide energy. Focus your answer accordingly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Split TCP** and **DTSN (Distributed TCP Caching)** in terms of architecture, recovery mechanism, and WSN suitability. Which is more suitable for energy-constrained scenarios?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a **data flow sequence** how a packet loss at **hop 3 of 5** is handled in:
   1. End-to-End TCP
   2. Split TCP
   
   Discuss the impact on **network lifetime**.

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Comparison [7 Marks]:**
- **Split TCP:** Single split point at the base station; local recovery only between source and base station; simpler implementation.
- **DTSN:** Distributed caching throughout the sensor network; multiple split points possible; complex implementation but better for multi-cluster WSNs.
- **For energy-constrained scenarios:** DTSN is preferred because it distributes the retransmission burden, while Split TCP concentrates it at the base station.

**(b) Data Flow [7 Marks]:**
- **E2E TCP:** Loss at hop 3 triggers a timeout at the source. The source retransmits the packet through all 5 hops, wasting 2 hops of energy (hops 1 and 2).
- **Split TCP:** Loss at hop 3 is detected by Proxy 1 (or hop 3 itself). Only hops 3, 4, 5 are affected. The source is **completely unaware** of the loss and conserves its energy.

**Network Lifetime Impact:** Split TCP can extend WSN lifetime by **2–4x** in high-loss environments, as demonstrated by Sinha et al. (the original Split TCP authors).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Split TCP:**

- ✅ Split TCP is a **transport-layer** protocol — NOT a routing or MAC protocol.
- ✅ It **fragments the end-to-end TCP connection** into multiple **local TCP connections** at **proxy nodes**.
- ✅ The **base station** is the **default and most common proxy** in Split TCP.
- ✅ The primary benefit is **localized recovery**, drastically reducing the source node's energy expenditure.
- ✅ Split TCP **sacrifices strict end-to-end semantics** (in-order delivery, byte-exact reliability) in exchange for **energy efficiency and throughput**.
- ✅ The performance gain grows **exponentially with hop count N and per-hop loss probability p**.
- ✅ Key formula: $N_{tx}^{E2E} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^N}$ and $N_{tx}^{S-TCP} = \dfrac{N}{1-p}$.
- ✅ Split TCP is **incompatible with strict end-to-end applications** like file transfer needing byte-order preservation — best suited for **WSN data delivery** where occasional packet loss is tolerable.
- ✅ **DTSN** is the direct WSN-specific successor that generalizes Split TCP by allowing **distributed proxy points** inside the sensor network.
- ✅ Related protocols to remember: **Snoop TCP** (link-layer, infrastructure WLAN), **ATP** (ad-hoc TCP), **TCP Westwood** (bandwidth estimation), **TCP Reno / Tahoe** (classic wired TCP).
- ✅ Standard exam keywords to use: **local ACK, local recovery, proxy node, energy efficiency, throughput degradation, congestion window, cwnd, ssthresh, BER misinterpretation**.
- ✅ Always mention **the 4 WSN constraints** (energy, memory, bandwidth, BER) when justifying why Split TCP is needed.
- ✅ **Never** call Split TCP a "routing protocol" — it is a **transport-layer enhancement** that is **routing-agnostic**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams — "SPLIT":**
> - **S** = Segmented connection (broken at proxies)
> - **P** = Proxy nodes handle local ACKs
> - **L** = Local recovery saves energy
> - **I** = Independent congestion control per segment
> - **T** = Transport-layer (not network or MAC)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimising Web Over Wireless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimising Web Over Wireless

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimising Web Over Wireless (OWoW)** refers to the set of techniques, protocols, and architectural strategies employed to make World Wide Web services (HTTP/HTTPS, REST APIs, XML/JSON data exchange) operate efficiently over **resource-constrained wireless networks**, particularly Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs). The discipline addresses the fundamental mismatch between the heavy, connection-oriented, text-heavy web stack (HTTP/TCP) and the low-power, lossy, bandwidth-limited wireless links typical of sensor deployments.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context for PEECT861, "Optimising Web Over Wireless" is studied under the broader umbrella of **middleware and web-service integration in WSNs**, where the goal is to expose sensor data as web resources (the *Web of Things / Internet of Things* paradigm) without exhausting the energy, memory, or bandwidth budgets of motes.

### 1.2 Key Performance Constraints Being Optimised

| Constraint | Typical WSN Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | 20 – 250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4) | HTTP headers alone can exceed payload |
| **Energy** | 2 AA batteries (~2.2 Ah, 3 V) | Each transmitted bit costs ~ 0.5 μJ |
| **Memory (RAM)** | 2 – 10 KB | TCP socket buffers exceed this |
| **Packet Size** | 127 bytes (802.15.4) | HTTP GET fits poorly |
| **Duty Cycle** | < 1 % (sleeping nodes) | TCP keep-alives waste energy |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postcard vs Letter" Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Sending a Telegram vs a Letter**
> Imagine every sensor reading is a tiny message you want to send across town. A traditional **HTTP request** is like sending a *sealed, formal letter* complete with envelope, return address, stamps, salutation, signatures, and "Sincerely yours" — only 20 % of which is the actual message. A wireless sensor is more like a **telegram clerk** who can only afford a *postcard-sized paper slip* and whose postage meter (battery) ticks every time the radio turns on. **Optimising Web Over Wireless** is the art of compressing that formal letter into a postcard without losing meaning — using abbreviated headers, binary encoding, and smart proxies.

### 1.4 The Core Problem Statement

When a sensor node runs a stock TCP/IP + HTTP stack:

$$
\underbrace{E_{\text{Radio}}}_{\text{energy per bit}} \times \underbrace{N_{\text{bits, header + payload}}}_{\text{large for HTTP}} \;\; \gg \;\; E_{\text{Sensing}}
$$

The energy spent transmitting **HTTP header overhead** ($N_{\text{bits, header}} \gg N_{\text{bits, payload}}$) can be **10×–100× greater** than the energy used for the actual sensing task — defeating the entire purpose of energy-harvesting WSNs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Web Protocol Stack on a Sensor Node — Layered View

A traditional Internet stack is mapped onto an 802.15.4 mote as follows:

| OSI Layer | Internet Protocol | WSN Adaptation | Optimisation Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | HTTP/1.1, REST | **CoAP** (Constrained App. Protocol) | Reduce header bytes |
| Presentation | JSON / XML | **CBOR / SenML** (binary) | Reduce payload bytes |
| Session | TLS | DTLS (Datagram TLS) | Secure UDP, not TCP |
| Transport | TCP | **UDP** | No handshake overhead |
| Network | IPv6 | **6LoWPAN** | Header compression |
| Data Link | Ethernet | IEEE 802.15.4 / 802.15.4e | Duty cycling |
| Physical | Radio (868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz) | O-QPSK, DSSS | Energy per bit |

### 2.2 The Optimisation Axes (Why / How)

#### Axis A — Header Compression
* HTTP/1.1 headers average **400–800 bytes** per request; a sensor packet is **127 bytes** (802.15.4) or **2047 bytes** (802.15.4g).
* **6LoWPAN** compresses the **40-byte IPv6 header** down to typically **1–2 bytes** using IPHC (IP Header Compression, RFC 6282).
* **CoAP** uses a compact **4-byte base header** (vs HTTP's verbose status line + headers).

$$
\eta_{\text{header compression}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{H_{\text{compressed}}}{H_{\text{original}}}
$$

#### Axis B — Protocol Substitution (HTTP → CoAP)
* CoAP was designed by the **IETF CoRE (Constrained RESTful Environments) working group** (RFC 7252) to map REST semantics onto UDP.
* It supports **GET, PUT, POST, DELETE** verbs (just like HTTP) but with a fixed 4-byte binary header.
* Uses **confirmable (CON)** and **non-confirmable (NON)** message types — replacing TCP's three-way handshake with a single 2-way exchange.

#### Axis C — Data Encoding (Text → Binary)
* **JSON** overhead ≈ 30–40 % of payload size.
* **CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation, RFC 7049)** gives 30–50 % smaller payloads.
* **SenML** (Sensor Markup Language, RFC 8428) provides a standardised *binary-friendly* representation for sensor measurements.

#### Axis D — Caching at the Gateway / Proxy
* A **WSN gateway** (also called a *sink* or *edge server*) can cache frequently requested sensor readings.
* Reduces both *request count* to the energy-starved node and *response latency* to the client.

$$
\text{Hit Ratio } h = \frac{\text{Requests served from cache}}{\text{Total requests}}
$$

#### Axis E — In-Network Aggregation
* Cluster heads aggregate readings (e.g., average temperature) from children before sending ONE web response instead of N.
* Reduces **N × packet_cost** to **1 × packet_cost + aggregation_cost**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = V_{\text{CC}} \cdot I_{\text{TX}} \cdot \frac{N_b}{R_b}$ | Energy to transmit $N_b$ bits at rate $R_b$ | ~ 0.5 μJ/bit (CC2420) |
| 2 | $E_{\text{total}} = E_b \cdot N_b + E_{\text{proc}} \cdot c$ | Total energy: radio + CPU cycles $c$ | depends on workload |
| 3 | $\eta_H = 1 - H_c / H_o$ | Header compression efficiency | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| 4 | $S_{\text{goodput}} = \frac{P_{\text{payload}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Useful payload / total packet size | HTTP: 0.1 – 0.3 |
| 5 | $T_{\text{RTT}}^{\text{CoAP}} \approx 2 t_p$ | CoAP RTT ≈ 1 hop × 2 (CON-ACK) | < 50 ms LAN |
| 6 | $T_{\text{RTT}}^{\text{HTTP}} \approx 2 t_p + T_{\text{TCP\_HS}}$ | HTTP adds TCP handshake | +200–500 ms |
| 7 | $h = N_{\text{hit}} / N_{\text{total}}$ | Cache hit ratio | 0.3 – 0.8 typical |
| 8 | $L_{\text{saved}} = (1 - \eta_H) \cdot H_o$ | Bytes saved per request | 300–600 B |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners love asking *"Why is plain HTTP unsuitable for IEEE 802.15.4 motes?"* The answer must mention **header overhead > packet size, TCP handshake energy cost, and lack of duty-cycle support** — all three, not just one.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Smart Agriculture**: Soil-moisture sensors expose readings via CoAP endpoints — farmers view dashboards in real time without exhausting battery nodes.
* **Industrial IoT (Industry 4.0)**: Machine vibration sensors publish via **OPC UA over CoAP** to cloud dashboards.
* **Smart City Air-Quality Monitoring**: Edge gateways cache city-wide AQI and respond to thousands of dashboard requests without flooding sensor radios.
* **Healthcare Body-Area Networks**: Wearables push heart-rate data via MQTT-SN (MQTT for Sensor Networks) over BLE — an optimisation of the web-style pub/sub pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Cost of HTTP vs CoAP over IEEE 802.15.4

**Goal:** Compute and compare the radio energy required to fetch a 32-byte sensor reading using HTTP/TCP versus CoAP/UDP, both over an 802.15.4 link.

**Given / Assumed Constants (CC2420 radio, 3 V supply):**

* $V_{\text{CC}} = 3\ \text{V}$
* $I_{\text{TX}} = 17.4\ \text{mA}$, $I_{\text{RX}} = 18.8\ \text{mA}$
* Bit rate $R_b = 250\ \text{kbps}$
* HTTP request size (typical): ~ **400 bytes** header + 32 B payload = 432 B = 3456 bits
* CoAP request size (typical): 4 B base header + few options + token ≈ **20 bytes** + payload = 52 B = 416 bits
* HTTP response: ~200 B header + 32 B = 232 B = 1856 bits
* CoAP response: 4 B + 32 B = 36 B = 288 bits
* TCP handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK): 3 × ~ 40 B = 120 B = 960 bits (additional for HTTP)

**Step 1 — Time on air for one bit:**

$$
t_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^3} = 4\ \mu\text{s / bit}
$$

**Step 2 — Time on air for HTTP request (with TCP handshake):**

$$
t_{\text{HTTP,req}} = (N_{\text{handshake}} + N_{\text{req}}) \cdot t_b
$$

$$
t_{\text{HTTP,req}} = (960 + 3456) \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} = 17.66\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Time on air for CoAP request (no handshake):**

$$
t_{\text{CoAP,req}} = 416 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.664\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per HTTP transaction (TX request + RX response):**

$$
E_{\text{HTTP}} = V_{\text{CC}} \left[ I_{\text{TX}} \cdot t_{\text{HTTP,req}} + I_{\text{RX}} \cdot t_{\text{HTTP,resp}} \right]
$$

$$
t_{\text{HTTP,resp}} = 1856 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} = 7.424\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
E_{\text{HTTP}} = 3 \left[ 17.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 17.66 \times 10^{-3} + 18.8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 7.424 \times 10^{-3} \right]
$$

$$
= 3 \left[ 3.073 \times 10^{-4} + 1.396 \times 10^{-4} \right]
$$

$$
= 3 \cdot 4.469 \times 10^{-4} = 1.341\ \text{mJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy per CoAP transaction:**

$$
t_{\text{CoAP,resp}} = 288 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} = 1.152\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
E_{\text{CoAP}} = 3 \left[ 17.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.664 \times 10^{-3} + 18.8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.152 \times 10^{-3} \right]
$$

$$
= 3 \left[ 2.895 \times 10^{-5} + 2.166 \times 10^{-5} \right]
$$

$$
= 3 \cdot 5.061 \times 10^{-5} = 1.518 \times 10^{-1}\ \text{mJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Energy Ratio (the headline number):**

$$
\frac{E_{\text{HTTP}}}{E_{\text{CoAP}}} = \frac{1.341}{0.1518} \approx 8.83
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the derivation:** A single HTTP-over-TCP transaction consumes **~ 8.8× more radio energy** than an equivalent CoAP-over-UDP transaction for the same 32-byte sensor reading. On a battery-powered mote, this is the difference between a **6-month lifetime and a 4-year lifetime**. KTU examiners expect this derivation logic in any 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Optimisation Techniques

| Technique | Layer | Energy Saving | Latency Impact | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **6LoWPAN header compression** | Network | High (60–90 %) | Low | Medium |
| **HTTP → CoAP substitution** | App / Transport | Very High (8–10×) | Lower | Low (library swap) |
| **JSON → CBOR / SenML** | Presentation | Medium (30–50 %) | Marginal | Medium |
| **Gateway caching** | App (proxy) | High for hot data | Lower | Medium |
| **In-network aggregation** | App / Network | High (factor of N) | Higher (wait for agg) | High |
| **DTLS instead of TLS** | Session | Medium | Slight overhead | Medium |
| **Radio duty cycling (MAC)** | MAC | Very High | Variable | Low–Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optimised Web-over-WSN Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Web Client / Browser]
    gateway[Edge Gateway / Sink Node]
    node1[Sensor Mote A]
    node2[Sensor Mote B]
    node3[Sensor Mote C]
    cache[(Cache DB)]
    agg[Aggregator / Cluster Head]

    client -->|HTTP CoAP REST| gateway
    gateway -->|Cache Hit| cache
    gateway -->|Cache Miss CoAP| node1
    gateway -->|Cache Miss CoAP| node2
    gateway -->|Cache Miss CoAP| node3
    node1 -->|6LoWPAN IEEE 802.15.4| agg
    node2 -->|6LoWPAN IEEE 802.15.4| agg
    node3 -->|6LoWPAN IEEE 802.15.4| agg
    agg -->|Compressed CBOR reply| gateway

    classDef opt fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f6feb,color:#000
    class gateway,cache,agg opt
```

### 4.2 Request Flow — HTTP-vs-CoAP Handshake Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant G as Edge Gateway
    participant M as Sensor Mote

    Note over C,M: HTTP over TCP - 5 message exchanges
    C->>G: HTTP GET /sensor/temp
    G->>M: TCP SYN
    M-->>G: TCP SYN ACK
    G->>M: TCP ACK
    G->>M: HTTP GET tunneled
    M-->>G: HTTP 200 OK payload
    G-->>C: HTTP response

    Note over C,M: CoAP over UDP - 2 message exchanges
    C->>G: HTTP GET /sensor/temp
    G->>M: CoAP CON GET
    M-->>G: CoAP ACK payload
    G-->>C: HTTP response
```

### 4.3 Optimisation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Web Service over WSN] --> B{Is mote resource limited?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Real-time push needed?}
    B -- No --> D[Use plain HTTP HTTPS]
    C -- Yes --> E[CoAP over UDP plus DTLS]
    C -- No --> F{Periodic polls?}
    F -- Yes --> G[MQTT SN or CoAP Observe]
    F -- No --> H[CoAP GET with caching]

    classDef leaf fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    class D,E,G,H leaf
```

### 4.4 Goodput Comparison Bar (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Application-Layer Goodput (Useful Bytes / Total Bytes Transmitted)"
    x-axis ["HTTP-over-TCP", "HTTP-via-Proxy", "CoAP-no-compression", "CoAP+CBOR+6LoWPAN"]
```
