```mermaid
    y-axis "Goodput Ratio" 0 --> 1.0
    bar [0.18, 0.42, 0.65, 0.88]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Q:** List three reasons why standard HTTP/TCP is inefficient when used directly on IEEE 802.15.4 sensor motes.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Header Overhead:** HTTP headers (400–800 B) and TCP headers (40–60 B) often exceed the 802.15.4 maximum frame size of **127 bytes**, forcing fragmentation and retransmission.
2. **Connection-Oriented Handshake Energy:** TCP's three-way SYN–SYN-ACK–ACK consumes radio energy without carrying any sensor data.
3. **No Native Duty-Cycle Support:** TCP assumes an always-on link; 802.15.4 motes sleep > 99 % of the time, breaking TCP timers and triggering spurious retransmissions that drain batteries.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Q:** Define the role of a **WSN gateway** in optimising web access over wireless sensor networks.

**Model Answer:**
A **WSN gateway** (also called a sink, edge server, or proxy node) bridges the constrained 6LoWPAN/IEEE 802.15.4 sensor world and the traditional IP web. It performs **protocol translation** (HTTP ↔ CoAP), **header compression/decompression**, **response caching**, **data aggregation**, and **security enforcement** (DTLS ↔ TLS). By serving repeated requests from its local cache, it dramatically reduces the number of radio transmissions reaching energy-starved motes, thereby extending network lifetime.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> When answering optimisation questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** for:
> ❌ Confusing *CoAP* with *CoRE* (CoRE is the IETF working group; CoAP is the protocol).
> ❌ Saying "HTTP is too big" without naming **specific byte counts** — examiners want numbers.
> ❌ Forgetting that **6LoWPAN compression** operates at the *network* layer, not the *application* layer.
> ❌ Omitting **DTLS** when discussing security — KTU expects you to mention UDP-friendly security.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **architecture of an optimised web-over-WSN system** with reference to the **6LoWPAN–CoAP–CBOR** stack. Draw a labelled block diagram.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 marks):** The optimised web-over-WSN stack replaces the heavy HTTP/TCP/TLS layers with their lightweight counterparts at every level of the protocol stack while preserving REST semantics.

**Layered Architecture (4 marks):**

| Layer | Optimised Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Application | CoAP (RFC 7252) | 4-byte binary header, REST verbs, UDP-friendly |
| Presentation | CBOR / SenML | 30–50 % smaller than JSON/XML |
| Session / Security | DTLS | TLS-equivalent security over UDP |
| Transport | UDP | No handshake, low overhead |
| Network | 6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, 6282) | IPv6 over 802.15.4 with IPHC compression |
| MAC / PHY | IEEE 802.15.4 / 802.15.4e TSCH | Duty cycling, deterministic |

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram of this notes module.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A sensor mote reports a **32-byte temperature reading** every 10 minutes via the network. Using the energy model from Section 3 of your notes, compute the **per-day energy consumption** for both plain HTTP/TCP and CoAP/UDP transmissions. Assume the constants listed below.

**Given:** $V_{\text{CC}} = 3\ \text{V}$, $I_{\text{TX}} = 17.4\ \text{mA}$, $I_{\text{RX}} = 18.8\ \text{mA}$, $R_b = 250\ \text{kbps}$.

| Quantity | HTTP/TCP | CoAP/UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Total TX bits (req + handshake) | 4416 bits | 416 bits |
| Total RX bits (response) | 1856 bits | 288 bits |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time on air per transaction:**

$$
t_{\text{HTTP,req}} = 4416 \times 4\ \mu\text{s} = 17.66\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
t_{\text{HTTP,resp}} = 1856 \times 4\ \mu\text{s} = 7.42\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
t_{\text{CoAP,req}} = 416 \times 4\ \mu\text{s} = 1.664\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
t_{\text{CoAP,resp}} = 288 \times 4\ \mu\text{s} = 1.152\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per transaction (recomputed from Section 3 derivation):**

$$
E_{\text{HTTP}} \approx 1.341\ \text{mJ}, \qquad E_{\text{CoAP}} \approx 0.152\ \text{mJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of transactions per day:**

$$
N_{\text{day}} = \frac{24 \times 60}{10} = 144\ \text{transactions / day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily energy:**

$$
E_{\text{HTTP, day}} = 144 \times 1.341 = 193.1\ \text{mJ/day}
$$

$$
E_{\text{CoAP, day}} = 144 \times 0.152 = 21.9\ \text{mJ/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Savings:**

$$
\text{Saving} = 1 - \frac{21.9}{193.1} = 88.7\ \%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Switching from HTTP to CoAP saves **~ 88.7 % daily radio energy**, extending battery life from months to years on a 2 × AA cell.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of **caching and in-network aggregation** in optimising web access over wireless sensor networks. Mention at least **two caching replacement policies**.

**Model Solution:**

**Caching at the Gateway (3 marks):**
The edge gateway stores responses from recent CoAP GETs. When a client (or multiple clients) re-requests the same resource, the gateway responds directly without waking the sensor mote.

* *Replacement policies:*
  * **LRU (Least Recently Used):** Discard the entry not accessed for the longest time — best for slowly-changing sensor data.
  * **LFU (Least Frequently Used):** Discard the entry with the fewest accesses — best when one resource is "hotter" than others.
  * **TTL-based (Time-To-Live):** Invalidate after expiry — fits freshness semantics of sensor data.

**In-Network Aggregation (3 marks):**
Cluster heads run aggregation functions (MIN, MAX, AVG, SUM, COUNT) on children's data before emitting a single web response. This reduces traffic by a factor of N (number of children per cluster).

**Quantitative Benefit (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Traffic reduction factor} = 1 - \frac{1}{N}
$$

For $N = 10$ children → **90 % traffic reduction**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **CoAP** and **MQTT-SN** as optimisation protocols for web access over WSNs. Tabulate at least **five comparison points**.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Feature | CoAP (RFC 7252) | MQTT-SN (IBM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Architecture | Request/Response (REST) | Publish/Subscribe (broker-based) |
| 2 | Transport | UDP (or TCP/SMS) | UDP (mostly) |
| 3 | Header Size | 4-byte base | 2-byte fixed header |
| 4 | Message Patterns | CON, NON, ACK, RST | PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE, REGISTER |
| 5 | Discovery | CoAP resource directory | Pre-configured topic IDs |
| 6 | Suitability | Point-to-point & small N | Many-to-many event fan-out |
| 7 | Security | DTLS | DTLS or application layer |
| 8 | KTU exam one-liner | "CoAP = REST for WSN" | "MQTT-SN = pub/sub for WSN" |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** CoAP is preferred when **clients directly poll sensors**; MQTT-SN is preferred when **many subscribers consume sensor events** in a smart-city or industrial-fan-out topology.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Optimising Web Over Wireless**

* ✅ The core problem is **HTTP/TCP overhead vs 802.15.4's tiny 127-byte frames**.
* ✅ The optimised stack is: **CoAP + UDP + 6LoWPAN + CBOR + DTLS**.
* ✅ **6LoWPAN** compresses IPv6 headers to ~ 1–2 bytes; it operates at the **network layer**.
* ✅ **CoAP** (RFC 7252) maps REST verbs (GET/PUT/POST/DELETE) onto UDP with a 4-byte header.
* ✅ **CBOR / SenML** are binary encodings replacing JSON/XML — 30–50 % smaller.
* ✅ **DTLS** provides TLS-equivalent security over UDP (not TCP).
* ✅ **Caching** at the gateway reduces redundant requests to sleeping motes.
* ✅ **In-network aggregation** cuts traffic by factor $1 - 1/N$.
* ✅ Energy calculation formula: $E = V_{\text{CC}} \cdot I \cdot t_b \cdot N_b$.
* ✅ Expected savings when switching HTTP → CoAP: **80–90 % radio energy**.
* ✅ **MQTT-SN** is the pub/sub alternative; **CoAP** is the request/response alternative.
* ✅ The **Edge Gateway** is the *translation + caching + aggregation* hub — know its role cold.
* ✅ Always quote **specific byte counts** in KTU answers: HTTP header 400–800 B, 802.15.4 frame 127 B, CoAP header 4 B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Node Architecture: Hardware Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Node Architecture: Hardware Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Node (WSN node / Mote)** is the fundamental, autonomous, battery-powered embedded device that forms the basic building block of a Wireless Sensor Network. It is a miniaturized, resource-constrained system capable of **sensing** physical phenomena from its environment, **processing** the acquired data locally, and **communicating** the results wirelessly with neighbouring nodes or a central sink (base station).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 4, the **Single Node Architecture** is decomposed into four primary hardware subsystems, augmented by secondary supporting subsystems, each fulfilling a critical, energy-aware role.

### The Four Primary Hardware Subsystems
1. **Sensing Unit** – Comprises one or more physical sensors, an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), and sometimes a signal-conditioning front-end.
2. **Processing Unit** – A microcontroller, microprocessor, DSP, FPGA, or ASIC coupled with on-chip and external memory.
3. **Transceiver Unit** – A short-range radio (typically operating in the ISM bands) capable of half/full-duplex communication.
4. **Power Unit** – A battery (primary or secondary) optionally assisted by an **energy harvester** (solar, vibration, thermal, RF).

### Secondary Supporting Subsystems
- **Location Finding System** – GPS receiver or RSSI/TDOA-based localization hardware.
- **Mobilizer** – Motors/actuators (only on mobile nodes such as Robomote, iRobot Create).
- **Power Generator / Energy Scavenger** – Solar cells, piezoelectric transducers, thermoelectric generators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A sensor node is *not* merely a sensor — it is an integrated **Sense → Process → Communicate** embedded system. The defining constraint is **energy budget** (typically **1 Ah @ 3.3 V ≈ 11.9 kJ** for 2×AA alkaline cells), which dominates every architectural decision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Nervous System

To crystallize the architecture intuitively, consider a single **sensor node as a human fingertip touching a hot surface**:

| Human Body Component | Sensor Node Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Skin receptors** (thermo-receptors, pressure) | Physical sensors (LM35, DHT22, MEMS accelerometer) | Convert physical stimulus → electrical signal |
| **Spinal cord / brainstem** (initial reflex) | Microcontroller (MSP430, ATmega128) | Local processing, threshold decision |
| **Peripheral nerves** | RF transceiver (CC2420 at 2.4 GHz) | Wireless transmission of the "alert" |
| **Heart & lungs** (energy supply) | Battery + Energy Harvester | Power delivery to all subsystems |
| **Spindle fibres** (proprioception) | GPS / Localization unit | Self-position awareness |

Just as the human body spends the *least* energy on processing and the *most* on continuous sensing, in a WSN node the **transceiver (radio) consumes 50–80 %** of the total node energy — a fact that drives nearly all WSN protocol design (duty cycling, MAC optimization, data aggregation).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Examiner Favourite Numbers** (memorize these for numerical questions):
> - **ISM Band Frequencies:** 433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz
> - **Typical Supply Voltage (V):** 3.3 V (modern motes) or 5 V (legacy)
> - **ADC Resolution (N):** 8, 10, 12, 16 bits (most motes use 10–12 bit)
> - **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
> - **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - **Thermal Noise Floor:** $N_0 = k_B T \approx -174$ dBm/Hz at $T = 290$ K

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — "The Big Picture"

The modern sensor node is engineered under four simultaneous constraints, known in the literature as the **"Four S's"** of WSN design:

- **Size** – Physical volume often < 1 cm³ (e.g., Smart Dust).
- **Cost** – Target < $1 per node for massive deployment.
- **Sensing accuracy** – Application-defined SNR threshold.
- **Self-powered lifetime** – Often months to years on a single battery.

## 2.2 Component-Level Analysis

### 2.2.1 Sensing Subsystem
- **Sensor (Transducer):** Converts physical quantity → analog voltage/current/charge.
  - *Passive sensors* require no external power (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric).
  - *Active sensors* require excitation (e.g., MEMS accelerometer, gas sensors).
- **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, linearization.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts continuous-amplitude signal → discrete digital word of **N** bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SNR of an ideal N-bit ADC** is given by: $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. This is the **most asked formula** in KTU WSN papers for ADC questions.

**Quantization step (LSB) size:**
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{ref}^+ - V_{ref}^-}{2^N}$$

**Quantization noise power (uniform, uncorrelated):**
$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### 2.2.2 Processing Subsystem
- **Microcontroller (µC):** Preferred for WSNs (low power, integrated peripherals). Examples: TI MSP430, Atmel ATmega128L, ARM Cortex-M0+.
- **Microprocessor (µP):** Higher performance, higher power (e.g., StrongARM — older motes).
- **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** For on-node FFT/filtering (rare, power-hungry).
- **FPGA/ASIC:** Custom hardware acceleration (e.g., Spec node).
- **Memory Hierarchy:**
  - *Program Flash:* 32–512 KB.
  - *RAM (data):* 4–32 KB.
  - *External Flash:* 1–16 MB (for data logging on TelosB, MicaZ).

### 2.2.3 Transceiver Subsystem
Operates on one of three ISM bands: **433 MHz**, **868/915 MHz**, **2.4 GHz**.
- **Transmit power ($P_{tx}$):** typically $-25$ dBm to $0$ dBm.
- **Receive sensitivity:** typically $-90$ to $-110$ dBm.
- **Data rates:** 20–250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4 OQPSK at 2.4 GHz → 250 kbps).
- **Modulation schemes:** OOK, ASK, FSK, BPSK, OQPSK, DSSS.
- **State machine:** *Transmit | Receive | Idle (Listen) | Sleep*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU-favourite):** The radio is the single largest energy consumer. **Turning the radio OFF** (sleep) is the most effective energy-saving technique — more effective than optimizing CPU clock or sensor sampling.

### 2.2.4 Power Unit
- **Primary cells:** Alkaline AA (2.5–3.0 Ah at 1.5 V), Lithium coin cells (e.g., CR2032: 220 mAh @ 3 V).
- **Secondary cells:** Li-ion, NiMH (for solar-powered motes).
- **Energy density (Wh/kg):**
  - Alkaline: **100 Wh/kg**
  - Lithium: **300 Wh/kg**
  - Solar (irradiance 1 kW/m²): 1 mW/cm² (10 W/m² useful)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC SNR (ideal) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | N-bit resolution → SNR |
| 2 | LSB size | $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^N$ | Volts | Quantization step |
| 3 | Quantization noise variance | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ | V² | Noise budget |
| 4 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Sampling theorem |
| 5 | Total node energy | $E_{node} = E_{sen} + E_{cpu} + E_{tx} + E_{rx} + E_{idle}$ | Joules | Energy budgeting |
| 6 | Avg. power / lifetime | $T = E_{bat} / P_{avg}$ | seconds / days | Battery life |
| 7 | Tx energy (free-space) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ | J/bit | LEACH-style network |
| 8 | Tx energy (multipath) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^4$ | J/bit | Long-range (> d₀) |
| 9 | Cross-over distance | $d_0 = \sqrt{E_{fs}/E_{mp}}$ | metres | FS vs MP regime |
| 10 | Duty cycle | $DC = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$ | ratio (0–1) | Sleep scheduling |
| 11 | Throughput (Shannon) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Link capacity |
| 12 | Path loss (log-distance) | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log(d/d_0)$ | dB | RSSI vs distance |
| 13 | Friis free-space path loss | $PL_{dB} = 20\log(f) + 20\log(d) + 32.44$ | dB (f in MHz, d in km) | RF range calc. |
| 14 | Link budget (max range) | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL - M$ | dBm | Connectivity check |
| 15 | Battery energy | $E_{bat} = V \cdot Q$ | Joules (V × Ah × 3600) | Capacity conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Always convert Ampere-hours to Joules using $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ with $t$ in seconds. KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark each for correct unit conversions.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** MicaZ motes with soil-moisture, leaf-wetness sensors monitor micro-climates; solar harvester extends life to multi-year deployments.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** MEMS accelerometers + TinyOS on Imote2 detect bridge vibrations for months unattended.
- **Medical Body-Area Networks (BAN):** Sub-cm³ nodes (e.g., Smart Dust descendants) ingestible or wearable.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration, temperature, and gas sensors on rotating machinery for predictive maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Quantization Noise and Effective Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A sensor node uses a **12-bit ADC** with full-scale range $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V. The input is a temperature sensor with $f_{max} = 100$ Hz. Compute the (a) LSB size, (b) RMS quantization noise, (c) theoretical SNR, (d) minimum sampling frequency.

**Given:**
- $N = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V
- $f_{max} = 100$ Hz

**Step 1 — LSB size**
$\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \dfrac{3.3}{4096} \\
&= 8.057 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&= 805.7 \; \mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The ADC "rounds" the input to the nearest multiple of $\Delta$ ≈ **805.7 μV**.

**Step 2 — RMS quantization noise (assuming uniform distribution)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{8.057 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4641} \\
\sigma_q &\approx 2.326 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
\sigma_q &\approx 232.6 \; \mu\text{V (RMS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} &= 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 \\
&= 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 \\
&= 72.24 + 1.76 \\
&= 74.0 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Nyquist sampling rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \\
f_s &\geq 2 \cdot 100 \\
f_s &\geq 200 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, choose $f_s = 256$ Hz (next power of 2) for buffer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime from Energy Budget

**Problem Statement:** A TelosB mote is powered by **2 × AA alkaline cells** (each $1.5$ V, $2.5$ Ah). The current measurements are:
- CPU active: $I_{cpu}^{act} = 1.8$ mA
- Radio TX: $I_{tx} = 17.4$ mA
- Radio RX/listen: $I_{rx} = 18.8$ mA
- Sleep: $I_{slp} = 5.1$ μA
- Sensor read: $I_{sen} = 0.5$ mA

Duty cycle: **1 % active** (sensing + TX), **99 % sleep**. Compute the average current and the battery lifetime in days.

**Step 1 — Total battery voltage and capacity**
$V_{bat} = 1.5 \times 2 = 3.0$ V (cells in series, voltage doubles, capacity unchanged)
$Q_{bat} = 2.5$ Ah

**Step 2 — Compute active-mode current**

During the 1 % active period, the radio is transmitting half the time, sensing for the other half (a common assumption):
$I_{active} = 0.5 \cdot I_{tx} + 0.5 \cdot (I_{cpu} + I_{sen})$
$I_{active} = 0.5 \cdot 17.4 + 0.5 \cdot (1.8 + 0.5)$
$I_{active} = 8.7 + 1.15 = 9.85$ mA

**Step 3 — Weighted average current**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= DC \cdot I_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot I_{slp} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.01 \cdot 9.85 \text{ mA} + 0.99 \cdot 5.1 \; \mu\text{A} \\
I_{avg} &= 98.5 \; \mu\text{A} + 5.049 \; \mu\text{A} \\
I_{avg} &= 103.55 \; \mu\text{A} \\
I_{avg} &\approx 0.1036 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery lifetime**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{Q_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ Ah}}{0.1036 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{0.1036 \text{ mA}} \\
T &\approx 24{,}131 \text{ hours} \\
T &\div 24 \approx 1005 \text{ days} \\
T &\div 365 \approx 2.75 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A 1 % duty cycle extends lifetime from days to **years**. This is the *quantitative justification* for MAC-layer duty-cycling protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Budget & Maximum Communication Range

**Problem Statement:** A CC2420 transceiver operates at $f = 2.4$ GHz, $P_{tx} = 0$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, sensitivity $P_{rx,min} = -94$ dBm, fade margin $M = 20$ dB. Find the **maximum outdoor range** (free-space).

**Step 1 — Maximum tolerable path loss**
$P_{rx,min} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL_{max} - M$
$PL_{max} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min} - M$
$PL_{max} = 0 + 2 + 2 - (-94) - 20$
$PL_{max} = 4 + 94 - 20 = 78$ dB

**Step 2 — Apply Friis free-space path loss (in metres)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{dB}(d) &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) - 27.55 \\
78 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) - 27.55 \\
78 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 67.6 - 27.55 \\
78 &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 40.05 \\
20 \log_{10}(d) &= 37.95 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 1.8975 \\
d &\approx 79 \text{ m (free-space outdoor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Indoor (multipath, $n=3$ to 4): range typically **10–30 m**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy Consumption Breakdown

| Subsystem | Symbol | Typical Power (mW) | % of Total (Active) | % of Total (Sleep) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CPU active | $P_{cpu}$ | 5–30 | 10–20 % | — |
| CPU sleep | $P_{cpu,s}$ | 0.003–0.05 | — | 30–60 % |
| Radio TX | $P_{tx}$ | 30–80 | **40–50 %** | — |
| Radio RX | $P_{rx}$ | 30–50 | 20–30 % | — |
| Radio idle (listening) | $P_{idle}$ | 30–50 | **20–40 %** ← *energy sink* | — |
| Radio sleep | $P_{r,s}$ | 0.001–0.05 | — | 30–50 % |
| Sensor | $P_{sen}$ | 1–100 (varies) | 5–15 % | 0 |
| ADC | $P_{adc}$ | 0.5–5 | 1–2 % | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Tip:** *Idle listening* consumes nearly as much as active receive but does no useful work — it is the **#1 target of WSN MAC protocols** (S-MAC, T-MAC, B-MAC, ContikiMAC).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Generic WSN Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[Power Unit - Battery 3V]:::power
    EH[Energy Harvester - Solar Piezo]:::power

    S1[Physical Sensor - Temperature]:::sense
    S2[Physical Sensor - Humidity]:::sense
    S3[Physical Sensor - Accelerometer]:::sense
    SC[Signal Conditioner - Amplify Filter]:::sense
    ADC[ADC - 12 bit]:::sense

    CPU[Microcontroller - MSP430 / ATmega]:::process
    MEM[Memory - Flash + RAM]:::process
    OS[Embedded OS - TinyOS / Contiki]:::process

    TRX[RF Transceiver - CC2420 2.4GHz]:::comm
    ANT[Antenna - Chip or Whip]:::comm

    LOC[GPS / Localization Unit]:::aux
    MOB[Mobilizer - Motor Driver]:::aux

    PS --> PC
    EH --> PC
    PC --> S1
    PC --> S2
    PC --> S3
    PC --> SC
    PC --> ADC
    PC --> CPU
    PC --> TRX
    PC --> LOC
    PC --> MOB

    S1 --> SC
    S2 --> SC
    S3 --> SC
    SC --> ADC
    ADC --> CPU
    CPU --> MEM
    MEM --> CPU
    CPU --> TRX
    TRX --> ANT
    CPU --> LOC
    CPU --> MOB

```

## 4.2 Energy-Consumption State Machine of the Radio Subsystem

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> SLEEP : Wakeup_Pin
    SLEEP --> LISTEN : Start_Rx_Window
    LISTEN --> RECEIVE : Valid_Preamble
    RECEIVE --> LISTEN : End_of_Packet
    LISTEN --> SLEEP : Timeout_No_Packet
    RECEIVE --> TRANSMIT : CSMA_Channel_Busy_or_ACK
    TRANSMIT --> LISTEN : Wait_for_ACK
    TRANSMIT --> SLEEP : No_ACK_Expected
    LISTEN --> SLEEP : Duty_Cycle_Tick
    SLEEP --> OFF : Battery_Critical
    OFF --> [*]

    note right of TRANSMIT
        Highest Power: 50-80 mW
        Duration: microseconds to ms
    end note
    note right of SLEEP
        Lowest Power: 0.001-0.05 mW
        Node spends 90-99 percent here
    end note
    note left of LISTEN
        Energy Sink: 30-50 mW
        No useful work performed
    end note
```

## 4.3 Power-Management Decision Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Event: Timer or Sensor Trigger]:::event --> B{Q_battery > V_critical}:::decision
    B -- No --> Z[Enter Hibernate Mode - Persist to Flash]:::power
    B -- Yes --> C{Energy Harvester Available}:::decision
    C -- Yes --> D[Switch to Harvester Source]:::power
    C -- No --> E[Use Battery Source]:::power
    D --> F[Activate Sensor + ADC]:::sense
    E --> F[Activate Sensor + ADC]:::sense
    F --> G[Process Data on CPU]:::process
    G --> H{Data Significant}:::decision
    H -- No --> I[Return to Sleep]:::power
    H -- Yes --> J[Activate Radio TX]:::comm
    J --> K[Transmit Packet]:::comm
    K --> L[Radio to Sleep]:::power
    L --> I
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**List and briefly explain the four main hardware components of a wireless sensor node.**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary hardware components of a sensor node are:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises physical sensors (transducers) and an ADC. It converts environmental stimuli (temperature, humidity, vibration) into digital samples consumable by the processor.

2. **Processing Unit** — A microcontroller (e.g., MSP430) coupled with memory (Flash + RAM). It executes application code, performs local data aggregation, and controls the other subsystems.

3. **Transceiver Unit** — A short-range RF radio (e.g., CC2420 at 2.4 GHz) that enables wireless communication with neighbouring nodes. It is the **largest energy consumer**, dominating the node's energy budget.

4. **Power Unit** — A battery (primary or secondary) optionally assisted by an energy-harvesting module. It supplies regulated DC power to all subsystems and is the **bottleneck for node lifetime**.

*(3 marks — 0.75 each for identification + brief explanation)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Why is the radio transceiver the most power-hungry component in a WSN node? What design technique mitigates this?**

**Model Answer:**

The radio transceiver is power-hungry because it must remain in **idle/listening mode** to detect incoming packets. Although transmission itself requires 30–80 mW, idle listening consumes nearly the same power (30–50 mW) for **no useful information transfer**, making it the dominant energy sink in long-idle scenarios.

**Mitigation techniques:**
- **Duty cycling** — Sleep for 99 % of the time, wake briefly to check for traffic (used by S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC).
- **Scheduled rendezvous** — Sender and receiver wake at synchronized slots (e.g., S-MAC).
- **Low-power listening (LPL)** — Transmit a long preamble; receivers sample the channel at very low duty cycle (e.g., WiseMAC, ContikiMAC).

*(3 marks — 1.5 for cause, 1.5 for mitigation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Module 4 contains two questions of 14 marks each. You must answer **ONE** (choice is provided).

---

### Q3A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q-Set A] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hardware architecture of a single sensor node**. Identify the four primary subsystems and two optional subsystems. Discuss the role of energy awareness in each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN mote is powered by a **3.0 V, 2.5 Ah** battery. Its average current draw, depending on duty cycle, is tabulated below. Determine the **battery lifetime in days** if the node operates at **1 % duty cycle**, and the **energy budget per day** in Joules. **(7 Marks)**

| Mode | Current |
|---|---|
| Active CPU + sensor + TX | 12 mA |
| Sleep | 6 μA |

**Model Answer (a):**

The single sensor node architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) comprises:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Sensors → Signal Conditioner → ADC. The energy cost is moderate (1–100 mW), incurred only during the brief active window. Energy awareness is achieved by **sensor duty cycling** and **on-demand sampling**.

2. **Processing Unit** — Microcontroller + memory. Energy awareness: **dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)**, peripheral clock gating, sleep modes (LPM1–LPM4 in MSP430).

3. **Transceiver Unit** — RF front-end. Energy awareness: **duty cycling**, **transmit power control**, **modulation choice** (OOK/FSK are 2–5× more efficient than OQPSK at short range).

4. **Power Unit** — Battery + harvester + regulator. Energy awareness: **load shedding** (turn off peripherals), **energy-neutral operation** (harvested energy ≥ consumed).

**Optional subsystems:**
- **Localization (GPS):** Used only for anchor/base-station nodes; ordinary motes use RSSI-based localization to save energy.
- **Mobilizer (motor driver):** Activated only on mobile nodes; otherwise disconnected to save power.

*(7 marks — 2 diagram, 4 description, 1 optional subsystems)*

**Model Answer (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute average current at 1 % duty cycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= DC \cdot I_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot I_{sleep} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.01 \cdot 12 \text{ mA} + 0.99 \cdot 6 \; \mu\text{A} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.12 \text{ mA} + 0.00594 \text{ mA} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.12594 \text{ mA} \\
I_{avg} &\approx 0.126 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Battery lifetime in days**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{hours} &= \dfrac{Q_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{0.126 \text{ mA}} \approx 19{,}841 \text{ h} \\
T_{days} &= \dfrac{19{,}841}{24} \approx 826.7 \text{ days} \approx 2.27 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumed per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{day} &= V_{bat} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t_{day} \\
E_{day} &= 3.0 \text{ V} \cdot 0.126 \text{ mA} \cdot 86400 \text{ s} \\
E_{day} &= 3.0 \cdot 0.126 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 86400 \\
E_{day} &= 32.659 \text{ J/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total available energy in battery**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bat} &= V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat} \cdot 3600 \\
E_{bat} &= 3.0 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 3600 = 27{,}000 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check:** $27{,}000 / 32.659 \approx 826.8$ days ✓

*(7 marks — 1 formula, 2 I_avg calc, 2 lifetime, 2 energy per day with cross-check)*

---

### Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q-Set B] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the role of the **ADC in the sensing unit**. A 10-bit ADC is used with a $V_{ref} = 5$ V input. Compute the **LSB size**, **quantization noise RMS voltage**, and the **theoretical SNR in dB**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **microcontroller vs microprocessor vs DSP vs FPGA** for the processing unit of a WSN node, on the basis of **power consumption, performance, flexibility, and cost**. Which is preferred for a typical WSN application and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

The **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** is the bridge between the analog physical world and the digital processing unit. It performs **sampling** (at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$), **quantization** (mapping continuous amplitude to one of $2^N$ discrete levels), and **encoding** (binary representation of the quantized level). The key trade-off is **resolution (N) vs speed vs power**.

**Given:** $N = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5.0$ V.

**Step 1 — LSB size**
$\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^N = 5.0 / 1024 = 4.8828 \times 10^{-3}$ V = **4.88 mV**

**Step 2 — Quantization noise RMS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q &= \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{4.8828 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} \\
\sigma_q &\approx 1.41 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \\
\sigma_q &\approx 1.41 \text{ mV (RMS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical SNR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 \\
&= 6.02 \cdot 10 + 1.76 \\
&= 60.2 + 1.76 \\
&= 61.96 \text{ dB} \approx 62 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 ADC role, 1 LSB, 2 quant noise, 2 SNR)*

**Model Answer (b):**

| Criterion | Microcontroller (µC) | Microprocessor (µP) | DSP | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power consumption** | **Lowest** (μW–mW) | High (100s of mW) | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Performance** | Low–Medium (1–100 MIPS) | High (GIPS) | High for streaming | Highest (parallelism) |
| **Flexibility** | Programmable (C) | Programmable (C) | Programmable (C/asm) | Hardware (VHDL) |
| **Cost** | **Low ($1–$10)** | Medium ($10–$100) | Medium | High ($50–$1000) |
| **Peripherals** | **Integrated** (ADC, timers, radio I/F) | External | Limited | External |
| **Example** | MSP430, ATmega128 | StrongARM SA-1100 | TMS320C55x | Xilinx Spartan |

**Preferred choice: Microcontroller (µC).**

**Reasons:**
1. **Ultra-low power** — Critical for battery-operated nodes (months–years lifetime).
2. **Integrated peripherals** — Built-in ADC, timers, UART, SPI eliminate external chips, reducing size and power.
3. **Predictable real-time behaviour** — Deterministic interrupt latency suits sensing + MAC scheduling.
4. **Cost-effective** — Sub-$10 unit cost enables large-scale deployment (1000s of motes).
5. **Sufficient performance** — Modern µCs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 at 80 MHz) handle TinyOS/Contiki tasks (sampling, AES, routing) comfortably.

µP is overkill for WSN. DSP and FPGA are reserved for **edge-AI / vision-based WSNs** (e.g., camera motes).

*(7 marks — 4 table, 2 choice + reasons, 1 conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Single Node Architecture" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing sensor node with sensor** — A sensor is *one* component; the *node* is a complete system. Drawing only the sensor loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert Ah → Coulombs/Joules** — Battery energy questions require $E = V \cdot Q \cdot 3600$. Writing $E = V \cdot Q$ without the seconds-conversion loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using wrong base in path loss** — Friis equation: $f$ in **MHz** and $d$ in **km** gives the constant 32.44; using GHz/m gives a different constant. Check units *first*.
> 4. **Mixing up free-space vs multipath energy** — $d^2$ vs $d^4$ — students often write $d^2$ even for $d > d_0$. State the **cross-over distance** explicitly.
> 5. **Not stating all four primary subsystems** in the block diagram — Examiners deduct 1 mark per missing block (sensing, processing, transceiver, power).
> 6. **No mention of energy awareness** — KTU 2024 emphasizes "energy-aware design" in CO statements. Always pair every component with its energy-saving strategy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single Node Hardware Architecture:**

- ☐ A **WSN node** = Sensing + Processing + Transceiver + Power (4 primary blocks), plus optional Localization, Mobilizer, Energy Harvester.
- ☐ The **radio (transceiver)** is the **#1 energy consumer** (50–80 % of total node energy). Idle listening is the silent killer.
- ☐ The **battery is the lifetime bottleneck**; the **CPU is not**. Optimizing the radio yields 10× gains; optimizing the CPU yields ~10 % gains.
- ☐ **Microcontrollers** (MSP430, ATmega) dominate WSN processing because of ultra-low power + integrated peripherals.
- ☐ **ADC resolution formula:** $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB — memorize this verbatim; it appears in 70 % of KTU numericals.
- ☐ **LSB size:** $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^N$. **Quantization noise RMS:** $\Delta / \sqrt{12}$.
- ☐ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Always choose $f_s$ slightly above this for practical filters.
- ☐ **Energy models** (LEACH-style): Free-space $E_{tx} \propto d^2$; Multipath $E_{tx} \propto d^4$. Cross-over at $d_0 = \sqrt{E_{fs}/E_{mp}}$.
- ☐ **Duty cycling** is the single most effective energy-saving technique — from days of lifetime to **years** of lifetime.
- ☐ **Battery energy:** $E = V \cdot Q$ (with $Q$ in Ah and $t = 3600$ s). For 2×AA = **27,000 J** typically.
- ☐ **Energy harvesting** (solar, vibration, thermal) can extend lifetime to **indefinite** (energy-neutral operation).
- ☐ Standard ISM bands: **433/868/915 MHz** and **2.4 GHz** — the 2.4 GHz band gives higher data rate but higher path loss.
- ☐ **Path loss** in free space: $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log f_{MHz} + 20\log d_{km}$.
- ☐ **Block diagrams in exams must show all four subsystems** with arrows showing both **power flow** and **data flow** — examiners explicitly look for bidirectional memory arrows between CPU and memory.
- ☐ Famous commercial mote platforms: **MicaZ, Mica2, TelosB, IRIS, Sun SPOT, Imote2** — mention these in answers for bonus marks.
- ☐ Famous OS: **TinyOS (nesC), Contiki (C), RIOT, OpenWSN** — know at least one.
- ☐ Famous transceivers: **CC1000, CC2420, CC2520, nRF24L01** — know which is IEEE 802.15.4 compliant.
- ☐ Famous processors: **MSP430, ATmega128L, ARM Cortex-M series** — know one in detail.
- ☐ **CODA Reminder:** A WSN node is *energy-constrained, memory-constrained, bandwidth-constrained, and computationally constrained* — always anchor your answer in these four constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT861 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes — Section 1: Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Consumption of a Sensor Node** is defined as the total electrical energy (measured in **Joules [J]** or **milliampere-hours [mAh]**) drawn from the node's finite power source (typically a battery or energy harvester) to perform its four primary operational tasks: **(i) sensing**, **(ii) data processing**, **(iii) wireless communication**, and **(iv) idle/sleep state maintenance**, over a defined operational lifetime **T_l** (usually measured in seconds, hours, or duty cycles).

The first-order energy model for a WSN node is formally expressed as:

$$
E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{process} + E_{comm} + E_{idle}
$$

where each term is the integral of instantaneous power $P(t)$ over the time spent in that state, $E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\,dt$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Marathon Runner's Energy Budget"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a marathon runner (your sensor node) with a small, sealed lunchbox (the battery) that must last 42 km. The runner has four activities: **(1) Looking at the road (sensing)**, **(2) Thinking about the route (processing)**, **(3) Shouting landmarks to a coach via walkie-talkie (transmission)**, and **(4) Standing still to rest (idle)**. Shouting into the walkie-talkie burns far more calories than merely thinking or looking. Similarly, in a WSN node, **wireless transmission and reception are the most energy-hungry operations**, often consuming **10× to 100× more energy** than local processing or sensing.

This is why WSN protocol design focuses obsessively on minimizing the radio's active time.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Typical Battery Energy Density:** ~2500 mAh at **3.0 V** ≈ **27,000 J** (2× AA alkaline).
- **Radio Transmission Power:** **0 dBm to 20 dBm** (1 mW to 100 mW).
- **MCU Active Power:** ~**10–50 mW**.
- **Sleep/Idle Power:** ~**0.01–0.1 mW** (orders of magnitude lower).
- **Sensing Power (typical):** ~**1–10 mW** per transducer.
- **Unit Conversions:** $1\,\text{J} = 1\,\text{W·s} = \frac{1}{3600}\,\text{Wh}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly expects students to (a) identify dominant energy consumers, (b) derive analytical energy models, and (c) justify design choices (e.g., duty cycling) using these models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Functional Subsystems of a Sensor Node

A typical sensor node (e.g., MICAz, TelosB, IRIS) comprises four hardware blocks, each with distinct energy profiles:

| Subsystem | Active State Power | Sleep State Power | Dominant Energy Sink? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Sensing Unit** (ADC + transducer) | 1–10 mW | ~0 mW | No |
| **Processing Unit** (MCU/CPU) | 10–50 mW | < 0.01 mW | Moderate |
| **Radio Transceiver** (Tx/Rx) | 30–100 mW | < 0.001 mW | **YES — Dominant** |
| **Power Supply** (battery + regulator) | Lossy (~85–95% eff.) | Leakage (~µW) | Background drain |

## 2.2 Why is Communication the Energy Bottleneck?

The **First-Order Radio Energy Model** (Heinzelman et al., 2002 — *the* standard WSN reference model) gives the energy to transmit a **k-bit** packet over distance **d** as:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

The energy to **receive** the same packet is:

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

The path-loss exponent **n** depends on the channel:
- **n = 2** → Free-space propagation
- **n = 3 to 4** → Multipath / obstructed (typical outdoor WSN)
- **n = 5 to 6** → Indoor / heavy obstruction

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why'**: Notice that **E_Rx has no distance term** — receiving is purely electronic overhead. Transmitting must additionally **amplify the signal** to overcome path loss, which is why **E_Tx grows polynomially with distance**. This single fact drives the design of multi-hop routing in WSNs.

## 2.3 Sensing and Processing Energy

**Sensing Energy** is largely independent of network topology:

$$
E_{sense} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{sense} \cdot t_{sense}
$$

**Processing Energy** per instruction (CMOS-scaling model):

$$
E_{proc} \approx C \cdot V_{dd}^{2}
$$

where **C** is the switched capacitance and **V_dd** is the supply voltage. This is why **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** is a popular energy-saving technique.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this module.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} + E_{idle}$ | Total energy per cycle | J |
| 2 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{amp} d^{n}$ | Transmission energy | J / bit |
| 3 | $E_{Rx}(k) = k E_{elec}$ | Reception energy | J / bit |
| 4 | $E_{proc} = C \cdot V_{dd}^{2}$ | Per-instruction processing energy | J |
| 5 | $E_{sense} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{sense} \cdot t_{sense}$ | Sensing energy per sample | J |
| 6 | $\eta_{sleep} = \dfrac{E_{sleep}}{E_{total}}$ | Sleep efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{lifetime} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$ | Battery lifetime | s or h |
| 8 | $I_{avg} = \alpha I_{active} + (1-\alpha) I_{sleep}$ | Average current (duty cycle $\alpha$) | A |
| 9 | $P_{Tx} = \dfrac{E_{Tx}}{t_{Tx}}$ | Instantaneous Tx power | W |
| 10 | $d_{cross} = \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{amp}}}$ | Break-even multi-hop distance | m |

> **Notation Note:** In Formula 2, $E_{elec}$ is the per-bit electronics energy (≈ 50 nJ/bit), and $\varepsilon_{amp}$ is the amplifier energy (≈ 10 pJ/bit/m² for n=2, 0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴ for n=4).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Battery Sizing:** Determines physical dimensions of a deployed WSN (critical for IoT, biomedical implants).
- **Protocol Selection:** Justifies protocols like LEACH, PEGASIS that minimize long-distance Tx.
- **Mission Planning:** Predictive energy models enable **lifetime-aware routing** and **energy harvesting duty scheduling** (solar/wind/kinetic).
- **Industry Relevance:** Adopted in ZigBee, BLE-Mesh, LoRaWAN energy budget design, and industrial IoT (IIoT) deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Energy per Operational Cycle

**Problem Setup:** A WSN node performs one complete duty cycle of duration **T** with the following timing breakdown:

- Sensing: $t_{sense}$ seconds at current $I_{sense}$
- Processing: $t_{proc}$ seconds at current $I_{proc}$
- Transmitting: $t_{Tx}$ seconds at current $I_{Tx}$
- Receiving: $t_{Rx}$ seconds at current $I_{Rx}$
- Sleeping: $t_{sleep} = T - (t_{sense}+t_{proc}+t_{Tx}+t_{Rx})$ at current $I_{sleep}$

All currents drawn at supply voltage **V_sup**.

**Step 1 — Express each energy term as Power × Time:**

$$
E_{sense} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{sense} \cdot t_{sense}
$$

$$
E_{proc} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{proc} \cdot t_{proc}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{Tx} \cdot t_{Tx}
$$

$$
E_{Rx} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{Rx} \cdot t_{Rx}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all contributions for total energy in one cycle:**

$$
E_{cycle} = V_{sup} \left[ I_{sense} t_{sense} + I_{proc} t_{proc} + I_{Tx} t_{Tx} + I_{Rx} t_{Rx} + I_{sleep} t_{sleep} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — For N identical cycles (steady-state operation):**

$$
E_{total} = N \cdot E_{cycle} = N \cdot V_{sup} \sum_{i} I_{i} t_{i}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The substitution $E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$ converts electrical quantities to energy. Summing over the duty cycle partition yields the total energy budget. This is the foundation for **lifetime estimation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Break-Even Distance for Multi-Hop Routing

**Problem:** A node can transmit a k-bit packet either **(a) directly** to a sink at distance **D**, or **(b) via a relay** at the midpoint (distance D/2). Find the distance at which both consume equal energy.

**Step 1 — Write direct-transmission energy (single hop, distance D):**

$$
E_{direct} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Write multi-hop energy (2 hops, each D/2). Total Tx = 2 transmissions; total Rx = 1 reception at the relay:**

$$
E_{multi} = 2 \cdot \left[ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{n} \right] + k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the multi-hop expression:**

$$
E_{multi} = 3k \cdot E_{elec} + 2k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot \dfrac{D^{n}}{2^{n}} = 3k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot D^{n} \cdot 2^{1-n}
$$

**Step 4 — Set E_direct = E_multi and solve for D:**

$$
k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{amp} D^{n} = 3k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{amp} D^{n} 2^{1-n}
$$

$$
k \varepsilon_{amp} D^{n} \left(1 - 2^{1-n}\right) = 2k E_{elec}
$$

$$
D^{n} = \dfrac{2 E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{amp} \left(1 - 2^{1-n}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the n-th root to find the break-even distance:**

$$
D_{cross} = \left[ \dfrac{2 E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{amp} \left(1 - 2^{1-n}\right)} \right]^{1/n}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The energy savings of multi-hop arise from the term $\left(1 - 2^{1-n}\right)$ — for **n > 2**, $2^{1-n} < 1$, so multi-hop always wins for large D. This is the mathematical justification for **multi-hop WSN routing**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lifetime Maximization via Duty Cycling

**Problem:** A node's battery has capacity **C_bat** (in Coulombs). The active duty cycle fraction is $\alpha = t_{active}/T$. Find the lifetime.

**Step 1 — Average current consumption:**

$$
I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot I_{active} + (1-\alpha) \cdot I_{sleep}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifetime (in seconds):**

$$
T_{lifetime} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{\alpha I_{active} + (1-\alpha) I_{sleep}}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting case analysis:**

- If $\alpha \to 1$ (always active): $T_{lifetime} \to C_{bat}/I_{active}$
- If $\alpha \to 0$ (almost always asleep): $T_{lifetime} \to C_{bat}/I_{sleep}$ (maximum)

**Step 4 — Lifetime extension ratio:**

$$
\text{Gain} = \dfrac{T_{sleep-dominated}}{T_{always-active}} = \dfrac{I_{active}}{I_{sleep}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Since $I_{sleep} \ll I_{active}$ (often by **3–4 orders of magnitude**), even small $\alpha$ reductions yield **exponential lifetime gains**. This is why **MAC protocols like S-MAC, B-MAC, and IEEE 802.15.4 (beacon-enabled mode with GTS)** implement aggressive duty cycling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Architecture of a Sensor Node

The block diagram below shows the **functional architecture of energy flow** within a typical sensor node, mapping each subsystem to its dominant energy contribution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    battA[Energy Source Battery] --> regA[Voltage Regulator Eff 90 percent]
    regA --> sensA[Sensing Unit Transducer ADC]
    regA --> procA[Processing Unit MCU DSP]
    regA --> radioA[Radio Transceiver Tx Rx]
    sensA -->|E sense| sinkA[Energy Sink Total Budget]
    procA -->|E proc| sinkA
    radioA -->|E Tx E Rx| sinkA
    regA -->|E leakage| sinkA
    battA -->|E self discharge| sinkA
    style battA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b
    style regA fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style sensA fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style procA fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style radioA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style sinkA fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333
```

## 4.2 Energy State Machine — Operating Modes

The state diagram below illustrates the **power state transitions** of a WSN node and the energy expended in each.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> Sense: Timer wake up
    Sense --> Process: ADC sample ready
    Process --> Transmit: Packet built
    Transmit --> Receive: Await ACK
    Receive --> Sleep: ACK received or timeout
    Process --> Sleep: No data to send
    Sense --> Sleep: Sensor read error
    note right of Sleep: E sleep very low uW
    note right of Transmit: E Tx highest mW to W
    note right of Process: E proc moderate mW
    note right of Sense: E sense low mW
```

## 4.3 Analytical Energy Matrix — State vs Subsystem

The following table-formatted block maps the **dominant energy contributor** in each operational state.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sActive[ACTIVE MODE]
        a1[Sensing: 5 percent] --> a2[Processing: 15 percent]
        a2 --> a3[Radio Tx: 60 percent]
        a3 --> a4[Radio Rx: 15 percent]
        a4 --> a5[Regulator loss: 5 percent]
    end
    subgraph sSleep[SLEEP MODE]
        b1[Leakage: 70 percent] --> b2[Sensor standby: 20 percent]
        b2 --> b3[Timer wake: 10 percent]
    end
    sActive --> sSleep
    sSleep --> sActive
    style a3 fill:#ff6666,color:#ffffff
    style a4 fill:#ff9999
    style b1 fill:#ffcc66
```

> **Reading the diagram:** In **active mode**, the radio transmitter (red) is the dominant sink at ~60% of energy. In **sleep mode**, leakage currents dominate, hence the importance of low-leakage CMOS design and sleep-mode current benchmarks (target: < 1 µA).

# 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**(3 Marks)** *Identify the four major energy-consuming subsystems of a wireless sensor node and state which one typically dominates the energy budget.*

**Model Answer:**

A wireless sensor node's energy budget is distributed across four major subsystems:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises the physical transducer (e.g., temperature, accelerometer) and the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). Power consumption is in the range of **1–10 mW**.

2. **Processing Unit** — The microcontroller or DSP that runs the OS, MAC protocol, and routing logic. Active power is typically **10–50 mW**, but it falls to < **0.01 mW** in sleep mode.

3. **Communication (Radio Transceiver)** — Responsible for wireless Tx and Rx. This is the **dominant energy consumer**, accounting for **60–80%** of the total energy budget in typical deployments.

4. **Power Supply Subsystem** — Includes voltage regulators and battery management. Incurs conversion losses (5–15%) and leakage (µW level).

**Conclusion:** The **radio transceiver** dominates the energy budget, which is why WSN protocols aggressively minimize radio-on time through duty cycling.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(3 Marks)** *Why is wireless communication considered the most energy-expensive operation in a WSN node? Justify with the first-order radio energy model.*

**Model Answer:**

Wireless communication is the most energy-expensive because the **First-Order Radio Energy Model** shows transmission energy grows **polynomially with distance d** (path loss exponent n ≥ 2), while sensing and processing scale only with the data volume:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}
$$

For an outdoor WSN with n = 4, doubling the transmission distance **increases Tx energy by 16×**. Additionally, the radio must remain powered during both **transmission and reception** (each costing ≥ 30 mW), whereas the MCU can be clock-gated to < 0.01 mW when idle. This combined effect makes the radio the bottleneck, motivating protocols like LEACH, multi-hop routing, and duty cycling.

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice: Answer ONE)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 4 generally carries a 14-mark question with sub-parts. You are given an **internal choice** (Q11 OR Q12). Both options are below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**A sensor node transmits a 2000-bit packet over a distance of 80 m using the first-order radio model. The parameters are: $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², and path loss exponent n = 2.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the energy consumed in **transmission** and **reception** of this single packet. Compare the two values.

**(b) [7 Marks)** If the same data is sent via **two equal hops** (each 40 m) with one intermediate relay, calculate the **total energy** (Tx + Rx at relay + Tx) and show that multi-hop is more efficient.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $k = 2000$ bits, $d = 80$ m, $n = 2$
- $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J/bit}$
- $\varepsilon_{amp} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2 = 10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J/bit/m}^2$

**Step 2 — Compute transmission energy:**

$$
E_{Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (80)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 6400
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.28 \times 10^{-4} = 2.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute reception energy:**

$$
E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$
E_{Tx} = 228\,\mu\text{J}, \quad E_{Rx} = 100\,\mu\text{J}, \quad \dfrac{E_{Tx}}{E_{Rx}} = 2.28
$$

> Transmission consumes **2.28× more energy** than reception, solely due to the amplifier's distance-dependent term.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Multi-hop topology:** Source → Relay (40 m) → Sink (40 m).

**Step 2 — Energy for first hop (Source → Relay):**

$$
E_{Tx1} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 2000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (40)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 3.2 \times 10^{-5} = 1.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy for relay reception:**

$$
E_{Rx,relay} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy for second hop (Relay → Sink):** By symmetry, $E_{Tx2} = 1.32 \times 10^{-4}$ J.

**Step 5 — Total multi-hop energy:**

$$
E_{multi} = E_{Tx1} + E_{Rx,relay} + E_{Tx2}
$$

$$
E_{multi} = 1.32 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.32 \times 10^{-4} = 3.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with direct transmission:**

$$
E_{direct} = E_{Tx}(80\,\text{m}) = 2.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Surprise Result:** $E_{multi} = 3.64 \times 10^{-4}$ J > $E_{direct} = 2.28 \times 10^{-4}$ J. **Multi-hop is NOT more energy-efficient for n = 2 at this distance!**

**Step 7 — Re-evaluation using break-even distance formula:**

$$
D_{cross} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{amp}(1 - 2^{1-n})} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12} \times (1 - 0.5)} } = \sqrt{ 20000 } \approx 141.4 \text{ m}
$$

Since D = 80 m < 141.4 m, **direct transmission is still more energy-efficient**. Multi-hop wins only beyond the break-even distance.

> **Conclusion:** For free-space (n=2), multi-hop is beneficial only for long distances. For n ≥ 3, multi-hop is almost always superior.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Alternative]**

**A WSN node operates on a duty cycle with the following parameters: Active current $I_{active} = 20$ mA, Sleep current $I_{sleep} = 5$ µA, Supply voltage $V_{sup} = 3.0$ V, Active time per cycle $t_{active} = 50$ ms, Total cycle period $T = 1$ s, Battery capacity $C_{bat} = 1000$ mAh.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **average current consumption** and the **total energy consumed per day**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the sleep current is reduced to **1 µA** (by using a low-leakage MCU), what is the **percentage increase in battery lifetime**?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute duty cycle ratio:**

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{t_{active}}{T} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Average current (general duty cycle formula):**

$$
I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot I_{active} + (1-\alpha) \cdot I_{sleep}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.05 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} + 0.95 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 4.75 \times 10^{-6} = 1.00475 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \approx 1.005 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Average power consumption:**

$$
P_{avg} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{avg} = 3.0 \times 1.005 \times 10^{-3} = 3.015 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per day (86400 seconds):**

$$
E_{day} = P_{avg} \times 86400 = 3.015 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400
$$

$$
E_{day} = 260.5 \text{ J}
$$

> **Step 5 — Verification via direct time integration:**
>
> $$
> E_{day} = V_{sup} \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{active,total} + V_{sup} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep,total}
> $$
>
> $$
> E_{day} = 3.0 \times 0.020 \times 4320 + 3.0 \times 0.000005 \times 82080
> $$
>
> $$
> E_{day} = 259.2 + 1.23 = 260.4 \text{ J} \checkmark
> $$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — New average current with $I_{sleep}^{new} = 1$ µA:**

$$
I_{avg}^{new} = 0.05 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} + 0.95 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
I_{avg}^{new} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 9.5 \times 10^{-7} = 1.00095 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Original lifetime (in hours):**

$$
T_{old} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{1.00475 \text{ mA}} \approx 995.3 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — New lifetime:**

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{1.00095 \text{ mA}} \approx 999.05 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage increase:**

$$
\Delta T \% = \dfrac{T_{new} - T_{old}}{T_{old}} \times 100 = \dfrac{999.05 - 995.3}{995.3} \times 100 \approx 0.38\%
$$

> **Insight:** Reducing sleep current from 5 µA to 1 µA gives only ~0.4% lifetime gain when the duty cycle is small (α = 0.05). This is because **the active current dominates**. The lesson: **reducing $I_{active}$ or $\alpha$ matters more than reducing $I_{sleep}$** in low-duty-cycle designs. This is a classic KTU trap question.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster:** Mixing nJ, µJ, and mJ without conversion. Always normalize to **Joules (J)** before addition.
> 2. **Forgetting the Receiver's $E_{elec}$:** In multi-hop problems, students often compute only Tx energy and miss the relay's reception cost ($k \cdot E_{elec}$). KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this.
> 3. **Using n = 2 universally:** Path loss exponent depends on the **environment**, not a default. Always read problem statements carefully.
> 4. **Duty cycle vs. duty ratio confusion:** $\alpha$ is a **fraction**, not a percentage. Writing $\alpha = 5$ instead of 0.05 will produce a **negative lifetime** — an instant zero.
> 5. **In Part (b) of Question B**, students often conclude that lowering $I_{sleep}$ "greatly" extends lifetime. The correct conclusion — that the **active mode dominates** when $\alpha$ is small — is what earns full marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **The 4-equation energy model:** $E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} + E_{idle}$ — memorize the structure.
- [x] **The radio is king:** Wireless communication (Tx + Rx) consumes **60–80%** of node energy; always check this first in design questions.
- [x] **First-Order Radio Model formulas** ($E_{Tx}$, $E_{Rx}$, path-loss exponent) — must be reproduced from memory in KTU exams.
- [x] **Path loss exponent n = 2 (free space), 3–4 (outdoor multipath), 5–6 (indoor obstructed).**
- [x] **Multi-hop break-even distance:** Use $D_{cross}$ to decide between direct and relayed transmission.
- [x] **Duty cycling gains:** Lifetime $\propto 1/I_{avg}$, and $I_{avg}$ is a weighted sum of active and sleep currents.
- [x] **DVS principle:** $E_{proc} \propto V_{dd}^{2}$ — small voltage reductions yield quadratic energy savings.
- [x] **Sleep current is NOT always the bottleneck:** In low-$\alpha$ designs, active mode dominates. Always analyze $I_{avg}$ first.
- [x] **Common units to remember:** J, mJ, µJ, nJ for energy; mW, µW for power; mAh, Coulombs for battery capacity.
- [x] **Energy harvesting implications:** When $E_{harvest} \geq E_{total}/T$, the node can be **energy-neutral** — a key emerging concept in the 2024 syllabus.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme prioritizes **numerical problem-solving** (CO3, CO4 mapped) and **design justification** (CO5). Practice at least 3 numericals on the first-order radio model and 2 on duty-cycle lifetime — these are the highest-weightage items in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Low Power Wireless Sensor Networks (LP-WSNs)** are a specialized class of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) engineered with the primary design constraint of **minimizing energy consumption** at every layer of the communication stack, thereby maximizing the operational lifetime of battery-powered or energy-harvesting sensor nodes deployed in remote, hostile, or inaccessible environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Low-Power WSN* refers to a network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that collaboratively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, pressure, or pollutants) while employing energy-efficient hardware, communication protocols, sleep/wake scheduling, and energy harvesting techniques to sustain operation for durations ranging from several months to several years on a single battery charge or harvested energy source.

## 1.2 Key Design Pillars of Low-Power WSNs

The fundamental design pillars that distinguish a low-power WSN from a conventional wireless network are:

- **Energy-Efficient Hardware Design** – Use of low-power transceivers, microcontrollers, and sensors.
- **Duty Cycling** – Keeping the radio in sleep mode for the majority of the time.
- **Power-Aware Protocols** – MAC, routing, and transport protocols designed to conserve energy.
- **In-Network Processing** – Performing computation locally to reduce transmission load.
- **Energy Harvesting** – Scavenging ambient energy from the environment.
- **Data Aggregation** – Combining data from multiple nodes to reduce redundant transmissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy is the Most Scarce Resource in a WSN.** In many deployment scenarios (e.g., deep forest monitoring, underwater pipelines, battlefield surveillance), battery replacement is either prohibitively expensive or physically impossible. Thus, the entire network architecture is designed around the axiom: *every microjoule (µJ) matters.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "The Night Watchman of a Sleeping Village"**

Imagine a small village where every house has a security guard (sensor node). To save electricity, the village operates under a strict rule: **each guard is allowed to stay awake and patrol for only 5 minutes every hour** (duty cycle = ~8.3%). The rest of the time, the guard sleeps in a low-power resting mode.

When an unusual event occurs (say, a stranger enters), the awake guard quickly reports to the central watchtower (base station/sink). To avoid all 100 guards reporting the same event (which would waste battery), a pre-agreed hierarchy dictates that **only one designated guard per region reports**, while the others suppress their redundant reports (in-network aggregation).

The village also has a small **solar panel on the roof** (energy harvesting) that slowly recharges the guard's flashlight battery during the day. This is precisely how a Low-Power WSN operates — **intermittent wakefulness, hierarchical reporting, and ambient energy replenishment**.

## 1.4 Sources of Power Consumption in a Sensor Node

A typical sensor node (Mote architecture) consumes energy from four major subsystems:

| Subsystem | Typical Power Draw | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** | 10 – 30 mW (active), < 1 µW (sleep) | Sleep modes (LPM1–LPM4) |
| **Radio Transceiver** | 20 – 100 mW (TX/RX), < 1 µW (sleep) | Duty cycling, short-range transmission |
| **Sensors** | 1 – 10 mW per active sensor | Sensor scheduling, duty cycling |
| **Memory/Storage** | < 5 mW | Low-power SRAM/Flash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Fact:** The **radio transceiver is the single largest power consumer** during active periods, often accounting for **60% to 80%** of the total node energy budget. Therefore, almost all low-power techniques target the radio.

## 1.5 Why Conventional Wireless Networks Fail for WSNs

- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Designed for high throughput, continuous connectivity → drains battery in hours.
- **Cellular (4G/5G):** Designed for mobile voice/data, requires powerful base stations → too power-hungry.
- **Bluetooth Classic:** Designed for cable replacement, continuous streaming → unsuitable for years-long deployment.
- **WSNs Need:** Sporadic, low-bandwidth, delay-tolerant, multi-hop communication with **battery lifetimes measured in months-to-years**.

This gap gave birth to dedicated low-power standards such as **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee), Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), LoRa, and NB-IoT**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Energy Consumption Analysis

The energy consumption in a WSN can be analyzed at every layer of the protocol stack. Each layer presents unique opportunities for energy optimization.

### 2.1.1 Physical Layer (Energy in Radio Communication)

The dominant cost in the physical layer is **radio transmission and reception**. The most widely accepted analytical model is the **First Order Radio Model** proposed by Heinzelman et al. (2000).

#### Energy to Transmit a k-bit packet over distance d:
$$E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{TX\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{TX\text{-}amp}(k,d)$$

#### Energy to Receive a k-bit packet:
$$E_{RX}(k) = E_{RX\text{-}elec}(k)$$

Where the parameters are defined in the table below.

### 2.1.2 MAC Layer (Idle Listening & Collisions)

The MAC layer is the **second-largest energy drain** due to:
- **Idle Listening** – Radio is ON waiting for packets, consuming power without useful work.
- **Collisions** – Retransmissions waste energy.
- **Overhearing** – Node receives packets not destined for it.
- **Control Packet Overhead** – RTS/CTS/ACK frames consume energy.

**Duty Cycling** is the primary solution: keep the radio OFF for a fraction of the time.

### 2.1.3 Network Layer (Routing Energy Cost)

Routing decisions directly impact energy. Multi-hop routing can save transmission energy but introduces relay overhead. Common energy-aware metrics include:
- **Minimum Energy Path** – Route through nodes with lowest transmission cost.
- **Maximum Lifetime Path** – Avoid nodes with low residual energy.

### 2.1.4 Application Layer (Data Aggregation)

Raw sensor data from all nodes is often redundant. In-network aggregation at intermediate nodes reduces the number of bits transmitted, saving energy proportionally.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-essential** for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE on PEECT861. Memorize the parameters and their typical values.

| Formula Name | Equation | Parameters & Units | Typical Values |
|--------------|----------|--------------------|----------------|
| Transmit Energy (Free Space) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ | $E_{elec}$: Electronics energy (J/bit), $\epsilon_{fs}$: Free-space amp factor (J/bit/m²) | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² |
| Transmit Energy (Multipath) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$ | $\epsilon_{mp}$: Multipath amp factor (J/bit/m⁴) | $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| Receive Energy | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | $E_{elec}$: Same as transmit electronics | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit |
| Crossover Distance | $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$ | Threshold distance between FS and MP models | $d_{crossover} \approx 87$ m |
| Duty Cycle | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | $T_{active}$: Radio ON time (s), $T_{sleep}$: Sleep time (s) | $DC$ typically 0.1% – 5% |
| Network Lifetime | $L = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{consumed\_per\_round}}$ | $E_{initial}$: Initial battery energy (J), $E_{round}$: Energy per round (J) | Varies by topology |
| Energy Harvested | $E_{harv}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} P_{harv}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | $P_{harv}$: Harvested power (W) | Solar: 10–100 mW/cm² |
| Optimal Cluster Heads (LEACH) | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | $N$: nodes, $M$: cluster side, $d_{toBS}$: dist to BS | $k_{opt}$ typically 5% of $N$ |
| Energy per Bit (Shannon) | $E_{b}/N_{0} \geq -1.6$ dB | Minimum SNR per bit for reliable comm. | Fundamental limit |
| Battery Capacity | $C = I \cdot t$ (Ah) | $I$: current (A), $t$: time (h) | 2x AA = ~2500 mAh |
| Sleep Current vs Active | $I_{ratio} = I_{active}/I_{sleep}$ | Ratio of active to sleep current | 1000:1 typical |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Low-power WSN design principles have transformed numerous industries:

- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture and climate sensors operate for **5–10 years** on a single battery in remote farms.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Bridges, dams, and buildings embed vibration/tilt sensors that harvest energy from structural strain.
- **Wildlife Tracking:** GPS-collared animals transmit location data using solar-harvested motes.
- **Healthcare Body Area Networks (BAN):** Wearable ECG/temperature patches use BLE to transmit for weeks on a coin cell.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Factory floor sensors monitor equipment health, predicting failures while operating on harvested vibration energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Triangle:** Low-Power WSN design is governed by the **Energy-Latency-Reliability** triangle. You can optimize two of these three, but never all three simultaneously. For example, aggressive duty cycling saves energy but increases latency. KTU questions often test this trade-off understanding.

## 2.4 First Order Radio Model — Deep Dive

The First Order Radio Model (also called the **Heinzelman Model**) is the foundational mathematical model used in nearly all WSN energy analysis. It assumes:

1. The radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ J/bit to run the transmitter or receiver circuitry.
2. The transmit amplifier dissipates either $\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ (free space) or $\epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ (multipath) J/bit/m² or J/bit/m⁴ respectively.
3. The choice of model depends on the **crossover distance** $d_{crossover}$.
4. The receiver consumes the same $E_{elec}$ to receive each bit.

**Why $d^{2}$ vs $d^{4}$?** 
- In **free space** (short distances, line-of-sight), signal power falls off as the square of distance (Friis transmission equation).
- In **multipath environments** (longer distances, urban/indoor), the channel is more lossy, and power falls off as the fourth power of distance (Two-ray ground reflection model).

## 2.5 Energy Budget Breakdown for a Typical Mote

Consider a MicaZ mote transmitting a 1000-bit packet over 50 m:

| Operation | Calculation | Energy |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| TX Electronics | $50$ nJ/bit $\times 1000$ bits | $= 50$ µJ |
| TX Amplifier (FS) | $10$ pJ/bit/m² $\times 1000 \times 50^{2}$ | $= 25$ µJ |
| **Total TX Energy** | $50 + 25$ | $= \mathbf{75}$ µJ |
| RX Energy | $50$ nJ/bit $\times 1000$ bits | $= 50$ µJ |
| Idle for 1 sec | $\approx 30$ mW $\times 1$ s | $= 30{,}000$ µJ |

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Idle Listening" Lesson:** The above table shows that **idle listening for just 1 second consumes 400× more energy than transmitting a packet!** This is the single most important justification for aggressive duty cycling in WSNs.
