# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Savings of SPIN over Flooding

> [!NOTE]
> Below is an **analytical derivation** that KTU examiners love — comparing **Flooding vs. SPIN** in terms of total energy consumed.

### Setup

- Network: $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed.
- Source node $S$ has one data packet of $k$ bits to send to all other $N-1$ nodes.
- Each node has on average $m$ neighbours (network degree).
- Transmission range = $r$.

### Step 1: Energy for Flooding (Naïve)

In pure flooding, every node that receives the data retransmits it. Each transmission is heard by $m$ neighbours.

$$E_{flood} = (N-1) \cdot \left[ E_{TX}(k,r) + m \cdot E_{RX}(k) \right]$$

Substituting the first-order radio model:

$$E_{flood} = (N-1) \cdot \left[ (E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot r^2) + m \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k \right]$$

Factoring $k$:

$$E_{flood} = (N-1) \cdot k \cdot \left[ E_{elec}(1+m) + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot r^2 \right]$$

### Step 2: Energy for SPIN

In SPIN, only nodes that **did not previously receive the data** participate. With perfect negotiation (no redundancy), each node receives the data **once**.

The total transmissions equal $(N-1)$ ADV + $(N-1)$ REQ + $(N-1)$ DATA exchanges in the worst case, but on average only the *useful transmissions* matter.

$$E_{SPIN} = (N-1) \cdot \left[ E_{TX}(k_{adv}, r) + m \cdot E_{RX}(k_{adv}) + E_{TX}(k_{req}, r) + E_{TX}(k_{data}, r) \right]$$

Assuming $k_{adv} = k_{req} = k_m \ll k_{data} = k$:

$$E_{SPIN} \approx (N-1) \cdot k \cdot \left[ E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot r^2 \right] + (N-1) \cdot 3 k_m \cdot E_{elec}$$

### Step 3: Comparative Energy Reduction

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{E_{flood} - E_{SPIN}}{E_{flood}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = 1 - \frac{E_{elec} + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot r^2 + 3k_m E_{elec}/k}{E_{elec}(1+m) + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot r^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** SPIN achieves significant energy savings because it eliminates **redundant receptions** ($m \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k$ term vanishes per node).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: GEAR Cost Function — Choosing Optimal Neighbour

### Setup

A node $u$ must forward a packet toward target region $R$. It has $k$ neighbours $N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_k$. For each neighbour:

- $d(N_i, R)$ = Euclidean distance from $N_i$ to the **centroid** of $R$
- $e(N_i)$ = normalised **consumed energy** at $N_i$ (in $[0, 1]$)

### Step 1: Define the Learned Cost

When a packet successfully reaches $R$ through neighbour $N_i$, the **learned cost** is updated:

$$c(N_i, R) = \alpha \cdot d(N_i, R) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot e(N_i)$$

The node $u$ then estimates its own cost:

$$\hat{c}(u, R) = \min_{N_i} c(N_i, R) + e(u)$$

where $e(u)$ is the energy consumed by $u$ to forward the packet to $N_i$.

### Step 2: The Selection Rule

The neighbour $N^*$ chosen is:

$$N^* = \arg\min_{N_i} \left[ \alpha \cdot d(N_i, R) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot e(N_i) \right]$$

### Step 3: Limiting Cases

$$\alpha = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pure geographic (greedy)}$$

$$\alpha = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pure energy-aware (pick highest-residual-energy neighbour)}$$

$$0 < \alpha < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Balanced GEAR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Explain the role of α in GEAR."* The above derivation is the **complete answer**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of "Other Examples"

| Feature | SPIN | Directed Diffusion | GEAR | Rumor Routing | TTDD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Routing Paradigm** | Data-centric | Data-centric (query-driven) | Location-based | Query/Event hybrid | Two-tier grid |
| **Localization Required** | No | No | Yes | No | Yes (grid) |
| **Sink Mobility Support** | Poor | Poor | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High | Medium-High | High | Medium | High |
| **Scalability** | Medium | Low-Medium | High | High | Medium |
| **Major Drawback** | Energy variability at edges | Flooding cost of Interest | Holes handling | Event-Query ratio matters | Grid construction overhead |
| **Best Use Case** | Periodic monitoring | Event detection | Tracking mobile targets | Mixed traffic | Multi-source, multi-sink |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPIN Negotiation Protocol Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Node A: has new data] -->|1. ADV meta-data| nodeB[Node B]
    nodeA -->|1. ADV meta-data| nodeC[Node C]
    nodeB -->|2. REQ request data| nodeA
    nodeC -->|2. REQ request data| nodeA
    nodeA -->|3. DATA full payload| nodeB
    nodeA -->|3. DATA full payload| nodeC
    nodeB -->|4. ADV as new owner| nodeD[Node D]
    nodeC -->|4. ADV as new owner| nodeE[Node E]
    nodeD -->|5. REQ| nodeB
    nodeB -->|6. DATA| nodeD
```

## 4.2 Directed Diffusion Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sinkNode[Sink Base Station] -->|1. Flood Interest| region1[Sensor Region]
    region1 -->|2. Setup Gradients| sinkNode
    srcA[Source A detects event] -->|3. Send Data along gradient| sinkNode
    srcB[Source B detects event] -->|3. Send Data along gradient| sinkNode
    sinkNode -->|4. Reinforce best path| srcA
    sinkNode -->|4. Negatively reinforce| srcB
```

## 4.3 GEAR Two-Phase Routing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Source Node] --> phase1{Phase 1: Forward to Target Region R}
    phase1 -->|cost function evaluation| neigh1[Neighbour 1: high residual energy, far]
    phase1 -->|cost function evaluation| neigh2[Neighbour 2: low energy, close]
    phase1 -->|cost function evaluation| neigh3[Neighbour 3: balanced]
    neigh3 -->|Selected as best| phase2{Phase 2: Recursive forwarding inside R}
    phase2 --> destNode[Destination in R]
```

## 4.4 TTDD Grid Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNode[Source Node] --> gridBuild[Build Grid Crossing Points]
    gridBuild --> cellA[Cell A dissemination node]
    gridBuild --> cellB[Cell B dissemination node]
    gridBuild --> cellC[Cell C dissemination node]
    gridBuild --> cellD[Cell D dissemination node]
    mobileSink[Mobile Sink] -->|Local 2-cell flood query| cellB
    cellB -->|Reply along grid| srcNode
    srcNode -->|Data along grid| mobileSink
```

## 4.5 Rumor Routing — Agent and Query

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eventNode[Event Detected] --> agentGen[Generate Agent]
    agentGen --> agentPath1[Node 1: record event in table]
    agentPath1 --> agentPath2[Node 2: record event in table]
    agentPath2 --> agentPath3[Node 3: record event in table]
    queryNode[Query Origin] --> queryPathA[Node A: random walk]
    queryPathA -->|match found at Node 2| queryMatch[Route query to event]
    queryMatch --> eventNode
```

## 5.1 PART A — 2 Mark Questions (Model Answers)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meta-data in the SPIN protocol? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Meta-data in SPIN is a **high-level descriptor** of the actual sensor data (e.g., "temperature reading, node ID, timestamp") sent via the **ADV** message before the full data is transmitted. It is important because it enables **data negotiation** — nodes can determine if the data is *new to them* before requesting the full payload, thereby eliminating **redundant transmissions** and saving significant energy.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "gradient" in Directed Diffusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **gradient** in Directed Diffusion is a **direction and data-rate attribute** set up at a node that has received an **Interest** message. It tells the node **which neighbour to forward data to** when a matching event occurs, and the **desired sending rate** (interval). Multiple gradients may exist (one per neighbour that forwarded the Interest); the sink later **reinforces** the best one to establish a single efficient path.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** confuse SPIN with LEACH — SPIN is data-centric; LEACH is cluster-based.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** state "Directed Diffusion uses flooding" without clarifying it floods *only the Interest*, not the data.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget to mention that GEAR has **two phases** (target region + recursive forwarding). Most students write only Phase 1 and lose 4-5 marks.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** describe TTDD as a single-tier protocol — the *two-tier* grid is the key.
> - ✅ **Always include the cost function / formula** when describing GEAR — it is the most-marks-bearing element.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) Explain the SPIN family of protocols in detail. Discuss SPIN-PP, SPIN-EC, and SPIN-RL with their features. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
SPIN (Sensor Protocols for Information via Negotiation) is a data-centric family of protocols designed by Heinzelman, Kulik, and Balakrishnan to overcome the inefficiencies of classical **flooding** and **gossiping** protocols in WSNs. SPIN uses three message types: **ADV (Advertisement), REQ (Request), and DATA**.

**SPIN-PP — Point-to-Point (2 marks):**
- Idealised baseline protocol.
- Operates on point-to-point links.
- Sequence: Node with new data → sends **ADV** → neighbour sends **REQ** → node sends **DATA**.
- Assumes no packet loss and no energy constraints.
- **Limitation:** Not realistic for wireless broadcast media.

**SPIN-EC — Energy-Conserving (2 marks):**
- Extension of SPIN-PP that introduces an **energy threshold**.
- A node with low remaining energy **refuses to participate** in the protocol (does not initiate ADV or REQ).
- This **conserves energy** for its own sensing duties.
- **Trade-off:** Some data may not propagate fully to all interested nodes.

**SPIN-RL — Reliable (2 marks):**
- Adds **reliability** on top of SPIN-EC.
- Uses a **history of which ADV messages were replied to**.
- If a node sends ADV and does not receive a REQ within a timeout, it **re-advertises** until the data is acknowledged.
- Handles packet loss and ensures delivery to interested neighbours.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The SPIN family scales well, is energy-efficient due to meta-data negotiation, but cannot guarantee delivery to all nodes (especially those geographically distant from the source whose neighbours may all decline).

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the operation of Directed Diffusion. How does it differ from SPIN? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Directed Diffusion Concept (1 mark):**
Directed Diffusion is a **data-centric, application-aware** routing paradigm where communication is driven by **named data** (interests) rather than node addresses. It has **four key elements**: Interest, Gradients, Data, and Reinforcement.

**Phase 1 — Interest Dissemination (1.5 marks):**
The sink **floods an Interest** message throughout the network. The interest contains attribute-value pairs describing what the sink wants:

```
type = vehicle, interval = 20ms, rect = [-100,100,-200,200]
```

**Phase 2 — Gradient Setup (1.5 marks):**
Each node that receives the Interest creates a **gradient** toward the neighbour from which it was received. A gradient specifies:
- A **direction** (the neighbour)
- A **desired data rate** (from the Interest's `interval` field)

**Phase 3 — Data Propagation (1.5 marks):**
When a source detects the matching event, it begins sending **Data** messages along its gradient(s). Data propagates back to the sink by following gradient directions in reverse.

**Phase 4 — Reinforcement (1.5 marks):**
The sink **reinforces** one of its neighbours (e.g., the one that delivered data with the lowest delay) by sending a **reinforced Interest** with a **smaller interval** (faster rate). Other paths are **negatively reinforced**. This creates a single optimised path from source to sink.

**Comparison with SPIN (1 mark bonus for distinction):**

| Aspect | SPIN | Directed Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Initiation | **Source-driven** (source advertises data) | **Sink-driven** (sink requests data via Interest) |
| Flooding | Avoids flooding | Uses **controlled flooding** of Interest |
| Path | Multiple hops, no specific path | Single reinforced path |
| State | Stateless (negotiation only) | Stateful (gradients maintained) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Describe the GEAR protocol in detail. Explain its cost function and the two-phase forwarding mechanism. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
GEAR (**Geographic and Energy Aware Routing**) is a location-based routing protocol proposed by **Yu, Govindan, and Estrin**. It improves on pure geographic greedy forwarding by incorporating **residual energy** awareness and a special handling strategy for **routing holes**.

**Cost Function (2 marks):**
Each node $u$ estimates the cost of reaching the **target region $R$** through neighbour $N_i$:

$$c(N_i, R) = \alpha \cdot d(N_i, R) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot e(N_i)$$

- $d(N_i, R)$ = distance from $N_i$ to centroid of $R$
- $e(N_i)$ = normalised consumed energy at $N_i$
- $\alpha$ = tunable weight in $[0, 1]$

The node $u$ chooses the neighbour $N^*$ with the **minimum** $c(N_i, R)$.

**Phase 1 — Forwarding to Target Region (2 marks):**
Once a packet is destined for region $R$, GEAR forwards it from neighbour to neighbour using the cost function above. When a node has no closer neighbour to $R$ than itself (a **hole**), it marks this and uses **learned cost** estimation based on its neighbours' costs.

**Phase 2 — Recursive Forwarding Inside Region (2 marks):**
When the packet reaches a node within $R$, GEAR switches to **restricted recursive geographic forwarding**: 
- If a destination node is specified, use greedy forwarding.
- If a node has no closer neighbour, it **backtracks** to the previous node and tries a different neighbour.
- This avoids needing complex planar graph construction (as in GPSR).

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
GEAR achieves a balance between energy efficiency and routing accuracy, and is particularly suited to **target-tracking** applications in WSNs.

---

**(b) Explain the Rumor Routing and TTDD protocols. Compare their suitability for mobile sinks. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Rumor Routing (2.5 marks):**
- **Concept:** Trade-off between **query flooding** and **event flooding**.
- **Agents:** When an event is detected, an **Agent** (long-lived packet) walks a **random path** through the network. At each node, the agent **stores the event path** in an **event table**.
- **Query Path:** A query is also a long-lived packet; it walks randomly until it finds a node whose event table matches. The query is then routed back to the event source via the agent's recorded path.
- **Limitation:** Performance depends on the **ratio of events to queries** — works best when they are roughly equal.

**TTDD — Two-Tier Data Dissemination (2.5 marks):**
- **Tier 1 — Grid Construction:** Each source builds a **2D grid of dissemination nodes** (only the crossing points store forwarding state).
- **Tier 2 — Mobile Sink Update:** When a **mobile sink** arrives, it floods a **local query** (only within a 2-cell radius). Once the nearest dissemination node replies, the sink **continuously receives** data updates as it moves — the data follows the sink along the grid.
- **Advantage:** No need to flood the entire network when the sink moves.

**Comparison for Mobile Sinks (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Rumor Routing | TTDD |
|---|---|---|
| Mobile Sink Support | **Poor** — agent paths are static | **Excellent** — designed for mobile sinks |
| Flooding | Not designed for mobility | Local 2-cell flood only |
| Energy on Sink Movement | N/A | Efficient (only local re-query) |
| Best Use | Event monitoring with static sinks | Multi-source, mobile-sink applications |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Other Examples":**

- ✅ **SPIN** uses **3 message types**: ADV, REQ, DATA. It is **source-initiated, data-centric**, and uses **meta-data negotiation** to avoid flooding.
- ✅ The **SPIN variants** to remember are: **PP** (baseline), **EC** (energy threshold), **BC** (broadcast), **RL** (reliability with retransmission), and **ML** (mobility).
- ✅ **Directed Diffusion** is **sink-initiated, query-driven** with 4 phases: Interest, Gradient, Data, Reinforcement.
- ✅ **Reinforcement** in Directed Diffusion uses a **smaller interval** (higher data rate) to strengthen the best neighbour's path.
- ✅ **GEAR** uses a **cost function** $c(N_i, R) = \alpha \cdot d(N_i, R) + (1-\alpha) \cdot e(N_i)$ with tunable weight $\alpha$.
- ✅ GEAR has **two phases**: (1) toward target region using cost, (2) recursive geographic forwarding inside the region.
- ✅ **Rumor Routing** uses **agents** (event-side) and **queries** (sink-side) that walk randomly; performance depends on the **event-to-query ratio**.
- ✅ **TTDD** uses a **per-source grid** and **localised queries from mobile sinks**; only crossing points (dissemination nodes) store state.
- ✅ **First-Order Radio Model** is **universal** for energy calculation in WSNs:
  - $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^n$ (where $n=2$ free-space, $n=4$ multipath)
  - $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$
- ✅ **Constant values to memorise**: $E_{elec}$ = **50 nJ/bit**, $\epsilon_{fs}$ = **10 pJ/bit/m²**, $\epsilon_{mp}$ = **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴**, $d_0$ ≈ **87.7 m**.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **protocol message exchange diagram** for full marks in SPIN/DD questions.
